content: Let me start by reading the necessary files to understand my task as the blueprint-reviewer subagent for iteration 188.
I'll start by reading the blueprint-reviewer instructions and project context.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
152

30	    with the one-line rationale. Do NOT skip me when the prior verdict

33	  - I always read the WHOLE blueprint. Do not pass me a scope-limiting
34	    directive — even when the iteration's focus is narrow, the cross-
35	    chapter view is the point of running me.
36	  - Read my per-chapter checklist and use it to decide which chapters
37	    need a follow-up writer dispatch this iter (consult your catalog
38	    for the blueprint-writing subagent). You do not need to re-read
39	    the chapters yourself; the checklist is your view into them.
40	  - **Act on my unstarted-phase proposals.** My report's
41	    `## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals` section names every
42	    strategy phase with no blueprint coverage and provides a concrete
43	    chapter outline for each. These are not informational — treat
44	    each proposal as a blueprint-writer directive seed. Dispatch a
45	    blueprint-writing subagent for each proposed chapter this iter
46	    (or record an explicit deferral rationale in the iter sidecar).
47	    Do NOT let unstarted phases accumulate across iters: a phase
48	    with no blueprint is a phase that cannot be parallelised, cannot
49	    be reviewed, and cannot be handed to a prover. Writing the
50	    blueprint early is the cheapest action in the loop.
51	
52	  ### HARD GATE — per-file prover dispatch
53	
54	  This is the rule that protects the project from low-quality prover work.
55	  Apply it verbatim, every iter, no exceptions:
56	
57	  - For each `.lean` file F you are considering adding to
58	    `## Current Objectives` (i.e. about to send a prover to), identify
59	    the corresponding blueprint chapter C. The mapping is: if some
60	    chapter declares `% archon:covers ... F ...` near its top, THAT
61	    chapter is C (a consolidated chapter blueprints several files while
62	    its siblings are thin pointers); otherwise fall back to the 1:1
63	    `Foo/Bar.lean → Foo_Bar.tex` slug. Look up C in my per-chapter
64	    checklist. When a chapter declares `covers:`, its single verdict
65	    gates EVERY file it lists — so a `correct: false` on the
66	    consolidated chapter defers all of them, and one writer directive
67	    against that chapter fixes the content behind all of them.
68	  - If C has `complete: true` AND `correct: true` AND no must-fix-this-iter
69	    finding touches it, F may go into the objectives.
70	  - Otherwise (C is `partial | false` on either axis, OR a must-fix
71	    finding names C, OR a broken `\uses{}` in C points at a label F's
72	    blueprint depends on):
73	    1. DROP F from this iter's objectives (by default — but see the
74	       same-iter fast path below).
75	    2. Dispatch the catalog's blueprint-writing subagent for C THIS
76	       iter with a directive targeting the specific must-fix items I
77	       flagged.
78	    3. Record in iter/iter-NNN/plan.md why F was at risk and what the
79	       writer was asked to fix.
80	  - **Same-iter fast path (sanctioned — removes a wasted iter).** After
81	    the writer returns AND `lake build` is green, you MAY re-dispatch me
82	    scoped to C alone — a fresh slug, a directive naming only C and the
83	    must-fix items I flagged. If that scoped re-review returns C as
84	    `complete: true` AND `correct: true` with no must-fix finding, the
85	    gate is satisfied for C: add F to THIS iter's objectives and send a
86	    prover. No need to burn an iter waiting. If the scoped re-review
87	    still fails, F stays deferred.
88	  - If you do NOT take the fast path, F simply waits: the next iter's
89	    mandatory dispatch of me re-confirms C before any prover runs on F.
90	    The fast path is purely a latency optimization — it never weakens
91	    the gate. F enters objectives ONLY on a fresh `complete + correct`
92	    verdict with no must-fix, whether that verdict arrives this iter (via
93	    the fast path) or next (via the mandatory dispatch). A green build
94	    alone is NOT sufficient; the re-review is what clears the gate.
95	
96	  ### Strategy / multi-route handling
97	
98	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and I report that one
99	    or more routes have no blueprint coverage, dispatch the catalog's
100	    blueprint-writing subagent (one call per missing-coverage route)
101	    in the same iteration. Do not let provers begin work on a route
102	    until its blueprint coverage is in place.
103	  - If I flag a definition that may require a strategy modification,
104	    treat that as a STRATEGY.md update task before any further Lean work
105	    — provers cannot be dispatched until the strategy update is reflected
106	    in the blueprint.
107	
108	  ### What "deferred" means in practice
109	
110	  Deferring a prover round is the correct, intentional action — not a
111	  failure. The 1-iter latency cost of waiting for a writer is small
112	  compared to the cost of a prover formalizing a broken blueprint and
113	  the work being thrown away. Log the deferral cleanly in plan.md and
114	  move on; the next iter's mandatory me-dispatch will green-light F.

116	
117	# Blueprint Reviewer
118	
119	You read the **entire blueprint** plus a context bundle from the plan agent and produce a per-chapter checklist + a set of cross-cutting summaries. You are **read-only on project source** and the blueprint — your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
120	
121	You are **mandatory in the plan phase**: the plan agent dispatches you every iteration before writing prover objectives. Your output is the plan agent's primary window into blueprint health, so the plan agent doesn't have to read every chapter itself.
122	
123	## Your Job
124	
125	The plan agent gives you a directive containing the current strategy snapshot, the references you should treat as authoritative, and any specific concerns. You then audit:
126	
127	- **Completeness** — for each chapter, are the definitions / theorems the strategy says the project needs actually present? Is the proof sketch detailed enough for a prover to formalize without guessing? Are the cross-references (`\uses{...}`) accurate?
128	- **Correctness** — does any definition contradict its references? Does any proof sketch contain a step that doesn't follow? Does any `\lean{...}` hint name a declaration that doesn't exist or has the wrong signature?
129	- **Lean target formulation quality** — for each `\lean{...}` hint, is the named theorem/definition a *useful* target for the prover? Vague or under-specified hints lead to wrong formalizations; surface those.
130	- **Multi-route coverage** — if the strategy lists multiple viable routes (alternative proof approaches, alternative definitions), is each route represented in the blueprint? Routes the strategy mentions but the blueprint does not cover are red flags.
131	- **Unstarted phases** — for each phase in the strategy that has no blueprint chapter at all, produce a concrete chapter outline (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" below). This is a proactive planning function, not just a gap report.
132	- **Citation discipline** — for every definition / theorem / lemma block that derives from external reference material, audit all four elements:
133	  1. **`% SOURCE:` pointer with local-file parenthetical.** Format must be `% SOURCE: <pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. Verify the named local file EXISTS under `references/`. A `% SOURCE:` with no `(read from …)` parenthetical, or with a parenthetical naming a file that doesn't exist on disk, is a hard fail — the writer fabricated the citation.
134	  2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` verbatim text** for definitions / theorems / lemmas. Audit dimensions:
135	     - **Original language**: the quote must be in the source's original language. A quote in English when the source is Bourbaki / EGA (French) signals translation, which is not allowed — flag it.
136	     - **Original notation**: the quote must use the source's notation, even when it differs from the project's. If the project writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ everywhere but the `% SOURCE QUOTE:` also writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ when the source is Hartshorne (who writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$), the quote was rewritten — flag it.
137	     - **Verbatim, every word**: a quote that reads like a paraphrase ("essentially says that …", "the source states …") rather than direct copy is a hard fail. The whole point of the verbatim is anti-hallucination; paraphrased quotes are exactly the failure mode.
138	  3. **`% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`** immediately before the `\begin{proof}` environment, when the block has a proof and the proof derives from the source. Same verbatim rules as `% SOURCE QUOTE:`. Missing `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` on a theorem whose proof clearly comes from the cited source is a citation-discipline finding. (Archon-original proofs of external statements are allowed — flag only when the proof prose itself reads as a translation of an obvious source proof.)
139	  4. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}`** line as the first line of the block's prose. Missing → flag.
140	
141	  Cross-check: the visible `\textit{Source: ...}` pointer must match the `% SOURCE:` pointer. Drift between them signals copy-paste error or hallucination.
142	
143	  Spot-check against `## References consulted` in the corresponding writer's report (when available in `task_results/`): every distinct `references/<file>.md` named in `% SOURCE:` parentheticals across the chapter should appear in that list. A `% SOURCE: ... (read from references/X.md)` where the writer's "References consulted" list does NOT mention `references/X.md` means the writer cited a file they did not actually open this session — fabrication.
144	
145	  **Archon-original / project-bespoke** results (no external source) omit the source lines entirely — do not falsely flag those. The signal that a block is Archon-original: the directive that produced it didn't name an external source, or the chapter prose explicitly characterizes it as new (e.g. "This is the technical heart of our argument"). When in doubt, ask the plan agent in "Notes for Plan Agent" rather than flagging.
146	
147	You audit the blueprint **against the context the plan agent gave you**, not against your own opinions about how the math should be set up. But you are critical of weak prose — under-specified blueprints fail provers and are not safe to merge.
148	
149	`/references/summary.md` lists the project's reference materials. The planner writes the blueprints, while its knowledge might be enough to write some parts of the blueprint, some parts may be subject to hallucination and require reference material. If you believe a reference is required to write mathematically correct and complete blueprint chapters, you should mention it in your report so that the planner can retrieve it for the writer.
150	
151	## Always read everything
152	
153	**Read every chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/`**, no exceptions, regardless of project size. The cross-chapter view is the entire reason for running me. If the directive contains a "scope" hint, treat it as a focus suggestion (which chapters need extra attention), not as a permission to skip reading.
154	
155	## Unstarted-phase proposals
156	
157	This is a proactive function — you produce it in addition to the per-chapter audit, not instead of it.
158	
159	After completing the per-chapter checklist, cross-reference the strategy snapshot's `## Phases & estimations` table against the set of chapters you just read. For each phase row whose `Status` is not `complete` and whose content has **zero blueprint coverage** (no chapter exists, or the only existing chapter is a stub with fewer than ~3 meaningful declaration blocks), produce a **chapter outline proposal**.
160	
161	A chapter outline proposal is not a flag or a complaint — it is a concrete, actionable seed that the plan agent can hand directly to a blueprint-writing subagent. It must contain enough mathematical detail that a writer receiving it as a directive can produce a complete chapter without further research by the plan agent.
162	
163	The proposal answers:
164	
165	1. **Which Lean file(s) will this chapter cover?** Name the expected file path(s) and whether this should be a consolidated chapter (one chapter covering multiple files via `% archon:covers`) or a 1:1 chapter.
166	
167	2. **What are the declaration blocks the chapter needs?** For each: the block type (definition / lemma / theorem / proposition), a proposed `\label`, a one-sentence description of the mathematical content, the expected `\lean{...}` hint (even if speculative — tag it `[expected]`), and the likely reference source (from `references/summary.md` or a named standard reference). Do not write the full prose — that is the writer's job — but give enough that the writer knows exactly what to write.
168	
169	3. **What are the internal dependencies?** Which declarations within this chapter use others (the `\uses{...}` graph in skeletal form). Identifying this upfront prevents the writer from writing declarations in an order that creates circular `\uses` references.
170	
171	4. **What is the proof strategy for the chapter's main theorem(s)?** Two to four sentences naming the key steps, the supporting lemmas, and any Mathlib infrastructure the proofs will depend on. This is the piece most likely to require a reference; if you can identify the relevant section of a source in `references/summary.md`, name it.
172	
173	5. **What references should the blueprint-writer read first?** List the specific local files under `references/` that are most relevant, and the sections within them. If no local file covers the material, flag it as a retrieval need so the plan agent can dispatch a reference-retriever before the writer.
174	
175	6. **Are there subphase choices the outline exposes?** If the chapter's material admits multiple natural decompositions (e.g. the main theorem could be approached via method A or method B, or the definition could be bundled or unbundled), name them explicitly. The plan agent cannot make an informed subphase choice without seeing what the choices are — surfacing them here is the primary value of writing the blueprint early rather than late.
176	
177	**Format for each proposal** (one block per unstarted phase):
178	
179	```
180	### Proposed chapter: <expected filename, e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Foo_Bar.tex`>
181	
182	**Covers**: `Foo/Bar.lean` (+ any sibling files if consolidated)
183	**Strategy phase**: <phase name from STRATEGY.md>
184	**Why now**: <one sentence on why writing this chapter this iter, before any prover work, is the right move — what ambiguity it resolves, what parallelism it enables>
185	
186	**Key declarations** (in dependency order):
187	1. `\definition` `\label{def:foo}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.foo}` [expected]. Source: <reference + section, or "no source identified — Archon-original">
188	2. `\lemma` `\label{lem:bar}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.bar}` [expected]. Source: <...>
189	3. `\theorem` `\label{thm:baz}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.baz}` [expected]. Source: <...>
190	...
191	
192	**`\uses` skeleton**:
193	- `thm:baz` uses `lem:bar`, `def:foo`
194	- `lem:bar` uses `def:foo`
195	
196	**Main theorem proof strategy**: <2–4 sentences>
197	
198	**References for writer**:
199	- `references/<slug>.md` → `<file>.<ext>`, §<section> — <why relevant>
200	- <or: "retrieval needed: <source name> — no local file exists yet">
201	
202	**Subphase choices exposed**:
203	- Choice A vs Choice B: <one sentence describing each option and the trade-off>. Recommendation: <your read on which is better given the strategy, or "unclear — plan agent should decide">
204	- (omit this field if the chapter has a clear single decomposition)
205	```
206	
207	**Tone and completeness.** The proposal should be aggressive about detail — it is cheaper to write a thorough proposal now than to have the writer ask for clarification later. If the phase is large enough that one chapter cannot cover it, say so and propose the split (two or more chapter outlines, each covering a natural sub-unit).
208	
209	**Do not propose chapters for phases that already have adequate coverage.** A phase with ≥3 meaningful declaration blocks across its chapters is covered; do not reproduce a proposal for it. The signal for "adequate" is the same as for the per-chapter checklist: `complete: true` (or `partial` with only minor gaps) on the phase's relevant chapters.
210	
211	**Proposals are must-act-this-iter.** They land in `## Must-fix-this-iter` under the label `unstarted-phase proposal` alongside the hard-gate findings. The plan agent must either dispatch a blueprint-writer for each proposed chapter or record an explicit one-line deferral rationale in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`. Silently ignoring the proposals is the same failure mode as silently ignoring a CHURNING verdict.
212	
213	## Directive Format
214	
215	```markdown
216	# Blueprint Reviewer Directive
217	
218	## Slug
219	<slug>
220	
221	## Strategy snapshot
222	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted: the project's end-state and the chapters that bear on it. Tells you what each chapter MUST contain to support the strategy. MUST include the full `## Phases & estimations` table so you can identify unstarted phases.>
223	
224	## Routes
225	<if the strategy has more than one viable route, list each route here with one line on what's distinctive about it and which chapters / definitions are exclusive to that route. If only one route, write "single route".>
226	
227	## References
228	- <path/to/reference.md>: <topic + which chapters depend on it>
229	- <arxiv ID>: <topic>
230	
231	## Focus areas (optional)
232	<chapters or theorems the plan agent wants extra attention on this iter — bias for thoroughness here, do not skip the others>
233	
234	## Known issues
235	<things the plan agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
236	```
237	
238	## What you do
239	
240	1. **Read your directive completely.** Extract the `## Phases & estimations` table — you will need it for the unstarted-phase proposals.
241	2. **List every chapter** under `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`.
242	3. **For each chapter** (no exceptions, no scope shortcuts):
243	   - Read the entire chapter.
244	   - Check every declaration block against the strategy snapshot.
245	   - For each proof block: are steps sound? Are `\uses{...}` cross-refs real labels? Is detail adequate for a prover?
246	   - For each `\lean{...}`: is the named target well-formulated? (You may verify existence using the `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools — read-only.)
247	4. **Compute completeness/correctness verdicts** per chapter (`true | partial | false`).
248	5. **Note cross-chapter inconsistencies** as you find them (e.g. `def X` in chapter A doesn't match the use of `X` in chapter B). These go in the "Cross-chapter notes" section.
249	6. **Check multi-route coverage**: for each route listed in the directive's `## Routes`, identify which chapters cover it. Flag any route that has zero or insufficient blueprint coverage.
250	7. **Cross-reference phases against chapters**: for each row in the strategy's `## Phases & estimations` table, determine whether adequate blueprint coverage exists. For every phase with no or stub coverage, produce a chapter outline proposal (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" above).
251	8. **Produce three top-level summaries** (see report format) — these are what the plan agent acts on first.
252	
253	You may also use:
254	- `archon-lean-lsp`: read-only Lean LSP operations (search, hover, diagnostics) to verify `\lean{...}` references.
255	- `${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" .` — quick view of the project's Lean ↔ blueprint dependency map.
256	
257	You do **not** modify any project file, including the blueprint. Even if you spot a clear fix, you report it; the plan agent decides what to do next iter.
258	
259	## Report format
260	
261	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
262	
263	**Omit-empty rule.** Required sections: `## Slug`, `## Iteration`, `## Per-chapter` (the HARD GATE depends on it), `## Severity summary`, `Overall verdict`. **Everything else is optional and must be omitted when empty.**
264	
265	Concretely:
266	
267	- `### Incomplete parts`, `### Proofs lacking detail`, `### Lean difficulty quality`, `### Multi-route coverage`, `### Citation discipline` under `## Top-level summaries`: omit each sub-section whose finding list is empty. If all five are empty, omit `## Top-level summaries` entirely — the per-chapter table already encodes "everything is fine".
268	- `## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals`: omit only when every phase in the strategy has adequate blueprint coverage. When present, this section is never empty — each proposal block is a concrete outline, not a one-line flag.
269	- `## Cross-chapter notes`: omit when no cross-chapter findings exist (the most common case on a clean blueprint).
270	- `## Strategy-modifying findings (if any)`: omit entirely when none. Do NOT write a section with "None" inside; the absence of the section IS the signal.
271	- `## Per-chapter` blocks for clean chapters: when a chapter is `complete: true`, `correct: true`, and `notes` is empty, render it as a single compact line — `### blueprint/src/chapters/Foo.tex — complete + correct, no notes.` — NOT a multi-line block with `notes: -` filler. Reserve full multi-line blocks for chapters that have actual findings.
272	- `## Severity summary`: when there are zero must-fix-this-iter and zero soon-severity items, render as `Severity summary: HARD GATE CLEARS — no findings.` and skip the per-tier breakdown.
273	
274	Filling templates with hollow "(none)" / "no drift" / "OK" content per chapter is the bloat the report should not produce. A clean 11-chapter audit should be a few hundred lines, not 16K tokens.
275	
276	```markdown
277	# Blueprint Review Report
278	
279	## Slug
280	<slug>
281	
282	## Iteration
283	<NNN>
284	
285	## Top-level summaries
286	
287	### Incomplete parts
288	<bullets naming chapter + which definition/theorem/proof is missing or shallow. Sorted by severity.>
289	- `Foo.tex`: definition of `<name>` is missing entirely.
290	- `Bar.tex`: theorem `\thm:baz` has a one-sentence "proof" — not enough for a prover to formalize.
291

3	\chapter{The Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero (RR.2)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, IV \S1, pp.~294--297
7	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages
8	% 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks below
9	% were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images of
10	% the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
11	
12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	
14	This chapter is the second of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
15	that close the project's headline RR bridge
16	\[
17	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
18	  \,\colon\,
19	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
20	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
21	\]
22	The bridge consumed by
23	REF of
24	\texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex} is the Hartshorne~IV.1.3.5 chain:
25	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) and the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
26	\to \mathbb Z$ (\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the
27	Riemann--Roch dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
28	\[
29	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
30	\]
31	(this chapter, \texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections identification
32	\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) (\texttt{RR.3}); the
33	``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)'' classification via
34	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
35	
36	% NOTE (Serre duality vs. direct \(\chi\) — iter-173 sub-phase choice).
37	% This chapter proves the RR formula via the \emph{direct \(\chi\)
38	% inductive proof} (Hartshorne IV.1.3 step ``Next, let \(D\) be any
39	% divisor\ldots'', p.~296), \emph{not} via Serre duality. The reason is
40	% strictly Mathlib-side: Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships no
41	% dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme and no Serre duality theorem
42	% \(H^i(X, \mathcal F) \cong H^{n-i}(X, \omega_X \otimes \mathcal F^\vee)^*\);
43	% routing through that machinery would force a multi-iter sub-build
44	% strictly heavier than the genus-\(0\) critical path requires. The
45	% direct \(\chi\) proof depends only on additivity of the Euler
46	% characteristic on a short exact sequence of coherent sheaves (a
47	% standard preadditive-category fact), the genus base case
48	% \(\chi(\mathcal O_C) = 1 - g\), and the closed-point skyscraper
49	% identity \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\).
50	
51	% NOTE (Genus-\(0\) carve-out vs. general \(g\) — iter-173 sub-phase
52	% choice). The chapter restricts the headline theorem
53	% REF to the genus-\(0\) specialisation
54	% \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\). The general-\(g\)
55	% statement \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) is downstream of
56	% \texttt{RR.2} (it requires the canonical divisor \(K\) and Serre
57	% duality / the \(K - D\) pairing) and is \emph{not} needed for the
58	% genus-\(0\) critical path: \texttt{RR.3} consumes only
59	% REF, and \texttt{RR.4} consumes
60	% \texttt{RR.3}. The intermediate \(\chi\)-identity
61	% REF is stated and
62	% sketched for general \(g\) (the inductive argument is genus-uniform);
63	% its full closure on a general-\(g\) curve waits for the project's
64	% subsequent Riemann--Roch chapter, but the proof body in
65	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean} pins the
66	% \emph{statement} for general \(g\) and may close the body initially
67	% under a \(g = 0\) hypothesis.
68	
69	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
70	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
71	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
72	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean}) carry the same
73	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
74	\begin{quote}
75	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
76	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
77	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
78	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
79	\end{quote}
80	The genus \(g(C)\) is the one defined in REF of
81	\texttt{Genus.tex}, namely \(g(C) = \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\)
82	via the project's \(\Module\bar k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology
83	(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}).

85	\section{The Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve}
86	
87	\begin{definition}
88	
89	
90	\leanok
91	[Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve]
92	  \label{def:eulerChar_curve}
93	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic}
94	  
95	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(\chi(\mathcal F)\)
96	  % inside the proof of Theorem~1.3) (read from
97	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
98	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
99	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
100	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
101	  % is the Euler characteristic
102	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
103	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295, the formula displayed
104	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3.}
105	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(\mathcal F\)
106	  be a coherent sheaf on \(C\). The \emph{Euler characteristic} of
107	  \(\mathcal F\) is the integer
108	  \[
109	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;:=\;
110	    \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \,\dim_{\bar k} H^i(C, \mathcal F)
111	    \;=\;
112	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal F)
113	    \,-\,
114	    \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal F).
115	  \]
116	  The second equality is a curve-specific simplification: because
117	  \(\dim C = 1\), Grothendieck vanishing (Hartshorne~III.2.7) gives
118	  \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i \geq 2\), so the alternating sum has
119	  only two terms. Coherence of \(\mathcal F\) on the proper $\bar
120	  k\(-scheme \)C\( guarantees that both \)H^0\( and \)H^1$ are
121	  finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector spaces (Serre's coherent-cohomology
122	  finiteness theorem, the same finiteness backing
123	  REF), so the two terms are honest natural numbers and
124	  the difference is a well-defined integer.
125	
126	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
127	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic} takes a coherent
128	  sheaf \(\mathcal F\) on \(C\) and returns an integer; the natural input
129	  shape is the project's \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf-cohomology
130	  wrapper \(\HModule \bar k\) (REF) applied to a
131	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured carrier built from \(\mathcal F\), with
132	  \(\dim_{\bar k}\) extracted via \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\). The
133	  declaration is \texttt{noncomputable} (inherited from
134	  \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\) and from the \(\Ext\)-based cohomology
135	  construction).
136	\end{definition}
137	
138	\section{The \(\ell\)-invariant of a Weil divisor}
139	
140	\begin{definition}
141	
142	
143	\leanok
144	[\(\ell\)-invariant of a Weil divisor]
145	  \label{def:l_invariant}
146	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l}
147	  
148	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(l(D)\) and of the
149	  % "dimension of \(|D|\)") (read from
150	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
151	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We denote \(\dim_k H^0(X, \mathscr L(D))\) by \(l(D)\),
152	  % so that the \emph{dimension of \(|D|\)} is \(l(D) - 1\). The number
153	  % \(l(D)\) is finite by (II, 5.19) or (III, 5.2)."
154	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(l(D)\)).}
155	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let
156	  \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) be a Weil divisor (REF). The
157	  \emph{\(\ell\)-invariant of \(D\)} is the integer
158	  \[
159	    \ell(D) \;:=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
160	    \;\in\; \mathbb N,
161	  \]
162	  the \(\bar k\)-dimension of the space of global sections of the
163	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) associated to \(D\). Finiteness of
164	  \(\ell(D)\) is a consequence of the coherent-cohomology finiteness
165	  theorem on a proper \(\bar k\)-scheme (Hartshorne~II.5.19 or
166	  III.5.2). The classical \emph{complete linear system \(|D|\)} has
167	  \(\bar k\)-dimension \(\ell(D) - 1\) as a projective space, but the
168	  chapter never uses the projective-space interpretation; \(\ell(D)\) is
169	  the only quantity it needs.
170	
171	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
172	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l} accepts a divisor
173	  \(D : C.\mathtt{WeilDivisor}\) (REF) and returns
174	  \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\;\bar k\;(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D)))\),
175	  computed through the same \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf-cohomology
176	  pipeline as REF. The associated invertible
177	  sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is the line bundle of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
178	  chapter, to be added); the present chapter's prose and Lean
179	  signature reference \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) by name and consume only its
180	  \(H^0\) functor. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
181	\end{definition}

183	\section{The \(\chi\) identity: \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\)}
184	
185	\subsection*{Substrate lemmas for the proof of the \(\chi\)-identity}
186	
187	The proof of REF below
188	decomposes into three substantive substrate lemmas, factored out of the
189	Hartshorne~IV.1.3 inductive argument. The first
190	(REF, \emph{additivity}) is the
191	classical statement that \(\chi\) descends to the Grothendieck group
192	of \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\); the second
193	(REF, \emph{iso-invariance}) is the
194	elementary fact that \(\chi\) is well defined on isomorphism classes
195	of sheaves; the third
196	(REF, \emph{skyscraper}) computes \(\chi\)
197	on a closed-point skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\). Pinning each as its own
198	Lean declaration lets the prover attack the three substrate gaps
199	independently of the parent inductive argument.
200	
201	\begin{lemma}\leanok
202	[Additivity of the Euler characteristic on a short exact sequence]
203	  \label{lem:euler_char_shortExact_add}
204	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add}
205	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
206	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (inside the proof of Theorem~1.3,
207	  % the citation ``III, Ex.~5.1'' for the additivity of \(\chi\) on a
208	  % short exact sequence) (read from
209	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
210	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
211	  % exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1), and \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), so we have
212	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\)."
213	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1 p.~296 (citation of III, Ex.~5.1
214	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3).}
215	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
216	  \(\bar k\), and let
217	  \[
218	    0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0
219	  \]
220	  be a short exact sequence in the category of \(\Module \bar k\)-valued
221	  sheaves on \(C\). Then
222	  \[
223	    \chi(\mathcal F_2) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3).
224	  \]
225	\end{lemma}
226	
227	\begin{proof}
228	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
229	  Apply the project-side \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured covariant Ext-LES
230	  (Mathlib's \texttt{Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence}) to the constant-sheaf
231	  test object \(L = (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;
232	  \bar k\;\bar k)\) and the short exact sequence
233	  \(0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0\). The
234	  resulting nine-term sequence
235	  \[
236	    H^0(\mathcal F_1) \to H^0(\mathcal F_2) \to H^0(\mathcal F_3)
237	    \to H^1(\mathcal F_1) \to H^1(\mathcal F_2) \to H^1(\mathcal F_3)
238	    \to H^2(\mathcal F_1) \to H^2(\mathcal F_2) \to H^2(\mathcal F_3)
239	  \]
240	  is exact in the category of \(\bar k\)-vector spaces. Grothendieck
241	  vanishing \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i \geq 2\) on the
242	  one-dimensional scheme \(C\) (Hartshorne~III.2.7) truncates the
243	  sequence to a six-term exact sequence
244	  \[
245	    0 \to K_0 \to H^0(\mathcal F_1) \to H^0(\mathcal F_2) \to
246	    H^0(\mathcal F_3) \to H^1(\mathcal F_1) \to H^1(\mathcal F_2)
247	    \to H^1(\mathcal F_3) \to 0
248	  \]
249	  (with \(K_0 = 0\) the kernel of the first map, kept here only as a
250	  bookkeeping placeholder). Rank-counting on a six-term exact sequence
251	  of finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector spaces — iterated applications
252	  of \(\mathtt{Submodule.finrank\_quotient\_add\_finrank}\) at each
253	  intermediate kernel/image quotient — yields the alternating identity
254	  \[
255	    \dim H^0(\mathcal F_1) - \dim H^0(\mathcal F_2) + \dim H^0(\mathcal F_3)
256	    - \dim H^1(\mathcal F_1) + \dim H^1(\mathcal F_2) - \dim H^1(\mathcal F_3)
257	    \;=\; 0,
258	  \]
259	  which rearranges, using the definition
260	  \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\)
261	  (REF), to
262	  \(\chi(\mathcal F_2) = \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3)\).
263	
264	  % NOTE: gated on project-side LES carrier + finiteness machinery.
265	  % Mathlib `b80f227` ships `Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence` but not (a)
266	  % the `ModuleCat kbar`-flavoured LES carrier specialised to the
267	  % project's sheaf-cohomology pipeline `Scheme.HModule`, (b) Grothendieck
268	  % vanishing `H^i(C, F) = 0` for `i ≥ 2` at the `Ext`-cohomology level
269	  % consumed by `HModule`, nor (c) the six-term rank-counting bridge
270	  % composed of `Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank` invocations.
271	  % The Lean helper `eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add` therefore
272	  % carries a Tier-3 honest typed sorry; its closure lands once the
273	  % three substrate pieces are in place. Off the genus-0 critical path
274	  % (the parent theorem `thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus`
275	  % is the only consumer in `RR.2`).
276	\end{proof}
277	
278	\begin{lemma}\leanok
279	[Iso-invariance of the Euler characteristic]
280	  \label{lem:euler_char_iso}
281	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_iso}
282	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
283	  \textit{Project-bespoke decomposition} (Archon-original packaging of
284	  the Mathlib \(\Ext\)-postcomposition API; no external source named).
285	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
286	  \(\bar k\) and let \(\mathcal F, \mathcal G\) be \(\Module \bar k\)-valued
287	  sheaves on \(C\) with an isomorphism \(\mathcal F \cong \mathcal G\).
288	  Then
289	  \[
290	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal G).
291	  \]
292	\end{lemma}
293	
294	\begin{proof}
295	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
296	  \leanok
297	  An isomorphism \(e \colon \mathcal F \xrightarrow{\;\cong\;} \mathcal G\)
298	  induces, in each cohomological degree \(n \in \mathbb N\), a
299	  \(\bar k\)-linear equivalence
300	  \[
301	    H^n(C, \mathcal F) \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\; H^n(C, \mathcal G)
302	  \]
303	  by post-composition with the Ext-degree-zero classes
304	  \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0(e.\mathtt{hom})\) and
305	  \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0(e.\mathtt{inv})\). The mutual-inverse identities
306	  for this pair of post-compositions reduce — via the associativity
307	  lemma \(\mathtt{Ext.comp\_assoc\_of\_third\_deg\_zero}\), the
308	  composition lemma \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0\mathtt{\_comp\_mk}_0\), and the
309	  iso identities \(e.\mathtt{hom} \circ e.\mathtt{inv} = \mathrm{id}\),
310	  \(e.\mathtt{inv} \circ e.\mathtt{hom} = \mathrm{id}\) — to the identity
311	  map on \(\Ext\) elements. Specialising to \(n = 0\) and \(n = 1\) and
312	  applying \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.finrank\_eq}\) at each of those two
313	  degrees gives
314	  \(\dim H^n(C, \mathcal F) = \dim H^n(C, \mathcal G)\) for
315	  \(n \in \{0, 1\}\). Unfolding the definition
316	  \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\)
317	  (REF) closes the goal.
318	\end{proof}
319	
320	\begin{lemma}\leanok
321	[Euler characteristic of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf]
322	  \label{lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}
323	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_skyscraperSheaf}
324	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
325	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (the \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\) input
326	  % cited inline inside the proof of Theorem~1.3) (read from
327	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
328	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
329	  % exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1), and \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), so we have
330	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\)."
331	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1 p.~296 (the ``\(\chi(k(P)) = 1\)''
332	  input inside the proof of Theorem~1.3).}
333	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
334	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Write \(k(P)\) for
335	  the closed-point skyscraper sheaf
336	  \[
337	    k(P) \;:=\; \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
338	    (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k),
339	  \]
340	  the \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf on \(C\) with stalk \(\bar k\) at
341	  \(P\) and zero stalks elsewhere. Then
342	  \[
343	    \chi(k(P)) \;=\; 1.
344	  \]
345	\end{lemma}
346	
347	\begin{proof}
348	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
349	  By the definition of \(\chi\)
350	  (REF),
351	  \[
352	    \chi(k(P)) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P))
353	    \;-\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)).
354	  \]
355	  The closure decomposes cleanly into two independent half-statements,
356	  attacked separately by the prover.
357	
358	  \emph{H\(^0\) half} — \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\).
359	  Compose the project's three-step \(\bar k\)-linear-equivalence chain
360	  \[
361	    H^0(C, k(P))
362	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\;
363	    \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Sh}}\!\bigl(
364	      (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k),\,
365	      k(P)
366	    \bigr)
367	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\;
368	    \Gamma(C, k(P))
369	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\;
370	    (k(P))(C),
371	  \]
372	  realised by the project lemmas
373	  \texttt{Scheme.HModule\_zero\_linearEquiv},
374	  \texttt{Scheme.constantSheafGammaHom\_linearEquiv}, and
375	  \texttt{Scheme.SheafGammaObj\_linearEquiv\_top} (the last step
376	  identifies the abstract \(\Gamma\) functor with the underlying
377	  presheaf evaluation at the top open \(\top\), i.e.\ at the whole
378	  underlying space of \(C\)). Applying
379	  \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.finrank\_eq}\) along this chain reduces the
380	  goal to
381	  \(\dim_{\bar k} (k(P))(C) = 1\). The closed point
382	  \(P\) lies in the top open of \(C\), so the skyscraper presheaf
383	  evaluates at the top open to its stalk
384	  \(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k\) by definition, and
385	  \(\dim_{\bar k} \bar k = 1\) closes the half. This half is
386	  axiom-clean closure projection (\(\sim 30\text{--}60\) LOC) against
387	  the existing project chain; it is the natural target for the
388	  iteration that follows the present blueprint expansion.
389	
390	  \emph{H\(^1\) half} — \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
391	  Mathematically, the closed-point skyscraper sheaf is flasque (any
392	  pushforward of a sheaf from a one-point space is flasque;
393	  Hartshorne~III.2.5(c)), and flasque sheaves have vanishing higher
394	  cohomology in degrees \(\geq 1\) on any topological space
395	  (Hartshorne~III.2.5). Hence \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\) as a
396	  \(\bar k\)-vector space.
397	
398	  % NOTE: gated on Mathlib flasque cohomology.
399	  % Mathlib `b80f227` does NOT ship an `H^n(X, F) = 0 for flasque F`
400	  % lemma at the `ModuleCat`-flavoured `Abelian.Ext`-cohomology level
401	  % used by the project's `Scheme.HModule kbar` pipeline. The closure
402	  % depends on either (a) an upstream Mathlib
403	  % `Sheaf.flasque_vanishing_cohomology` contribution at the
404	  % `Ext`-cohomology level for sheaves with values in an abelian
405	  % category with enough injectives, or (b) a project-side flasque-
406	  % cohomology bridge (~150-300 LOC, off the genus-0 critical path).
407	  % Until one of the two lands, the H¹ half is a typed-sorry blocker
408	  % independent of the H⁰ half — i.e. the prover can attack the H⁰
409	  % half against the existing project chain without waiting for the
410	  % H¹ machinery, and the present skyscraper lemma waits for the H¹
411	  % gap to clear.
412	
413	  Combining the two halves through the definition of \(\chi\),
414	  \(\chi(k(P)) = 1 - 0 = 1\), closes the lemma.
415	\end{proof}

417	\subsection*{The main \(\chi\)-identity}
418	
419	\begin{theorem}
420	
421	
422	\leanok
423	[Euler-characteristic identity for \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)]
424	  \label{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus}
425	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus}
426	  
427	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Theorem~1.3 (the reduction inside the
428	  % proof: ``Thus we have to show that for any \(D\), $\chi(\mathscr L(D))
429	  % = \deg D + 1 - g$'') (read from
430	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
431	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let \(D\) be a
432	  % divisor on a curve \(X\) of genus \(g\). Then}
433	  % \(l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g\)."
434	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
435	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
436	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
437	  % is the Euler characteristic
438	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
439	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295.}
440	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
441	  \(\bar k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\), and let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\). Then
442	  \[
443	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g.
444	  \]
445	\end{theorem}
446	
447	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "First we consider the case \(D = 0\). Then our
448	% formula says
449	% \(\dim H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = 1 - g\),
450	% because \(H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) = k\) for any projective variety
451	% (I, 3.4), and \(\dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = g\) by (1.1).
452	% Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any point. We will show
453	% that the formula is true for \(D\) if and only if it is true for
454	% \(D + P\). Since any divisor can be reached from \(0\) in a finite number
455	% of steps by adding or subtracting a point each time, this will show
456	% the result holds for all \(D\).
457	% We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its structure sheaf is
458	% the skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\), which we denote by
459	% \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\) by (II, 6.18).
460	% Therefore we have an exact sequence
461	% \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
462	% Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
463	% \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\),
464	% (since \(\mathscr L(P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
465	% does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\)). Now the Euler characteristic is
466	% additive on short exact sequences (III, Ex. 5.1), and $\chi(k(P))
467	% = 1$, so we have
468	% \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\).
469	% On the other hand, \(\deg D + P = \deg D + 1\), so our formula is true
470	% for \(D\) if and only if it is true for \(D + P\), as required."
471	\begin{proof}
472	  \leanok
473	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, def:l_invariant,
474	    lem:euler_char_shortExact_add, lem:euler_char_iso,
475	    lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}
476	
477	  The proof is by induction on the structure of \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) as the free
478	  abelian group on closed points (REF,
479	  REF). Both sides of the asserted
480	  equation are additive in \(D\) (the right-hand side by linearity of
481	  \(\deg\), REF; the left-hand side by the
482	  inductive step proved below), so it suffices to verify the formula
483	  for \(D = 0\) and to show that it transports across the elementary
484	  modification \(D \mapsto D + [P]\) at any closed point \(P \in C\).
485	
486	  \emph{Base case \(D = 0\).} The line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(0)\) is the
487	  structure sheaf \(\mathcal O_C\), and the formula
488	  \(\chi(\mathcal O_C) = 1 - g\) reads
489	  \[
490	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
491	    - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)
492	    \;=\; 1 - g.
493	  \]
494	  The second term is the definition of the genus,
495	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g\) (REF). The
496	  first term is \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1\): \(C\) is a
497	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over the algebraically
498	  closed field \(\bar k\), so its connected components agree with its
499	  geometrically connected components, and $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
500	  \cong \bar k\( as \)\bar k$-vector spaces (the Hartshorne~I.3.4
501	  statement that ``\(H^0(X, \mathcal O_X) = k\) for any projective
502	  variety'' over an algebraically closed field; the Mathlib home is
503	  the project's \(H^0\)-bridge in
504	  \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex}). Substituting these
505	  two values gives \(1 - g = 1 - g\), as required.
506	
507	  \emph{Inductive step (Hartshorne's ``\(D \leftrightsquigarrow D + P\)''
508	  step).} Let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. We follow Hartshorne's
509	  argument: view \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(C\); its structure
510	  sheaf is the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P) \cong \bar k\) supported at \(P\),
511	  and its sheaf of ideals is \(\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \subset \mathcal O_C\)
512	  (the locally principal ideal sheaf cutting out \(P\) in \(C\)). Hence
513	  the short exact sequence
514	  \[
515	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
516	  \]
517	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\) (Hartshorne~II.6.18). Tensoring with the
518	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) (a locally free
519	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-module of rank \(1\), so tensoring preserves exactness
520	  and leaves the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) unchanged) yields
521	  \[
522	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(D)
523	    \to \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
524	    \to k(P) \to 0.
525	  \]
526	  (Strictly: tensoring \(\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \subset \mathcal O_C\) with
527	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) produces a sheaf isomorphic — but not
528	  syntactically equal — to \(\mathcal O_C(D)\), and the skyscraper
529	  factor \(k(P)\) is preserved up to isomorphism because
530	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) is locally free of rank \(1\). We transport
531	  along this isomorphism via iso-invariance of \(\chi\),
532	  REF.)
533	  Additivity of \(\chi\) on the short exact sequence
534	  (REF, the project-side
535	  factoring of the ``Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
536	  exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1)'' input of Hartshorne~IV.1.3) gives
537	  \[
538	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P]))
539	    \;=\;
540	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + \chi(k(P)).
541	  \]
542	  The skyscraper-sheaf input
543	  \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\) is the standalone substrate lemma
544	  REF (factored from the
545	  same Hartshorne~IV.1.3 line); its proof splits into the
546	  H\(^0\) identification \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) and the
547	  H\(^1\) vanishing \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\), pursued
548	  independently by the prover (see the lemma's two-paragraph proof
549	  body). Therefore
550	  \[
551	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + 1.
552	  \]
553	  On the other hand, \(\deg([P]) = 1\) (every closed point contributes
554	  \(1\) to the bare-sum degree of REF), so by
555	  REF
556	  \[
557	    \deg(D + [P]) + 1 - g \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g + 1.
558	  \]
559	  Comparing the two displayed identities, the formula
560	  \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) holds for \(D\) if and only
561	  if it holds for \(D + [P]\). Since the closed-point divisors \([P]\)
562	  generate \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) as a free abelian group, the formula propagates
563	  from \(D = 0\) to every \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) by induction on the (signed)
564	  number of generators in the expression of \(D\).
565	
566	  \emph{Lean reference note on the three substrate lemmas.} The
567	  inductive step of this proof consumes three substantive substrate
568	  lemmas, each pinned to its own Lean declaration in
569	  \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean}: the additivity
570	  REF (\(\chi\) on a short
571	  exact sequence, gated on the project-side
572	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured Ext-LES carrier + Grothendieck
573	  vanishing + six-term rank-counting), the iso-invariance
574	  REF (\(\chi\) on
575	  isomorphism classes, axiom-clean against Mathlib's
576	  \(\Ext\)-postcomposition API; closed iter-186), and the skyscraper
577	  identity REF
578	  (\(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), splitting into an H\(^0\) half closed against
579	  the project's existing \(H^0\)-bridge and an H\(^1\) half gated on
580	  Mathlib flasque-cohomology — see the lemma's annotated proof body).
581	  Mathlib \texttt{b80f227} provides the Euler-characteristic API
582	  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.EulerCharacteristic}
583	  (\texttt{HomologicalComplex.eulerChar},
584	  \texttt{GradedObject.eulerChar}) but does \emph{not} ship the
585	  specific additivity statement
586	  \texttt{CategoryTheory.ShortExact.eulerChar\_additive} at the
587	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured sheaf-cohomology level the project
588	  consumes; the additivity lemma is the project-side bridge.
589	\end{proof}

591	\section{The Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero}
592	
593	\begin{theorem}
594	
595	
596	\leanok
597	[Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero]
598	  \label{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}
599	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l_eq_degree_plus_one_of_genus_zero}
600	  
601	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Example~1.3.5 (genus-\(0\) specialisation
602	  % of Riemann--Roch; the curve-is-rational consequence) (read from
603	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
604	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is \emph{rational}
605	  % if it is birational to \(\mathbf P^1\) (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in
606	  % this chapter are complete and nonsingular by definition, a curve
607	  % \(X\) is rational if and only if \(X \cong \mathbf P^1\) (I, 6.12). Now
608	  % using (1.3) we can show that \(X\) is rational if and only if \(g = 0\).
609	  % We already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) (I, Ex. 7.2), so
610	  % conversely suppose given a curve \(X\) of genus 0. Let \(P, Q\) be two
611	  % distinct points of \(X\) and apply Riemann--Roch to the divisor
612	  % \(D = P - Q\). Since \(\deg K - D = -2\), using (1.3.3) above, we have
613	  % \(l(K - D) = 0\), and so we find \(l(D) = 1\). But \(D\) is a divisor of
614	  % degree 0, so by (1.2) we have \(D \sim 0\), in other words $P \sim
615	  % Q\(. But this implies \)X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
616	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
617	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
618	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) with
619	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\). Then
620	  \[
621	    \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
622	  \]
623	\end{theorem}
624	
625	\begin{proof}
626	  \leanok
627	  
628	  Specialising
629	  REF to the genus-\(0\)
630	  hypothesis \(g = 0\) yields
631	  \[
632	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
633	  \]
634	  Unfolding \(\chi\) via REF and the definition
635	  \(\ell(D) = \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))\)
636	  (REF), this rewrites as
637	  \[
638	    \ell(D) - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
639	    \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
640	  \]
641	  Under the standing genus-\(0\) hypothesis and the assumption
642	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\), the higher-cohomology term vanishes:
643	  \[
644	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; 0
645	    \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0.
646	  \]
647	  Substituting yields \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\), as required.
648	
649	  \emph{The \(H^1\)-vanishing input.} On a smooth proper geometrically
650	  irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), the vanishing
651	  \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) = 0\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\) is the Hartshorne
652	  formulation Lemma~1.2 read covariantly: \(\ell(D) \neq 0\) forces
653	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\); equivalently, for a curve where every
654	  degree-\(0\)-or-higher invertible sheaf is generated by global
655	  sections after a single point-twist, \(H^1\) of the twist must vanish
656	  by the long exact sequence of the skyscraper extension at any
657	  closed point. In the chain of sub-build chapters, this \(H^1\)
658	  vanishing is the natural content of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
659	  \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added), where the line
660	  bundle \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) and its cohomology are constructed; once
661	  \texttt{RR.3} is closed, the present theorem inherits the
662	  vanishing as an instance, and the proof closes verbatim as
663	  displayed. Pending \texttt{RR.3}, the Lean signature
664	  \texttt{l\_eq\_degree\_plus\_one\_of\_genus\_zero} threads the
665	  \(H^1\)-vanishing hypothesis explicitly as a named premise; the
666	  iteration that closes \texttt{RR.3} then drops the premise in a
667	  follow-up signature simplification.
668	
669	  \emph{Sub-build note for \texttt{RR.4}.} The downstream consumer of
670	  this theorem is the genus-\(0\) classification
671	  REF of
672	  \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex}: with \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\)
673	  and the degree-zero principal-divisor identity
674	  (REF), Hartshorne's Example~1.3.5 chain
675	  produces a degree-\(1\) morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) via
676	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} applied to the two-dimensional
677	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\), which is then upgraded to an
678	  isomorphism in \texttt{RR.4}.
679	\end{proof}
680	
681	\section{Out of scope}
682	
683	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.2}
684	and are sequenced into the sibling sub-build chapters:
685	
686	\begin{itemize}
687	  \item The general-\(g\) Riemann--Roch theorem
688	    \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) (Hartshorne~IV.1.3 in
689	    its full form). This requires the canonical divisor \(K\), the
690	    canonical sheaf \(\Omega^1_{C / \bar k} = \omega_C\), and
691	    Serre duality $H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes
692	    \mathcal L^{-1})^*$. Mathlib has none of these for schemes, and
693	    the genus-\(0\) critical path does not require them; the general
694	    statement is queued for a follow-up chapter (post-RR-bridge).
695	  \item The construction of the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)
696	    from a Weil divisor \(D\), the linear-equivalence isomorphism
697	    \(\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')\) when \(D \sim D'\), and
698	    the computation of $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \cong \bar k \cdot
699	    1 \oplus \bar k \cdot t$ (\emph{a non-constant rational function
700	    \(t\) exists}) live in \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
701	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added). Inside the proof
702	    of REF the dependency on
703	    \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is reduced to its \(H^0\) functor and the short
704	    exact sequence of the closed point.
705	  \item The vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) = 0\) for
706	    \(\deg(D) \geq 0\) on a genus-\(0\) curve is consumed as a named
707	    premise by REF; its closure is
708	    part of \texttt{RR.3} (where the cohomology of
709	    \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(d)\) is computed) and is recovered for
710	    a general smooth proper genus-\(0\) curve via the isomorphism
711	    \(C \cong \mathbb P^1\) of \texttt{RR.4} or directly via the
712	    base-change argument inside \texttt{RR.3}.
713	  \item The classification ``a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
714	    curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g = 0\) is isomorphic to
715	    \(\mathbb P^1\)'' (Hartshorne Example~IV.1.3.5 proper) is the
716	    headline of \texttt{RR.4} (sibling chapter
717	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalIsoP1.tex}, to be added). The
718	    present chapter supplies one of its two inputs (the $\ell(D) =
719	    \deg(D) + 1\( identity); the other input is the degree-\)0$
720	    principal-divisor identity REF from
721	    \texttt{RR.1}.
722	  \item Higher-cohomology bookkeeping (\(\chi\) as an alternating sum
723	    over all \(i \geq 0\), the Grothendieck vanishing
724	    \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i > \dim C = 1\)) is not separately
725	    formalised here; the chapter consumes only the two-term form
726	    \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\) which is the standing
727	    curve specialisation.
728	\end{itemize}
