961	  \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling proof of \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} uses \(\mathbb G_m\) as a
962	  parameter scheme (via \(W\) in the additive decomposition), not its group-object axioms.
963	\end{definition}
964	\subsection*{Chart cover and chart-ring iso (formalisation infrastructure)}
965	The next three declarations are iter-168 formalisation infrastructure that the chartwise
966	construction of the \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling action \(\sigma_\times\) (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1})
967	and its downstream consumers (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}) ultimately rest on.
968	They realise, at the Lean level, the standard ``affine chart of \(\mathbb P^1\) is $\mathbb
969	A^1\('' picture: a two-chart affine open cover of \)\overline{\mathbb P^1}\( by \)D_+(X_0)$ and
970	\(D_+(X_1)\); the identification of each chart ring with the one-variable polynomial ring
971	\(\bar k[u]\); and the resulting reducedness of \(\overline{\mathbb P^1}\) as a scheme. None
972	of them is on the genus-\(0\) critical path conceptually, but each is consumed by the Lean
973	formalisation downstream.
974	\begin{definition}
975	[Two-chart affine open cover of \(\overline{\mathbb P^1}\)]
976	  \label{def:projlinebar_affine_cover}
977	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.projectiveLineBarAffineCover}
978	  % Archon-original Lean encoding of the textbook fact that \(\mathbb P^1\) is covered by its
979	  % two standard affine charts \(D_+(X_0)\) and \(D_+(X_1)\); the construction is built on top of
980	  % Mathlib's \(\mathrm{Proj.affineOpenCoverOfIrrelevantLESpan}\). No external % SOURCE QUOTE
981	  % needed for the Lean encoding itself.
982	  The two-chart affine open cover of \(\overline{\mathbb P^1}\) by the standard \(D_+(X_0)\) and
983	  \(D_+(X_1)\), built by specialising Mathlib's
984	  \(\mathrm{Proj.affineOpenCoverOfIrrelevantLESpan}\) to the index set
985	  \(\iota = \{0, 1\} = \mathrm{Fin}\,2\) with family \(f = (X_0, X_1)\) and weights
986	  \(m = (1, 1)\). The non-trivial input is that the irrelevant ideal of the bi-graded
987	  \(\bar k[X_0, X_1]\) is contained in \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\{X_0, X_1\}\), which holds by
988	  writing any irrelevant element as a sum of monomials of strictly positive total degree
989	  (so each monomial is divisible by \(X_0\) or \(X_1\)). This cover packages the two affine
990	  charts into the data that Mathlib's \(\mathrm{IsReduced.of\_openCover}\) and
991	  \(\mathrm{Scheme.Cover.glueMorphisms}\) APIs consume, and is the chartwise glue for the
992	  iter-169 attack on the body of \(\sigma_\times = \mathtt{gmScalingP1}\)
993	  (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}).
994	\end{definition}

1054	  % Archon-original Lean encoding of an elementary corollary of a Mathlib lemma. The
1055	  % external authority is the Mathlib statement
1056	  % \texttt{HomogeneousLocalization.Away.adjoin\_mk\_prod\_pow\_eq\_top}
1057	  % (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/GradedAlgebra/HomogeneousLocalization.lean:1064}); we do
1058	  % not include a verbatim \% SOURCE QUOTE because no Mathlib source quote is currently

1061	  For each \(i \in \{0, 1\}\), the ring map
1062	  \[ \mathrm{mvPolyToHomogeneousLocalizationAway}_i \colon
1063	     \mathrm{MvPolynomial}\,\{*\}\,\bar k \longrightarrow
1064	     \mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i \]
1065	  --- the inverse direction of the chart-ring iso of \cref{def:proj_chart_ring_iso},
1066	  built via \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial.eval}_2\mathrm{Hom}\) as the ring map sending the
1067	  generator \(u \in \mathrm{MvPolynomial}\,\{*\}\,\bar k\) to the homogeneous-localisation
1068	  element \(X_{1-i}/X_i \in \mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i\) and the constants
1069	  \(r \in \bar k\) along the composite $\bar k \to \mathcal A_0 \to \mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal
1070	  A\,X_i\( --- is surjective onto \)\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i$.
1071	  \medskip
1072	  \textit{Proof.} The image of \(\mathrm{mvPolyToHomogeneousLocalizationAway}_i\)
1073	  is the \(\mathcal A_0\)-subalgebra of \(\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i\) generated by the
1074	  single localised element \(X_{1-i}/X_i\), since \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\,\{*\}\,\bar k\)
1075	  is generated as a \(\bar k\)-algebra by its single generator \(u\) and the base-ring map
1076	  \(\bar k \to \mathcal A_0\) is bijective (via the canonical identification of degree-zero
1077	  scalars in the \(\bar k\)-algebra \(\bar k[X_0, X_1]\) with \(\bar k\)). Mathlib's lemma
1078	  \(\mathrm{HomogeneousLocalization.Away.adjoin\_mk\_prod\_pow\_eq\_top}\) says that for any
1079	  multi-index \(v \colon \iota' \to A\) of homogeneous elements of degree \(dv\) generating
1080	  \(\mathcal A\) up to degree \(d\), the \(\mathcal A_0\)-subalgebra of
1081	  \(\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,(\prod v_i^{dv_i})\) generated by the family of ratios
1082	  \(\{v_i^{dv_i}/\prod v_j^{dv_j}\}\) is the whole ring \(\top\). Specialising to
1083	  \(d = 1\), \(\iota' = \mathrm{Fin}\,2\), \(v = (X_0, X_1)\), and exponent vector
1084	  \(dv = (1, 1)\), the relevant generator \(\prod v_i^{dv_i} = X_0 \cdot X_1\) collapses
1085	  along the localisation at \(X_i\) (the \(X_i\)-component is already a unit, so only the
1086	  \(X_{1-i}/X_i\) ratio remains as a non-trivial generator), and the conclusion is that
1087	  \(\mathrm{Algebra.adjoin}\,\mathcal A_0\,\{X_{1-i}/X_i\} = \top\) inside
1088	  \(\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i\). This is exactly the image of
1089	  \(\mathrm{mvPolyToHomogeneousLocalizationAway}_i\), so the map is surjective. \qed
1090	\end{lemma}

1148	[\(\overline{\mathbb P^1}\) is reduced]
1149	  \label{lem:projlinebar_isReduced}

1156	  (\cref{def:projlinebar_affine_cover}) it suffices to show that each chart ring is
1157	  reduced. The chart-\(i\) ring \(\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i\) embeds into the ordinary
1158	  localization \(\bar k[X_0, X_1][X_i^{-1}]\) of the polynomial ring \(\bar k[X_0, X_1]\) at
1159	  the powers of \(X_i\) via the canonical injection $\mathrm{val} \colon

1190	  % pinned in this block's \lean{...} hook). The \(\mathbb G_a\)-translation \(\sigma\) (\lean name
1191	  % gaTranslationP1 [expected]) is retained only for the demoted additive alternative and is

1193	  \textbf{The scaling action (primary).} The \emph{multiplicative scaling action}
1194	  \[ \sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to \mathbb P^1, \qquad
1195	     \text{``}\sigma_\times(x, \lambda) = \lambda x\text{''} \]
1196	  (\lean name \texttt{gmScalingP1}) is a \emph{genuine total} scheme morphism on all of
1197	  \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m\) --- no indeterminacy, characteristic-free (M\"obius scaling
1198	  fixing both \(0\) and \(\infty\)) --- defined chartwise:
1199	  \begin{itemize}
1200	    \item On \(\mathbb A^1 \times \mathbb G_m\) (target chart \(x\)): \((x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x\),
1201	      a polynomial map, regular everywhere.
1202	    \item Near \(\infty\) (target chart \(u = 1/x\)): the target coordinate \(1/(\lambda x)\) rewrites as
1203	      \(u/\lambda\), regular everywhere on this chart because \(\lambda \in \mathbb G_m\) is invertible.
1204	  \end{itemize}
1205	  The two target charts cover the image and overlap consistently, so \(\sigma_\times\) is defined on
1206	  the whole surface. It is a left action of the group \(\mathbb G_m\) on \(\mathbb P^1\):
1207	  \(\sigma_\times(x, 1) = x\) and $\sigma_\times(\sigma_\times(x, \lambda), \mu) = \sigma_\times(x,
1208	  \lambda\mu)\(. Crucially it has \emph{two fixed points}, \)\sigma_\times(0, \lambda) = 0$ and
1209	  \(\sigma_\times(\infty, \lambda) = \infty\) for all \(\lambda\); the fixed point \(0\) is what makes the
1210	  \(W\)-axis of the additive decomposition collapse in the primary base-case proof
1211	  (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}, \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}).
1212	  \medskip
1213	  \noindent\textbf{Companion: the \(\mathbb G_a\)-translation action (demoted route only).} For the
1214	  Milne-faithful additive alternative we also record the \emph{translation action}
1215	  \[ \sigma \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_a \to \mathbb P^1, \qquad
1216	     \text{``}\sigma(x, y) = x + y\text{''} \]
1217	  (\lean name \texttt{gaTranslationP1} [expected]), likewise a \emph{total} scheme morphism on all
1218	  of \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_a\), defined chartwise: on \(\mathbb A^1 \times \mathbb G_a\)
1219	  (chart \(x\)) it is the polynomial map \((x, y) \mapsto x + y\); near \(\infty\) (\(u = 1/x\)) the
1220	  coordinate \(1/(x + y)\) rewrites as \(u/(1 + u y)\), regular at \(u = 0\) (value \(0\) there --- \(\sigma\)
1221	  fixes \(\infty\) for every \(y\)), while the locus \(x + y = 0\) is covered by the chart \(x\) on which it
1222	  is the finite value \(0\). It is a left action of \(\mathbb G_a\) on \(\mathbb P^1\) fixing \(\infty\):
1223	  \(\sigma(x, 0) = x\) and \(\sigma(\sigma(x, y), y') = \sigma(x, y + y')\). This companion underlies
1224	  only the alternative proof of \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial} and the demoted $\mathbb
1225	  G_a$-additive close of the base case (\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}); it is \emph{not} on the
1226	  genus-\(0\) critical path.
1227	\end{definition}

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:pic0_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper
33	geometrically integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\)
34	(\cref{def:genus}). By
35	\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
36	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of \(C/k\) is represented by
37	the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
38	separated, locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of
39	open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
40	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) stratifies its \(T\)-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
41	representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
42	the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
43	degree of a line bundle.
44	
45	The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) singled out by Kleiman is the open
46	and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
47	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
48	degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
49	abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\). This
50	chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.
51	
52	\section{The identity component of a group scheme}
53	\label{sec:identity_component_abstract}
54	
55	The identity component is an abstract feature of group schemes locally
56	of finite type over a field. We record the definition and its
57	open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure separately, so that the
58	Lean substrate
59	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} is reusable
60	outside the Picard context.
61	
62	\begin{definition}\leanok
63	[Identity component of a group scheme]
64	  \label{def:identity_component_group_scheme}
65	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}
66	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps;
67	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
68	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2851-L2863).
69	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
70	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lem:agps}
71	  %  Let \(k\) be a field, and \(G\) a group scheme locally of finite
72	  % type.  Let \(G^0\) denote the connected component of the identity element \(e\).
73	  %
74	  % \tu{(1)} Then \(G\) is separated.
75	  %
76	  % \tu{(2)} Then \(G\) is smooth if it has a geometrically
77	  % reduced open subscheme.
78	  %
79	  %  \tu{(3)} Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
80	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
81	  % extending \(k\).
82	  %  \end{lem}"
83	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps.}
84	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type
85	  whose identity section \(e : \Spec k \to G\) factors through \(G\). The
86	  \emph{identity component} of \(G\), denoted \(G^0\), is the locally closed
87	  subscheme of \(G\) whose underlying topological space is the connected
88	  component of \(|G|\) containing the image of \(e\), equipped with the
89	  reduced-induced (a priori) subscheme structure inherited from \(G\).
90	  In characteristic zero, or more generally when \(G\) has a
91	  geometrically reduced open subscheme, the substructure agrees with
92	  the open subscheme structure of \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
93	\end{definition}
94

105	[Identity component is a clopen subscheme]
106	  \label{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
107	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme}
108	
109	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
110	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
111	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
112	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
113	  % " \tu{(3)} Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
114	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
115	  % extending \(k\)."
116	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3).}
117	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
118	  type. Then the identity component \(G^0\)
119	  (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}) is an open and closed
120	  subscheme of \(G\): the inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is both an
121	  open immersion and a closed immersion of schemes.
122	\end{theorem}
123	
124	\begin{theorem}
125	
126	\leanok
127	[Identity component inclusion is a group-scheme homomorphism]
128	  \label{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}
129	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism}
130	
131	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
132	  % verbatim quote of (3) reproduced at
133	  % \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} above
134	  % (references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
135	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
136	  Lem.~lem:agps~(3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at
137	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}
138	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
139	  type. The clopen inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\)
140	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) is a homomorphism of
141	  \(k\)-group schemes: \(G^0\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure
142	  from \(G\), and the inclusion morphism in the over-category over
143	  \(\Spec k\) is compatible with the group laws on source and target.
144	\end{theorem}

146	\begin{theorem}
147	
148	\leanok
149	[Identity component is of finite type and geometrically irreducible]
150	  \label{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
151	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible}
152	
153	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
154	  % verbatim quote of (3) reproduced at
155	  % \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} above
156	  % (references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
157	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
158	  Lem.~lem:agps~(3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at
159	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}
160	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
161	  type. Then the identity component \(G^0\) is of finite type over
162	  \(k\) --- equivalently, locally of finite type \emph{and}
163	  quasi-compact --- and geometrically irreducible: after base change
164	  to the algebraic closure \(\bar k\), the underlying scheme
165	  \(G^0_{\bar k}\) is an irreducible \(\bar k\)-scheme.
166	\end{theorem}
167	
168	\begin{theorem}
169	
170	\leanok
171	[Formation of the identity component commutes with base change]
172	  \label{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
173	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso}
174	
175	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
176	  % verbatim quote of (3) reproduced at
177	  % \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} above
178	  % (references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
179	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
180	  Lem.~lem:agps~(3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at
181	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}
182	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
183	  type, and \(K/k\) a field extension. The natural map
184	  \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\), induced by base-changing the inclusion of
185	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} along \(\Spec K \to \Spec k\)
186	  and using the universal property of the identity component on
187	  \(G_K\), is an isomorphism of \(K\)-schemes.
188	\end{theorem}
189	
190	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
191	% L2864-L2911, proof of lem:agps; we reproduce the part dealing with
192	% (3).)
193	% "Consider (3).  By definition, \(G^0\) is the largest connected subspace
194	% containing \(e\).  But the closure of a connected subspace is plainly
195	% connected; so \(G^0\) is closed.  By \cite[6.1.9]{EGAI}, in any locally
196	% Noetherian topological space, the connected components are open.  Thus
197	% \(G^0\) is open too.
198	%
199	% Since \(G^0\) is connected and has a \(k\)-point, \(G^0\) is geometrically
200	% connected by \cite[4.5.14]{EGAIV2}.  Thus forming \(G^0\) commutes with
201	% extending \(k\).
202	%
203	% Furthermore, \(G^0\x G^0\) is connected by \cite[4.5.8]{EGAIV2}.
204	% So \(\alpha(G^0\x G^0)\subset G\) is connected, and contains \(e\),
205	% so lies in \(G^0\).  Thus \(G^0\) is a subgroup.
206	%
207	% To prove \(G^0\) is geometrically irreducible and quasi-compact, we may
208	% replace \(k\) by its algebraic closure.  Then \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) is
209	% reduced by \cite[4.6.1]{EGAIV2}.  Hence \(\alpha\) induces a map from
210	% \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) into \(G^0_{\red}\).  So \(G^0_{\red}\) is a
211	% subgroup.  Thus we may replace \(G^0\) by \(G^0_{\red}\).
212	% [\dots]
213	% Hence \(G^0\) is irreducible locally at \(h\).  But \(h\) is
214	% arbitrary, and \(G^0\) is connected.  So \(G^0\) is irreducible by
215	% \cite[6.1.10]{EGAI}.
216	% [\dots]
217	% Now, \(U\x U\) is affine, so quasi-compact.  Hence \(G^0\) is
218	% quasi-compact.  By hypothesis, \(G\) is locally of finite type. Hence
219	% \(G^0\) is of finite type.  Thus (3) holds."
220	\begin{proof}
221	  We establish
222	  \crefrange{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
223	  together, following Kleiman's proof of Lem.~lem:agps~(3). The four
224	  paragraphs below treat, in order: the clopen-subscheme structure
225	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}); base-change commutation
226	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}); the subgroup
227	  homomorphism (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism});
228	  and finite type plus geometric irreducibility
229	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}).
230	
231	  \emph{Clopen subscheme (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}).}
232	  Since \(e\) is a \(k\)-rational point, the singleton \(\{e\}\) is a closed
233	  point of \(G\). The connected component \(G^0\) of \(|G|\) at \(e\) is the
234	  largest connected subspace containing \(e\); the closure of a connected
235	  subspace is connected, hence \(G^0\) is closed. On the other hand, a
236	  locally Noetherian topological space has open connected components
237	  (EGA I 6.1.9), so \(G^0 \subseteq |G|\) is also open. Equip \(G^0\) with
238	  the open-subscheme structure; the inclusion
239	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is then both an open and a closed immersion.
240	
241	  \emph{Base-change commutation
242	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}).}
243	  Geometric connectedness of \(G^0\) follows from the existence of the
244	  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in G^0(k)\) (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.14); in
245	  particular for any field extension \(K/k\) the base change
246	  \((G^0)_K\) is connected, contains the image of the identity section
247	  of \(G_K\), and is open and closed in \(G_K\) (since open- and
248	  closed-immersion both descend under base change). Hence
249	  \((G^0)_K\) coincides, as a subscheme of \(G_K\), with the identity
250	  component \((G_K)^0\) of \(G_K\); the natural comparison map
251	  \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) is therefore an isomorphism.
252	
253	  \emph{Subgroup homomorphism
254	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}).}
255	  The product \(G^0 \times_k G^0\) is connected
256	  (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.8). Let \(\alpha : G \times_k G \to G\),
257	  \(\alpha(g, h) := g h^{-1}\), be the algebraic-group structure map.
258	  Since \(\alpha(G^0 \times_k G^0)\) is connected and contains \(e\), its
259	  image lies in \(G^0\). Hence \(G^0\) is closed under the group
260	  operation (and under inversion, by taking \(g = e\)): the inclusion
261	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is a homomorphism of \(k\)-group schemes,
262	  and \(G^0\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure from \(G\).
263	
264	  \emph{Finite type and geometric irreducibility
265	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}).}
266	  To establish geometric irreducibility and finite type, base-change
267	  to \(\overline k\) (which is permitted because formation of \(G^0\)
268	  commutes with field extension by the previous paragraph). Over
269	  \(\overline k\), the product
270	  \(G^0_{\red} \times G^0_{\red}\) is reduced (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.6.1), so
271	  \(G^0_{\red}\) is itself a subgroup; we may therefore replace \(G^0\) by
272	  \(G^0_{\red}\) for the geometric-irreducibility argument. Pick a
273	  nonempty smooth affine open subscheme \(U \subseteq G^0\) (such a \(U\)
274	  exists because \(G^0_{\red}\) is reduced and \(\overline k\) is
275	  algebraically closed). For any \(\overline k\)-point \(h \in G^0\) the
276	  translate \(hg^{-1} U\) (for \(g \in U(\overline k)\)) is a smooth open
277	  neighbourhood of \(h\), so \(G^0\) is locally irreducible at every closed
278	  point. Combined with connectedness, this gives irreducibility (EGA I
279	  6.1.10). The image \(\alpha(U \times U) = G^0\) is the surjective image
280	  of the affine, hence quasi-compact, scheme \(U \times U\), so \(G^0\) is
281	  quasi-compact; together with the locally-finite-type hypothesis on
282	  \(G\), this makes \(G^0\) of finite type over \(k\).
283	\end{proof}

286	\label{sec:pic0_definition}
287	
288	We now specialise the abstract identity-component construction to
289	\(G = \Pic_{C/k}\).
290	
291	\begin{definition}\leanok
292	[The identity component of the Picard scheme]
293	  \label{def:pic_zero_subscheme}
294	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme}
295	  
296	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, opening + Prp.~prp:pic0;

298	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2836-L2841 +
299	  % L2921-L2929).

303	  % \(\IPicz_{X/S}\) of the connected components of the identity element,
304	  % \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\), for \(s\in S\)."
305	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
306	  % "\begin{prp}\label{prp:pic0}

308	  % exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}\).  Then \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is

316	  Prp.~prp:pic0.}
317	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a field
318	  \(k\). The \emph{identity component of the Picard scheme}

328	
329	\section{The degree map}
330	\label{sec:pic_degree}
331	
332	The disjoint-union structure of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
333	(\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union of open
334	quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) is indexed by the Hilbert polynomial
335	\(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) of a representing invertible sheaf
336	(\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}). For a smooth proper geometrically
337	integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g\) with a fixed degree-one polarisation
338	\(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\), the Hilbert polynomial of an invertible
339	sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is, by Riemann--Roch,
340	\(\Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n) = n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g\), so it is
341	linear in \(n\) with leading coefficient $\deg \mathcal{L} \in
342	\mathbb{Z}$. This identifies the connected components of
343	\(\Pic_{C/k}(\overline k)\) with the integers, the index being the
344	degree.
345	
346	\begin{definition}\leanok
347	[Degree of a Picard class]
348	  \label{def:divisor_degree_pic}
349	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}
350	  
351	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
352	  % \S III.1, ``Definitions and main statements'', p.~88 (PDF p.~94)
353	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf).
354	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
355	  % "Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve over \(k\). The degree of a
356	  % divisor \(D = n_i P_i\) on \(C\) is \(n_i [k(P_i):k]\). Since every
357	  % invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is of the form \(\mathcal{L}(D)\)
358	  % for some divisor \(D\), and \(D\) is uniquely determined up to linear
359	  % equivalence, we can define \(\deg(\mathcal{L}) = \deg(D)\). Then
360	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L}^n) = \deg(n D) = n \deg(D)\), and the Riemann-Roch
361	  % theorem says that \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n) = n \deg(\mathcal{L}) + 1 - g\).
362	  % This gives a more canonical description of \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\): when
363	  % \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n)\) is written as a polynomial in \(n\),
364	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\) is the leading coefficient. We write
365	  % \(\Pic^0(C)\) for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves
366	  % of degree zero on \(C\)."
367	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
368	  \S III.1, p.~88.}
369	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
370	  \(g\), and let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
371	  \cref{def:pic_scheme}. The \emph{degree map}
372	  \[
373	    \deg \;\colon\; \Pic_{C/k}(k) \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}
374	  \]
375	  is defined as follows: a \(k\)-point \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\)
376	  represents (after \'etale base change) an invertible sheaf
377	  \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\); its Hilbert polynomial with respect to a fixed
378	  degree-one polarisation \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\) is, by
379	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial} and Riemann--Roch,
380	  \[
381	    \Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n)
382	    \;=\; \chi(C, \mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{O}_C(n))
383	    \;=\; n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g,
384	  \]
385	  a polynomial in \(n\) linear in \(n\). The integer
386	  \(\deg(\lambda) := \deg \mathcal{L}\) is the leading coefficient of
387	  this polynomial, well-defined on the isomorphism class
388	  \([\mathcal{L}]\) and (because \(\Pic_{C/k}\) represents the
389	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor) on the \(k\)-point
390	  \(\lambda\). The degree map is a group homomorphism: for
391	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}\) invertible sheaves on \(C\) one has
392	  \(\deg(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}) = \deg \mathcal{L} + \deg \mathcal{M}\),
393	  because the Euler characteristic is additive on the additive groups of
394	  twists. Equivalently, the degree on \(k\)-points is the index of the
395	  connected component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing \(\lambda\) under the
396	  Hilbert-polynomial decomposition of
397	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: the disjoint union
398	  \(\Pic_{C/k} = \coprod_{d \in \mathbb{Z}} \Pic^d_{C/k}\) is indexed by
399	  the degree, with \(\Pic^d_{C/k}\) the component representing classes
400	  of degree \(d\).
401	\end{definition}
402	
403	\section{The Pic-zero abelian variety}
404	\label{sec:pic0_abelian_variety}
405	
406	The terminal statement of the chapter identifies \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) with
407	an abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\).
408	Recall (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8) that a \(k\)-group scheme is an
409	\emph{abelian variety} iff it is a complete (proper) connected group
410	variety over \(k\); commutativity and projectivity then follow
411	automatically.
412	
413	% The three conclusions of the original ``\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian
414	% variety of dimension \(g\)'' theorem are split below into per-conclusion
415	% blocks, each with its own \lean{...} pin. The Kleiman ex:jac + rmk:Jac
416	% verbatim source quote (covering the abelian-variety structure) is
417	% reproduced once at \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}; the Milne
418	% III.1.4(e) verbatim quote (covering the dimension formula) is at
419	% \cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}; the Milne III.1 quote
420	% characterising \(\Pic^0(C)\) as degree-zero classes is already reproduced
421	% in \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} and referenced from
422	% \cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}. The combined proof,
423	% sharing the \texttt{\% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:} comment, follows the third
424	% block.
425	
426	\begin{theorem}\leanok
427	[\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety]
428	  \label{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}
429	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety}
430	
431	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Ex.~ex:jac + Rmk.~rmk:Jac;
432	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~50--51 (read from

480	  % \(\Jac(C) = C\) (provided \(C\) has a rational point; otherwise it differs

482	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),

494	\begin{theorem}
495	
496	\leanok
497	[\(k\)-points of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) are the kernel of the degree map]
498	  \label{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}
499	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.kPoints_iff_kerDegree}
500	
501	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
502	  % \S III.1, p.~88; verbatim quote reproduced at
503	  % \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} (read from
504	  % references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF p.~94).
505	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
506	  \S III.1, p.~88 (``\(\Pic^0(C)\)\dots\ degree zero''); the verbatim
507	  source quote is at \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}.}
508	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
509	  \(g\) over a field \(k\). A \(k\)-point
510	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the image of the inclusion of
511	  the identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
512	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} applied to
513	  \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\), specialised in \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme})
514	  if and only if the degree
515	  \(\deg(\lambda) \in \mathbb{Z}\) of \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}
516	  vanishes. Equivalently,
517	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker\bigl(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z}\bigr)\).
518	\end{theorem}
519	
520	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (substrate construction --- the relevant source
521	% statements above prove the existence + properties; we assemble the
522	% conclusion from those statements together with the abstract
523	% identity-component substrate.)
524	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2935-L2939,
525	% Thm.~th:qpp&p, on smoothness/projectivity of \(\IPicz_{X/k}\):)
526	% "\begin{thm}\label{th:qpp&p}
527	%  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(X/k\) is projective
528	% and geometrically integral.  Then \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) exists and is
529	% quasi-projective.  If also \(X/k\) is geometrically normal, then
530	% \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is projective."
531	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3421-L3426,
532	% Cor.~cor:sm, dimension equals \(h^1(\mathcal O)\) when smooth:)
533	% "\begin{cor}\label{cor:sm} Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field
534	% \(k\). Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Then
535	% \(\)\dim \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\)
536	%  Equality holds if and only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(0;\) if so, then
537	% \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\)
538	% everywhere."
539	\begin{proof}
540	  We establish
541	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety},
542	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}, and
543	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero} in turn, assembling
544	  the conclusions from the identity-component substrate of
545	  \cref{sec:identity_component_abstract} together with
546	  Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~th:qpp\&p, Cor.~cor:sm, and Ex.~ex:jac, and
547	  Milne~\S I.1 Cor.~1.4 (commutativity) + Rmk.~III.1.4(e) (dimension).
548	
549	  \emph{Abelian-variety structure
550	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}).} We assemble the four
551	  conjuncts from four inputs.
552	
553	  (i) \emph{Clopen subgroup of finite type, geometrically irreducible.}
554	  By
555	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup,thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
556	  applied to \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) (a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
557	  type by
558	  \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme,thm:fga_pic_representability}),
559	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
560	  of finite type over \(k\), geometrically irreducible, and its
561	  formation commutes with extending \(k\). In particular \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
562	  is separated and locally of finite type as a subscheme of
563	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\), and connected.
564	
565	  (ii) \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} The disjoint-union structure of
566	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union
567	  of open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) restricted to \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
568	  expresses the identity component as a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme
569	  (Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p: \(X/k\) projective and geometrically
570	  integral \(\Longrightarrow\) \(\Pic^0_{X/k}\) is quasi-projective).
571	
572	  (iii) \emph{Projectivity (properness).} For a smooth proper
573	  geometrically integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g > 0\), \(C/k\) is
574	  also geometrically normal (the smoothness implies geometric
575	  regularity, hence geometric normality). Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p
576	  then upgrades the quasi-projective conclusion to projective:
577	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is projective over \(k\), in particular proper.
578	  The proof of th:qpp\&p uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure
579	  theorem to reduce to ruling out non-constant maps
580	  \(\mathbb G_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\), which on a geometrically normal
581	  \(X\) becomes a divisor-theoretic computation.
582	
583	  (iv) \emph{Smoothness.} For a smooth proper curve \(C/k\), the
584	  Picard scheme is smooth at \(0\): this is Exercise ex:jac of
585	  Kleiman~\S 5, which asserts that for \(X/S\) projective and flat
586	  with integral geometric fibres of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), the
587	  generalised Jacobians \(\Pic^0_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
588	  quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\); specialising
589	  to \(S = \Spec k\) and \(X = C\) a smooth proper curve of (geometric
590	  = arithmetic) genus \(g\) gives smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\).
591	
592	  Combining (i)--(iv): \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth, projective,
593	  geometrically irreducible \(k\)-group scheme. A proper connected
594	  \(k\)-group variety is by definition an \emph{abelian variety}
595	  (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8), and is automatically commutative (Milne~\S I.1,
596	  Cor.~1.4). This establishes
597	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}.
598	
599	  \emph{Dimension equals the genus
600	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}).} By Kleiman~\S 5,
601	  Cor.~cor:sm, the inequality
602	  \(\dim \Pic_{C/k} \le \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\) holds, with
603	  equality if and only if \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\), in which
604	  case \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim_k H^1(C,
605	  \mathcal{O}_C)\) everywhere. Step (iv) above showed
606	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\); equality therefore holds on
607	  the open identity component, and
608	  \(\dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g(C)\)
609	  by \cref{def:genus}. This is Milne~\S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e): ``The
610	  dimension of \(J\) is the genus of \(C\)''.
611	
612	  \emph{Identification with the kernel of the degree map
613	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}).} A class
614	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the connected component of
615	  the identity --- i.e.\ in the image of the open immersion
616	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
617	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) --- precisely when
618	  the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf coincides
619	  with that of \(\mathcal{O}_C\), equivalently when its degree (the
620	  leading coefficient of the Hilbert polynomial of
621	  \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is zero. Hence
622	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z})\),
623	  which is the content of Milne~\S III.1, p.~88 (the verbatim quote
624	  at \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} explicitly writes ``\(\Pic^0(C)\)
625	  for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves of degree
626	  zero on \(C\)'').
627	\end{proof}

629	\section{Lean encoding}
630	\label{sec:pic0_lean_encoding}
631	
632	The Lean target file is
633	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/IdentityComponent.lean}, sibling to
634	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean}
635	(\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}). The blueprint declarations
636	of this chapter correspond to Lean targets as follows.
637	
638	\begin{itemize}
639	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}
640	    (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}) is a structural
641	    constant naming the open-and-closed identity-component subscheme
642	    of a \(k\)-group scheme \(G\) locally of finite type. The construction
643	    is the abstract substrate of Kleiman~\S 5 Lem.~lem:agps,
644	    independent of the Picard context; it is new project material
645	    (\emph{not} yet in Mathlib).
646	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme}
647	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) packages the
648	    clopen-subscheme inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) (open immersion
649	    \emph{and} closed immersion) --- conclusion (a) of Kleiman~\S 5
650	    Lem.~lem:agps~(3).
651	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism}
652	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}) packages
653	    the group-homomorphism property of the inclusion of
654	    \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}: \(G^0\) inherits a
655	    \(k\)-group-scheme structure from \(G\) compatible with the
656	    inclusion. Conclusion (b) of Kleiman~\S 5 Lem.~lem:agps~(3).
657	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible}
658	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible})
659	    packages the finite-type-ness and geometric-irreducibility
660	    conclusions of Kleiman~\S 5 Lem.~lem:agps~(3) (conclusion (c)).
661	    In Lean these are the instances
662	    \texttt{[LocallyOfFiniteType (IdentityComponent G).hom]},
663	    \texttt{[QuasiCompact (IdentityComponent G).hom]}, and
664	    \texttt{[GeometricallyIrreducible (IdentityComponent G).hom]}.
665	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso}
666	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}) packages the
667	    base-change-commutation conclusion (d) of Kleiman~\S 5
668	    Lem.~lem:agps~(3): for any field extension \(K/k\), the natural
669	    map \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) (in Lean,
670	    \texttt{(IdentityComponent G)\_K \(\to\) IdentityComponent (G\_K)})
671	    is an isomorphism of \(K\)-schemes.
672	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme}
673	    (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is the open subscheme of
674	    \(\Pic_{C/k}\) obtained by applying the
675	    \texttt{IdentityComponent} substrate to
676	    \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\), inheriting the \(k\)-group-scheme structure from
677	    \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}.
678	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}
679	    (\cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is the group homomorphism
680	    \(\Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z}\) extracting the leading coefficient
681	    of the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf
682	    relative to a fixed degree-one polarisation \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on
683	    \(C\). On the disjoint-union decomposition of
684	    \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability} the degree is the index of the
685	    component.
686	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety}
687	    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}) is the bundled
688	    abelian-variety structure on \texttt{Pic0Scheme}: the four-conjunct
689	    conjunction \texttt{[IsProper]}, \texttt{[Smooth]},
690	    \texttt{[GeometricallyIrreducible]} on the structural morphism
691	    \texttt{(Pic0Scheme C).hom} together with
692	    \texttt{Nonempty (GrpObj (Pic0Scheme C))}.
693	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.finrank_eq_genus}
694	    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}) is the dimension
695	    formula \(\dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)\) (Milne~\S III.1,
696	    Rmk.~1.4(e)). In Lean this is the equation
697	    \texttt{Module.finrank k (Pic0Scheme C).left = AlgebraicGeometry.genus C}
698	    (precise statement via the project's Krull-dimension API on the
699	    underlying scheme).
700	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.kPoints_iff_kerDegree}
701	    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}) is the
702	    \(k\)-point-characterisation
703	    \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg)\) (Milne~\S III.1, p.~88). In Lean,
704	    \texttt{∀ λ : Spec (.of k) ⟶ (PicScheme C).left,
705	    (∃ μ : Spec (.of k) ⟶ (Pic0Scheme C).left,\,
706	    μ ≫ pic0Inclusion = λ) ↔ PicScheme.degree C λ = 0}.

725	\section{Out of scope}
726	\label{sec:pic0_out_of_scope}
727	
728	This chapter packages \emph{only} the identity component
729	\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as an open-and-closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
730	its degree-zero characterisation, and its abelian-variety structure.
731	The following downstream constructions live in separate sibling
732	chapters and are explicitly out of scope here:
733	
734	\begin{itemize}
735	  \item \textbf{Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
736	    (A.4.d.ii) --- the universal property of \(f_P : C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\)
737	    is the content of \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}, gated on this
738	    chapter via \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}.
739	  \item \textbf{The autoduality \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \cong (\Pic^0_{C/k})^\vee\)}
740	    (Milne~\S III.6, Thm.~6.6) --- not needed for the Jacobian's
741	    existence as an abelian variety, hence out of scope.
742	  \item \textbf{The torsion component \(\Pic^\tau_{C/k}\)}
743	    (Kleiman~\S 6) --- for a smooth proper curve
744	    \(\Pic^\tau_{C/k} = \Pic^0_{C/k}\), so the finer torsion-component
745	    finiteness statements of Kleiman~\S 6 are not load-bearing on
746	    Route~A.
747	  \item \textbf{Construction of the degree map as a morphism of group
748	    schemes \(\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb{Z}}_k\).} This chapter
749	    defines the degree on \(k\)-points only; the upgrade to a
750	    morphism of group schemes (with the constant group scheme
751	    \(\underline{\mathbb{Z}}_k\)) is the natural assembly statement but
752	    is not required for any downstream consumer in this project.
753	\end{itemize}

1547	      corresponding to that \((x, \lambda)\)), and bypasses the simp coverage gap
1548	      that obstructs (III.a)/(III.b) entirely.
1549	      \emph{Concrete prover recipe.} The path closes in four steps:
1550	      \begin{enumerate}
1551	        \item Fix the diagonal
1552	          \(\Delta_{\overline{\mathbb P^1}} \colon \overline{\mathbb P^1}
1553	            \longrightarrow \overline{\mathbb P^1} \times_{\bar k}
1554	            \overline{\mathbb P^1}\)
1555	          via Mathlib's \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Limits.prod.lift}\,(\mathbf
1556	          1_{\overline{\mathbb P^1}})\,(\mathbf 1_{\overline{\mathbb P^1}})\), and
1557	          obtain the closed-immersion witness from
1558	          \(\mathtt{IsSeparated.diagonal\_isClosedImmersion}\) applied to the
1559	          separated structure morphism
1560	          \(\overline{\mathbb P^1}.\mathrm{hom} \colon \overline{\mathbb P^1} \to
1561	          \Spec \bar k\).
1562	        \item Build the pair-morphism
1563	          \((\mathrm{chart}_0, \mathrm{chart}_1) \colon
1564	            \mathrm{pullback}\,((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_0.f)\,
1565	              ((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_1.f)
1566	            \longrightarrow
1567	            \overline{\mathbb P^1} \times_{\bar k} \overline{\mathbb P^1}\)
1568	          via \(\mathtt{prod.lift}\) on the two named chart restrictions above.
1569	        \item Show this pair factors through \(\Delta_{\overline{\mathbb P^1}}\)
1570	          via the universal property of the intersection's pullback structure
1571	          combined with \(\mathtt{IsClosedImmersion.lift}\) (or the
1572	          \(\mathtt{\_iff\_range\_subset}\) variant, depending on which lifts
1573	          cleanly): produce the section
1574	          \(\mathrm{(intersection)} \to \overline{\mathbb P^1}\) whose post-
1575	          composition with \(\Delta\) equals \((\mathrm{chart}_0,
1576	          \mathrm{chart}_1)\).
1577	        \item The factorization reduces, by composing with the two projections
1578	          \(\mathtt{prod.fst}\) and \(\mathtt{prod.snd}\) out of the codomain
1579	          \(\overline{\mathbb P^1} \times_{\bar k} \overline{\mathbb P^1}\), to
1580	          two scheme-equalities on the components --- each of which is a direct
1581	          chart-bridge equality on \((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_i\)'s structure
1582	          (i.e.\ the \emph{single-chart} content, not the full cocycle), discharged
1583	          using the same chart-ring iso \cref{def:proj_chart_ring_iso} and
1584	          \(\mathtt{pullbackSpecIso}\) bridge that \cref{def:gmscaling_chart} uses.
1585	      \end{enumerate}
1586	      \emph{Substrate hooks.} Every Mathlib lemma named above is present at the
1587	      pinned Mathlib commit \texttt{b80f227}:
1588	      \(\mathtt{IsSeparated.diagonal\_isClosedImmersion}\),
1589	      \(\mathtt{IsClosedImmersion.lift}\) (and its
1590	      \(\mathtt{\_iff\_range\_subset}\) characterisation),
1591	      \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Limits.prod.lift}\),
1592	      \(\mathtt{pullback.lift}\), together with the standard
1593	      \(\mathtt{IsSeparated}\) instance for \(\Proj\) of a graded ring (Stacks
1594	      \texttt{01KU} / Hartshorne~II.4.6 diagonal-is-closed characterisation; the
1595	      Mathlib categorical packaging is available without an
1596	      \(\mathtt{Algebra.TensorProduct.isDomain\_of\_isAlgClosed\_left}\)-style shim).

1598	      chasing, with no further Mathlib gaps anticipated.

1600	  \medskip
1601	  \noindent\textbf{(IV) Substantive ring-level residual.} All three pickup paths reduce,
1602	  after their structural setup, to the same ring-level identity. On the basic-open
1603	  intersection \(D_+(X_0 \cdot X_1) \subseteq \overline{\mathbb P^1}\) both chart
1604	  coordinates are units (the chart-\(0\) coordinate \(t = X_1/X_0\) and the chart-\(1\)
1605	  coordinate \(u = X_0/X_1\) are reciprocals: \(t \cdot u = 1\)), and the agreement
1606	  reduces to the ring-level identity
1607	  \[ \lambda \cdot u \ =\ \frac{1}{t} \cdot \lambda \quad
1608	     \text{in } \mathrm{Localization.Away}\,t \otimes_{\bar k} \mathtt{GmRing}\,\bar k, \]
1609	  i.e.\ to the unit-of-the-localisation identity \(t \cdot u = 1\)
1610	  (\(\mathtt{IsLocalization.Away.mul\_invSelf}\)) together with commutativity of the
1611	  tensor product. The proof routes through the chart-ring iso
1612	  \cref{def:proj_chart_ring_iso} and the same \(\mathtt{pullbackSpecIso}\) bridge that
1613	  \cref{def:gmscaling_chart} uses; consequently \cref{lem:gmscaling_chart_agreement}
1614	  remains gated on \cref{def:gmscaling_chart} acquiring its concrete body \emph{and} on
1615	  one of the (III.a)--(III.c) structural setups landing. See
1616	  \(\mathtt{analogies/gmscaling{\text-}projection{\text-}idiom.md}\) (iter-184) for the
1617	  full Recipe~1/2/3 decomposition and empirical simp-probe verification.
1618	\end{lemma}

1755

1757	  Lean encoding of this fact, with \(\mathrm{toUnit}_{\mathbb G_m}\) supplying the
1758	  \(\Spec \bar k\)-factor and \(\mathbf 1_{\mathbb G_m}\) the parameter \(\lambda\).

1870	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.9.}
1871	  \textbf{Off the genus-\(0\) critical path (iter-164).} This \(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0\)
1872	  lemma is \emph{superseded by the \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut}
1873	  (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}, \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}), which closes the
1874	  base case without it. It is retained only as the classical Milne Proposition~3.9 ($\mathbb
1875	  G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility) argument feeding the alternative route
1876	  \cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}; no block on the genus-\(0\) critical path depends on it.
1877	  Let \(A\) be an abelian variety over the algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), and let
1878	  \(\varphi \colon \mathbb G_a \to A\) be a homomorphism of group objects (so \(\varphi(0) = 0_A\) and
1879	  \(\varphi(x + y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y)\)). Then \(\varphi\) is the trivial homomorphism: it
1880	  factors through the terminal object as the constant morphism at the identity,
1881	  \(\varphi = \mathtt{toUnit}\,\mathbb G_a \fatsemi \eta_A\).
1882	  \medskip
1883	  \noindent\textbf{Equivalent completeness phrasing.} Equivalently: a homomorphism from an
1884	  \emph{affine connected} group object into a \emph{complete} group object is trivial. That is the
1885	  abstract content; the proof below realises it characteristic-free.
1886	\end{lemma}
1887	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. According to (3.2), α extends to a regular map on the whole of A¹.
1888	% After composing α with a translation, we may suppose that α(0)=0. Then α is a homomorphism,
1889	% α(x+y)=α(x)+α(y); all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. But A¹−{0} is also a group variety, and similarly,
1890	% α(xy)=α(x)+α(y)+c; all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. This is absurd, unless α is constant." [Milne,
1891	% Prop 3.9, \S I.3, doc p.~19--20.]
1892	\begin{proof}
1893	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.9.}
1894	  Write \(A\) additively (an abelian variety is commutative). We give the conceptual
1895	  \emph{completeness} proof as the abstract argument, then record Milne's elementary $\mathbb
1896	  G_a/\mathbb G_m\( incompatibility, which applies whenever \)\varphi$ extends to a morphism on
1897	  \(\mathbb P^1\) (as it does in the genus-\(0\) application).
1898	  \textbf{Completeness proof.} The image \(\varphi(\mathbb G_a)\) is a connected subgroup of \(A\)
1899	  (image of the connected \(\mathbb G_a\) under a homomorphism). As the image of a homomorphism of
1900	  algebraic groups it is a \emph{closed} subgroup of \(A\), hence \emph{complete} (closed in the
1901	  complete \(A\)); as a homomorphic image of the \emph{affine} \(\mathbb G_a\) it is moreover
1902	  \emph{affine}. A connected variety that is simultaneously complete and affine over \(\bar k\) is a
1903	  single point: its ring of global regular functions is a field finite over \(\bar k\), hence equal
1904	  to \(\bar k\) (the \(\Gamma = \bar k\) collapse already packaged for proper integral sources in
1905	  \cref{lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}). Therefore \(\varphi(\mathbb G_a)\) is one point;
1906	  being a homomorphism, \(\varphi\) sends \(0\) there, so the point is \(0_A\) and
1907	  \(\varphi = \mathtt{toUnit}\,\mathbb G_a \fatsemi \eta_A\).
1908	  \medskip
1909	  \noindent\textbf{Milne's elementary \(\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m\) incompatibility (when \(\varphi\)
1910	  extends to \(\mathbb P^1\)).} Suppose \(\varphi = f|_{\mathbb G_a}\) for a morphism
1911	  \(f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A\) (the genus-\(0\) situation). Set \(g := f - f(1)\), so \(g(1) = 0_A\), and
1912	  form \(h_\times := \sigma_\times \fatsemi g \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to A\) with the
1913	  scaling companion \(\sigma_\times\) of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1}. Applying the additive
1914	  decomposition \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} with \(V = \mathbb P^1\) (complete),
1915	  \(W = \mathbb G_m\), base points \(1 \in \mathbb P^1\) and \(1 \in \mathbb G_m\) (so
1916	  \(h_\times(1, 1) = g(1) = 0_A\)), the axis restrictions are \(x \mapsto g(x)\) and
1917	  \(\lambda \mapsto g(\lambda)\), whence \(g(\lambda x) = g(x) + g(\lambda)\) on \(\bar k^\times\);
1918	  re-expanding \(g\) gives the multiplicative relation
1919	  \(\varphi(xy) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y) + c\) with \(c = -\varphi(1)\). Now \(\varphi\) is at once
1920	  additive, \(\varphi(x + y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y)\), and ``multiplicative-additive'',
1921	  \(\varphi(xy) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y) + c\). Computing \(\varphi((x + x')\,y)\) in two ways for
1922	  \(x, x' \in \bar k\), \(y \in \bar k^\times\),
1923	  \[ \varphi((x+x')y) = \varphi(xy) + \varphi(x'y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(x') + 2\varphi(y) + 2c, \]
1924	  while also $\varphi((x+x')y) = \varphi(x+x') + \varphi(y) + c = \varphi(x) + \varphi(x') +
1925	  \varphi(y) + c\(; equating gives \)\varphi(y) = -c = \varphi(1)\( for every \)y \in \bar k^\times$.
1926	  Hence \(\varphi\) is constant on the dense \(\mathbb G_m \subset \mathbb G_a\), so constant on
1927	  \(\mathbb G_a\) (density, \(A\) separated), with value \(\varphi(0) = 0_A\). This is Milne's argument
1928	  verbatim and uses only the proven \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} plus the scaling action,
1929	  avoiding the ``image is a closed subgroup'' input of the completeness proof. \qedhere
1930	\end{proof}
1931	\begin{remark}
1932	  \label{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}
1933	  \textbf{The primary route is the \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut; why no Theorem~3.2 and no
1934	  \(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0\) are needed.} The genus-\(0\) base case ``$f \colon \mathbb P^1
1935	  \to A$ is constant'' is closed \emph{directly} by the proven additive decomposition
1936	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} composed with a total \(\mathbb G\)-action on \(\mathbb P^1\),
1937	  with \emph{no} appeal to Milne's Theorem~3.2 / Lemma~3.3 (rational-map extension), the theorem of
1938	  the cube, Auslander--Buchsbaum, or differentials. The reason the earlier draft thought
1939	  Theorem~3.2 was needed --- that the additive defect \(\psi(x,y) = f(x+y) - f(x) - f(y)\) lives only
1940	  on the affine \(\mathbb G_a \times \mathbb G_a\) and must be extended over $\mathbb P^1 \times
1941	  \mathbb P^1$ --- evaporates once one composes with a \emph{total} action morphism of
1942	  \cref{def:gaTranslationP1}: the composite is already a total morphism out of $\mathbb P^1 \times
1943	  \mathbb G\(, and the complete factor is \)\mathbb P^1$, exactly what
1944	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} (whose Lean signature needs only the first factor
1945	  complete) consumes.
1946	  \medskip
1947	  \noindent\textbf{Primary route --- the scaling shortcut (strategy decision of iter-164).} The
1948	  \emph{multiplicative} scaling action $\sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to
1949	  \mathbb P^1\(, \)(x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}), closes the base case
1950	  in one shot, \emph{without} the additive homomorphism step or \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial} at
1951	  all. Normalise \(f(0) = 0_A\) (translate; here \(0\) is a scaling fixed point). Form $h_\times :=
1952	  \sigma_\times \fatsemi f\( and apply \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} with \)V = \mathbb P^1$,
1953	  \(W = \mathbb G_m\), base points \(0 \in \mathbb P^1\) and \(1 \in \mathbb G_m\) (so $h_\times(0, 1) =
1954	  f(0) = 0_A\(). Because \)0\( is a \emph{fixed point} of scaling, the \)W$-axis restriction collapses:
1955	  \(\lambda \mapsto h_\times(0, \lambda) = f(\lambda \cdot 0) = f(0) = 0_A\). The decomposition
1956	  therefore reads \(h_\times = p \fatsemi f\) with the second summand the group identity, i.e.
1957	  \[ \sigma_\times \fatsemi f = \mathrm{pr}_1 \fatsemi f \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to A,
1958	     \qquad\text{``}f(\lambda x) = f(x)\text{''}. \]
1959	  Precomposing with \(\lambda \mapsto (1, \lambda)\) gives \(f|_{\mathbb G_m} = \text{const}\,f(1)\);
1960	  since \(\mathbb G_m\) is dense in \(\mathbb P^1\) and \(A\) is separated, \(f\) is constant on $\mathbb
1961	  P^1$ (the dominant-source/separated-target handle \cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}, i.e.\ the
1962	  \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\) family already used by \texttt{rigidity\_core}). This uses
1963	  \emph{only} the proven \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, the total morphism \(\sigma_\times\),
1964	  and density --- no \(\mathbb G_a\) additivity, no \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}, and in
1965	  particular \emph{no} ``image of a group homomorphism is a closed subgroup'' input (a deep Mathlib
1966	  gap). It is the least-Mathlib-blocked realisation of the base case and is the route the live
1967	  consumer \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} formalises.
1968	  \medskip
1969	  \noindent\textbf{Retained alternative --- the \(\mathbb G_a\)-additive route.} The Milne-faithful
1970	  argument routes through the translation action $\sigma \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_a \to
1971	  \mathbb P^1\(: \)\sigma \fatsemi f\( is total, the additive decomposition makes \)f|_{\mathbb G_a}$ a
1972	  
1973	  homomorphism \(\mathbb G_a \to A\) (\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}), and a homomorphism from the
1974	  
1975	  additive group into an abelian variety is trivial (\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}, Milne's
1976	  \(\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m\) incompatibility, Proposition~3.9). This reaches the same conclusion but
1977	  needs the triviality lemma; it is retained for fidelity to Milne and is \emph{off} the genus-\(0\)
1978	  critical path.
1979	\end{remark}

2011	  \emph{4th route} (\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}): the additive defect map is never built on

2014	  additive decomposition applies directly. Milne's Theorem~3.2
2015	  (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) is \emph{not} used.
2016	  \textbf{\(f|_{\mathbb G_a}\) is an additive homomorphism, via the total action \(\sigma\).} Form
2017	  \[ h := \sigma \fatsemi f \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_a \to A, \qquad
2018	     \text{``}h(x, y) = f(x + y)\text{''}, \]
2019	  using the translation action \(\sigma\) of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1}; this is a \emph{total}
2020	  morphism (no rational-map extension needed). Apply the additive decomposition
2021	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} (Milne Cor~1.5) --- whose Lean signature requires only the
2022	  \emph{first} factor complete --- with \(V = \mathbb P^1\) (proper), \(W = \mathbb G_a\), and base
2023	  points \(0 \in \mathbb P^1\), \(0 \in \mathbb G_a\), noting \(h(0, 0) = f(0) = 0_A\). The axis
2024	  restrictions are \(x \mapsto h(x, 0) = f(x)\) and \(y \mapsto h(0, y) = f(y)\), so the decomposition
2025	  \(h = (p \fatsemi f) + (q \fatsemi (f|_{\mathbb G_a}))\) reads, on \(\bar k\)-points,
2026	  \[ f(x + y) = f(x) + f(y), \qquad x \in \mathbb P^1(\bar k),\ y \in \mathbb G_a(\bar k) = \bar k. \]
2027	  Restricting \(x\) to \(\mathbb G_a\) shows \(f|_{\mathbb G_a} \colon \mathbb G_a \to A\) is a
2028	  homomorphism of group objects (it sends \(0 \mapsto 0_A\) and respects addition).

2033	  separated, \(f\) is constant on all of \(\mathbb P^1\), with value \(f(0) = 0_A\) (i.e.\ the original

2077	  \emph{\(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut}: it proves constancy \emph{in one shot} from the proven
2078	  additive decomposition (\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}) and a scaling fixed point,
2079	  without the intermediate ``\(f|_{\mathbb G_a}\) additive'' step and \emph{without} the
2080	  \(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0\) triviality lemma. The theorem of the cube is \emph{not} used
2081	  (\cref{rmk:cube_not_needed}), and the map \(f\) is already a morphism out of the complete
2082	  nonsingular \(\mathbb P^1\), so Milne's Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) is
2083	  \emph{not} invoked either.

2085	  \mathbb P^1\( (one of the two fixed points of \)\sigma_\times\(). Translating in the group \)A$ by
2086	  \(-f(0)\), we may assume \(f(0) = 0_A\); un-translating at the end restores the original value.
2087	  \textbf{The scaling shortcut.} Form the \emph{total} morphism
2088	  \[ h := \sigma_\times \fatsemi f \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to A, \qquad
2089	     \text{``}h(x, \lambda) = f(\lambda x)\text{''}, \]
2090	  using the multiplicative scaling action \(\sigma_\times\) of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1} (a genuine
2091	  total morphism --- no rational-map extension needed). Apply the additive decomposition
2092	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} (Milne Cor~1.5) --- whose Lean signature requires only the
2093	  \emph{first} factor complete --- with \(V = \mathbb P^1\) (proper), \(W = \mathbb G_m\), and base
2094	  points \(0 \in \mathbb P^1\), \(1 \in \mathbb G_m\); here \(h(0, 1) = f(1 \cdot 0) = f(0) = 0_A\), as

2096	  \(V\)-axis restriction \(x \mapsto h(x, 1) = f(1 \cdot x) = f(x)\) (so \(f_0 = f\)) and \(g\) the
2097	  \(W\)-axis restriction \(\lambda \mapsto h(0, \lambda) = f(\lambda \cdot 0) = f(0) = 0_A\).

2177	\section{The headline: rigidity for a pointed genus-\(0\) curve}
2178	\begin{theorem}
2179	\leanok
2180	[Rigidity: a pointed morphism from a genus-\(0\) curve to an abelian variety is constant]
2181	  \label{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
2182	  
2183	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme}
2184	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field (\emph{arbitrary characteristic}; no
2185	  \(\mathtt{CharZero}\) hypothesis). Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
2186	  over \(\bar k\) with \(\genus(C) = 0\), and let \(A\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
2187	  group scheme over \(\bar k\) (an abelian variety) with identity \(\eta_A\). Then for every
2188	  morphism \(f \colon C \to A\) such that \(p \fatsemi f = \eta_A\) for some \(\bar k\)-point
2189	  \(p \colon \mathbf 1 \to C\), the morphism \(f\) equals the constant morphism at the identity,
2190	  \(f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A\).
2191	  This is the project's committed characteristic-free statement that the genus-\(0\)
2192	  Albanese-witness \(\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}\) consumes. Its signature mirrors
2193	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar}\) of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}
2194	  \emph{verbatim except} that the \([\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]\) instance is dropped: same
2195	  curve typeclasses (\(\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 1\), \(\mathtt{IsProper}\),
2196	  \(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\) on \(C\)), same abelian-variety typeclasses
2197	  (\(\mathtt{GrpObj}\), \(\mathtt{IsProper}\), \(\mathtt{Smooth}\), \(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\)
2198	  on \(A\)), and the same pointed-morphism conclusion shape $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi
2199	  \eta[A]$.
2200	\end{theorem}
2201	\begin{proof}
2202	  By \cref{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1} the genus-\(0\) curve \(C\) over the algebraically closed
2203	  field \(\bar k\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) via some isomorphism
2204	  \(\varphi \colon C \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\). The composite
2205	  \(f \circ \varphi^{-1} \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) is then a morphism from
2206	  \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) to the abelian variety \(A\), hence constant by
2207	  \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}: its image is a single \(\bar k\)-point \(a_0 \in A\).
2208	  Therefore \(f\) itself is constant with value \(a_0\). The pointed hypothesis
2209	  \(p \fatsemi f = \eta_A\) evaluates this constant at the \(\bar k\)-point \(p\) and pins it to the
2210	  identity, \(a_0 = \eta_A\). Consequently \(f\) factors through the terminal object as the
2211	  constant morphism at the identity, \(f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A\).
2212	  No differential (\(df = 0\)) input, no \(H^0(C, \Omega_C) = 0\) / Serre-duality decomposition,
2213	  and no Picard representability is used; the argument is valid in arbitrary characteristic.
2214	  Once formalised it lets the \([\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]\) hypothesis carried by
2215	  \(\mathtt{rigidity\_over\_kbar}\) be dropped, and (after the \(\bar k \to k\) descent step hosted
2216	  in \cref{chap:Jacobian}, not here) feeds \(\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}.\mathtt{key}\).
2217	  % NOTE (iter-166 review): proof body landed iter-166 via clean iso-transport
2218	  % (`obtain ⟨φ⟩ := genusZero_curve_iso_P1 _hgenus; set g := φ.inv ≫ f; obtain ⟨a₀, hga₀⟩ :=
2219	  % morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const g; pin a₀ = η[A] via `toUnit_unique` basepoint transport;
2220	  % back-transport via `φ.hom_inv_id`'). `lean_verify` axioms $\{\mathtt{propext},
2221	  % \mathtt{sorryAx}, \mathtt{Classical.choice}, \mathtt{Quot.sound}\}\( — \)\mathtt{sorryAx}$
2222	  % propagates honestly through (a) `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (RR-bridge sub-build, deferred to
2223	  % iter-167+) and (b) `morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const`'s 5 helper sorries. Lifts to axiom-clean
2224	  % once both upstreams discharge; checker confirmed no laundering.
2225	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Auslander--Buchsbaum}
2	\label{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.b sub-row of Route A

7	% Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and the corollary ``regular local ring

13	% target Lean file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean}
14	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
15	% creating it. Note that Mathlib already houses parts of the depth /
16	% projective-dimension API
17	% (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Depth.*},
18	% \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.RegularSequence},
19	% \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.ProjectiveResolution}); a Plan-Agent audit
20	% at the pinned commit (\texttt{b80f227}) should confirm what is present
21	% before the prover writes the file, since a fully Mathlib-backed
22	% Auslander--Buchsbaum would collapse this chapter to a re-export.

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface \(S\) over a field --- such as \(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1\)
28	or the product \(C \times C\) of a smooth projective curve \(C\) with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension \(2\). To extend a rational map
30	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from \(S\) to an abelian variety across a codimension-\(2\) closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} \(2\), hence a rational function with no codimension-\(1\) poles on a neighbourhood of \(x\)
33	extends across \(x\). The depth \(\geq 2\) inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring \(R\) one has \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\),
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension \(d\) admits an \(R\)-regular sequence of length \(d\) generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\), i.e.\ \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(Corollary~REF below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its \(\text{Ext}\) reformulation
47	    (Definition~REF and Lemma~REF),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (Definition~REF),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma
51	    (Lemma~REF),
52	  \item the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula proper
53	    (Theorem~REF), and
54	  \item the corollary regular \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay
55	    (Corollary~REF).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	A short closing paragraph (Section~REF) explains how A.4.a
58	consumes Corollary~REF to push a codim-1 extension across a
59	codim-2 point.
60	
61	\section{Depth of a module over a local ring}
62	\label{sec:ab_depth}
63	
64	\begin{definition}
65	
66	\leanok
67	  [Depth of a module]
68	  \label{def:depth}
69	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth}
70	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth)
71	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17748-L17759).
72	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
73	  % "Let \(R\) be a ring, and \(I \subset R\) an ideal. Let \(M\) be a finite \(R\)-module.
74	  %  The {\it \(I\)-depth} of \(M\), denoted \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is defined as follows:
75	  %  \begin{enumerate}
76	  %  \item if \(IM \not = M\), then \(\text{depth}_I(M)\) is the supremum in
77	  %  \(\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences in \(I\),
78	  %  \item if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\).
79	  %  \end{enumerate}
80	  %  If \((R, \mathfrak m)\) is local we call \(\text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M)\) simply
81	  %  the {\it depth} of \(M\)."
82	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth).}
83	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(I \subseteq R\) be an ideal, and let \(M\) be a finitely
84	  generated \(R\)-module. The \emph{\(I\)-depth} of \(M\), written \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is the
85	  supremum in \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences contained
86	  in \(I\), provided \(IM \neq M\); if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\). When
87	  \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is a Noetherian local ring we abbreviate
88	  \(\text{depth}(M) := \text{depth}_{\mathfrak{m}}(M)\) and call this simply the
89	  \emph{depth} of \(M\). The depth of the ring \(R\) itself is \(\text{depth}(R)\), the depth
90	  of \(R\) as a module over itself.
91	\end{definition}
92	
93	Recall that a sequence \(f_1, \dots, f_r \in R\) is called \emph{\(M\)-regular} if
94	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_r)M \neq 0\) and for each \(i\), the element \(f_i\) is a non-zero-divisor on
95	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_{i-1})M\). The empty sequence is regular on \(M\) iff \(M \neq 0\) (it is
96	\emph{not} regular on the zero module, but the convention \(\text{depth}(0) = \infty\)
97	above is the usual one).
98	
99	The two practical computations of depth are the regular-sequence definition (used to
100	establish a \emph{lower} bound) and the \(\text{Ext}\) characterisation (used to establish an
101	\emph{upper} bound and to prove the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequality).
102	
103	\begin{lemma}
104	
105	\leanok
106	  [Depth via \(\text{Ext}\)]
107	  \label{lem:depth_via_ext}
108	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index}
109	  
110	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext)
111	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17845-L17885).
112	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
113	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\).
114	  %  Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module. Then \(\text{depth}(M)\)
115	  %  is equal to the smallest integer \(i\) such that
116	  %  \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\) is nonzero."
117	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext).}
118	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring with residue field
119	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak{m}\) and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely generated \(R\)-module. Then
120	  \[
121	    \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \inf\bigl\{\, i \in \mathbb{N} \;\bigm|\; \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \,\bigr\}.
122	  \]
123	\end{lemma}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
126	% "Let \(\delta(M)\) denote the depth of \(M\) and let \(i(M)\) denote
127	%  the smallest integer \(i\) such that \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\)
128	%  is nonzero. We will see in a moment that \(i(M) < \infty\).
129	%  By Lemma REF we have
130	%  \(\delta(M) = 0\) if and only if \(i(M) = 0\), because
131	%  \(\mathfrak m \in \text{Ass}(M)\) exactly means
132	%  that \(i(M) = 0\). Hence if \(\delta(M)\) or \(i(M)\) is \(> 0\), then we may
133	%  choose \(x \in \mathfrak m\) such that (a) \(x\) is a nonzerodivisor
134	%  on \(M\), and (b) \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta(M) - 1\).
135	%  Consider the long exact sequence
136	%  of Ext-groups associated to the short exact sequence
137	%  \(0 \to M \to M \to M/xM \to 0\) by Lemma REF:
138	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
139	%  Since \(x \in \mathfrak m\) all the maps $\Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)
140	%  \to \Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)$ are zero, see
141	%  Lemma REF.
142	%  Thus it is clear that \(i(M/xM) = i(M) - 1\). Induction on
143	%  \(\delta(M)\) finishes the proof."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	\leanok
148	  Write \(\delta := \text{depth}(M)\) and \(i := \inf\{ j : \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \}\).
149	  The base case \(\delta = 0\) is exactly \(\text{Hom}_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0\) (some element of
150	  \(M\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\), i.e.\ \(\mathfrak{m} \in \text{Ass}(M)\), i.e.\
151	  \(\mathfrak{m}\) contains a zero-divisor on \(M\)), which is \(i = 0\). For the inductive step
152	  pick \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) a non-zero-divisor on \(M\) with
153	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta - 1\). The short exact sequence
154	  \(0 \to M \xrightarrow{x} M \to M/xM \to 0\) induces a long exact sequence of $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa,
155	  -)\(. Multiplication by \)x\( annihilates each \)\text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)$ (because
156	  \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) and \(\kappa\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\)), so the long exact
157	  sequence breaks into short pieces giving
158	  \(\text{Ext}^{j-1}_R(\kappa, M/xM) \cong \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)\) at the relevant
159	  indices, and the smallest non-vanishing index drops by \(1\). By induction
160	  \(i(M/xM) = i - 1 = \delta - 1\), so \(i = \delta\).
161	\end{proof}
162	
163	\section{Projective dimension}
164	\label{sec:ab_pd}
165	
166	\begin{definition}
167	
168	\leanok
169	  [Projective dimension]
170	  \label{def:projective_dimension}
171	  \lean{Module.projectiveDimension}
172	  % NOTE: Mathlib pinned at b80f227 may already expose this as
173	  % `Module.projectiveDimension` via
174	  % `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.ProjectiveDimension` /
175	  % `Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.ProjectiveResolution`; the prover should re-export
176	  % rather than re-define if the API is present. This block fixes the
177	  % mathematical content the prover must match.
178	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(M\) be an \(R\)-module. The \emph{projective
179	  dimension} of \(M\), written \(\text{pd}_R(M)\), is the infimum in
180	  \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths \(n\) of projective resolutions
181	  \[
182	    0 \longrightarrow P_n \longrightarrow P_{n-1} \longrightarrow \cdots
183	    \longrightarrow P_1 \longrightarrow P_0 \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
184	  \]
185	  with each \(P_i\) a projective \(R\)-module. We say \(M\) has \emph{finite projective dimension}
186	  if \(\text{pd}_R(M) < \infty\), and \emph{infinite projective dimension} otherwise. By
187	  convention \(\text{pd}_R(0) = -\infty\) (some references); we use \(\text{pd}_R(0) = 0\) here
188	  (the empty resolution \(0 \to 0\) of length \(0\)), but the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula in
189	  Theorem~REF concerns only nonzero \(M\).
190	  In the local Noetherian setting a finitely generated \(R\)-module of finite projective
191	  dimension admits a \emph{minimal finite free resolution}: a resolution with each \(P_i\)
192	  free of finite rank and each transition map \(P_{i+1} \to P_i\) landing in \(\mathfrak{m} P_i\).
193	\end{definition}
194	
195	The two basic facts about \(\text{pd}_R(M)\) needed downstream are:
196	\begin{itemize}
197	  \item \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) iff \(M\) is projective (in the local Noetherian setting,
198	    iff \(M\) is free);
199	  \item if \(0 \to K \to P \to M \to 0\) is a short exact sequence with \(P\) projective, then
200	    \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_R(K) + 1\) provided \(K \neq 0\).
201	\end{itemize}
202	Both are standard homological algebra and are recorded under
203	\texttt{Module.projectiveDimension\_succ\_iff} or similar in Mathlib.
204	
205	\section{Depth on a short exact sequence}
206	\label{sec:ab_depth_ses}
207	
208	\begin{lemma}
209	
210	\leanok
211	  [Depth on a short exact sequence]
212	  \label{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}
213	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_of_short_exact}
214	  
215	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses)
216	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17887-L17920).
217	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
218	  % "Let \(R\) be a local Noetherian ring. Let \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\)
219	  %  be a short exact sequence of nonzero finite \(R\)-modules.
220	  %  \begin{enumerate}
221	  %  \item
222	  %  \(\text{depth}(N) \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N'), \text{depth}(N'')\}\)
223	  %  \item
224	  %  \(\text{depth}(N'') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N') - 1\}\)
225	  %  \item
226	  %  \(\text{depth}(N') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N'') + 1\}\)
227	  %  \end{enumerate}"
228	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses).}
229	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let
230	  \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of nonzero finitely generated
231	  \(R\)-modules. Then
232	  \begin{enumerate}
233	    \item \(\text{depth}(N) \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N'),\; \text{depth}(N'')\bigr)\);
234	    \item \(\text{depth}(N'') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N') - 1\bigr)\);
235	    \item \(\text{depth}(N') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N'') + 1\bigr)\).
236	  \end{enumerate}
237	\end{lemma}
238	
239	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
240	% "Use the characterization of depth using the Ext groups
241	%  \(\Ext^i(\kappa, N)\), see Lemma REF,
242	%  and use the long exact cohomology sequence
243	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
244	%  from Lemma REF."
245	\begin{proof}
246	  
247	  
248	\leanok
249	  By Lemma~REF, \(\text{depth}(N')\) (resp.\ \(\text{depth}(N)\),
250	  \(\text{depth}(N'')\)) is the smallest index at which \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\) is
251	  nonzero on \(N'\) (resp.\ \(N\), \(N''\)). Apply the long exact \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\)
252	  sequence to \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\):
253	  \[
254	    \cdots \to \text{Ext}^{i-1}_R(\kappa, N'')
255	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N')
256	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N)
257	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N'')
258	    \to \text{Ext}^{i+1}_R(\kappa, N') \to \cdots
259	  \]
260	  Each of the three inequalities is then a direct read-off of the fact that if two
261	  of the three modules in a length-3 chunk vanish then the middle vanishes (for (1)) /
262	  the boundary terms vanish (for (2),(3)). Compare with the diagonal vanishing argument in
263	  the proof of Lemma~REF.
264	\end{proof}
