43	\section{Setup and motivation}
44	\label{sec:codim1_setup}
45	
46	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) throughout. By a \emph{variety} over \(\bar k\)
47	we mean a geometrically integral, separated scheme of finite type over \(\bar k\)
48	(Milne's convention, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~v); a variety is \emph{nonsingular}
49	(or \emph{smooth}) if its local ring at every closed point is regular. A variety is
50	\emph{complete} if it is proper over \(\bar k\).
51	
52	A \emph{rational map} \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) between varieties is the equivalence
53	class of a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) defined on a dense open subset
54	\(U \subseteq X\), where two pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\), \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) are equivalent
55	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\) (Milne~\S I.3, p.~16). The map \(f\)
56	is \emph{defined at} a point \(x \in X\) if there is some representative \((U, \varphi_U)\)
57	with \(x \in U\); the set of such \(x\) is an open subset \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \subseteq X\),
58	the \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\).
59	
60	The motivating question of this chapter, following Milne~\S I.3, is:
61	\begin{quote}
62	  \emph{When does a rational map \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) extend to a regular
63	  morphism on all of \(X\)?}
64	\end{quote}
65	Without hypotheses on \(X\) or \(Y\) the question has no positive answer (Milne lists three
66	counterexamples on p.~16: the inclusion of a proper open into the ambient variety; the
67	rational map to the cuspidal cubic whose inverse fails to extend; the inverse of a
68	blow-up at a smooth point). The two structural inputs that buy unconditional extension
69	in our setting are:
70	\begin{itemize}
71	  \item \emph{Target completeness:} if \(Y\) is complete (proper over \(\bar k\)), the
72	    valuative criterion of properness forces the rational map to extend across every
73	    codim-\(1\) point of a normal source \(X\) (Milne Theorem~3.1).
74	  \item \emph{Target group structure:} if \(Y\) is a group variety, the difference map
75	    \(\Phi(x, y) := f(x) \cdot f(y)^{-1}\) on \(X \times X\) has a vanishing/pole divisor
76	    along the diagonal that controls the codimension of the indeterminacy locus
77	    (Milne Lemma~3.3).
78	\end{itemize}
79	For \(Y\) an abelian variety both conditions hold, and combining them yields Milne's
80	Theorem~3.2 (the headline of \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}). The
81	present chapter packages the two inputs separately:
82	\cref{thm:codim_one_extension} is Milne~3.1, and
83	\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} is Milne~3.3. The Weil-divisor reformulation
84	\cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} translates the codim-\(1\) extension obstruction
85	in Milne~3.1's proof into the order-at-a-prime-divisor language pinned in
86	\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, which is the form the Lean prover consumes when
87	working with the project's \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API.
88	
89	\section{The indeterminacy locus of a rational map}
90	\label{sec:indeterm_locus}
91	
92	\begin{definition}
93	
94	\leanok
95	  [Indeterminacy locus of a rational map]
96	  \label{def:indeterminacy_locus}
97	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus}
98	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S I.3, p.~16, the paragraph "Let \(V\) and \(W\)
99	  % be varieties over \(k\)\ldots" defining the domain of definition of a rational map
100	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
101	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi\) is said to be \emph{defined} at a point
102	  % \(v\) of \(V\) if \(v \in U\) for some \((U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi\). The set \(U_1\) of
103	  % \(v\) at which \(\varphi\) is defined is open, and there is a regular map
104	  % \(\varphi_1 \colon U_1 \to W\) such that \((U_1, \varphi_1) \in \varphi\) --- clearly,
105	  % \(U_1 = \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi} U\) and we can define \(\varphi_1\) to be
106	  % the regular map such that \(\varphi_1 | U = \varphi_U\) for all $(U, \varphi_U) \in
107	  % \varphi$."
108	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S I.3, p.~16.}
109	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
110	  The \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\) is the open set
111	  \[
112	    \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;:=\; \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in f} U \;\subseteq\; X,
113	  \]
114	  i.e.\ the union of the dense opens of all representatives of \(f\). The
115	  \emph{indeterminacy locus} of \(f\) is the closed complement
116	  \[
117	    Z(f) \;:=\; X \smallsetminus \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;\subseteq\; X.
118	  \]
119	  The map \(f\) is regular (i.e.\ defined on all of \(X\)) if and only if \(Z(f) = \emptyset\).
120	
121	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
122	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus} is a method on the
123	  Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure pinned at
124	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}; it returns a
125	  \texttt{Set X} (the underlying carrier of the closed complement of
126	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.domain}, which is already exposed by Mathlib). The
127	  closedness of \(Z(f)\) is a separate \texttt{IsClosed} witness, packaged either as a
128	  field of \texttt{indeterminacyLocus} or as a standalone lemma
129	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.isClosed\_indeterminacyLocus}, whichever is cleaner with
130	  Mathlib's existing API at commit \texttt{b80f227}.
131	\end{definition}
132	
133	\begin{definition}
134	
135	\leanok
136	  [Codimension-1-indeterminacy-free rational maps]
137	  \label{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}
138	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree}
139	  
140	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 --- the conclusion
141	  % "an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)"
142	  % is the standing predicate we abstract (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf,
143	  % PDF page 22).
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow W\) from a normal
145	  % variety \(V\) to a complete variety \(W\) is defined on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose
146	  % complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)."
147	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 (the
148	  conclusion).}
149	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
150	  We say \(f\) is \emph{codim-1-indeterminacy-free} (or has \emph{codimension-\(\geq 2\)
151	  indeterminacy}) if every irreducible component of the closed subset \(Z(f) \subseteq X\)
152	  has codimension \(\geq 2\) in \(X\). Equivalently, no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in
153	  \(Z(f)\); equivalently, every point \(\eta \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\)
154	  lies in \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\).
155	
156	  \paragraph{Why the abstraction.} Milne's Theorem~3.1 (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}
157	  below) and Lemma~3.3 (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} below) both produce
158	  conclusions about the codimension of \(Z(f)\). Phrasing the codim-\(\geq 2\) condition
159	  as a named predicate keeps the cited statements concise and lets the consumer
160	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} read off the combination
161	  (\(Z(f)\) has codim \(\geq 2\) from \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} \emph{and} \(Z(f)\) is
162	  empty or pure codim 1 from \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} \(\Rightarrow\) \(Z(f)\)
163	  is empty) as a single line.
164	
165	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
166	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree} is a \texttt{Prop}-valued
167	  predicate, encoded via Mathlib's \texttt{Order.coheight} on the specialisation
168	  preorder of \texttt{X.carrier} as in
169	  \cref{def:prime_divisor}: \(f\) is codim-1-indeterminacy-free if and only if for every
170	  \(\eta \in X\) with \(\texttt{Order.coheight\ }\eta = 1\), $\eta \in
171	  \texttt{f.domain}\(. The encoding matches the standing \)\mathrm{Order.coheight}$
172	  convention pinned in \cref{def:prime_divisor}, so prime divisors of \(X\) and the
173	  hypothesis ``no codim-\(1\) indeterminacy'' use the same Mathlib idiom.
174	\end{definition}
175	
176	\section{Smoothness yields a DVR at every codim-1 point}
177	\label{sec:smooth_dvr}
178	
179	The codim-\(1\) extension argument in Milne's proof of Theorem~3.1 hinges on the local
180	ring at the generic point of a prime divisor being a discrete valuation ring (so that
181	the valuative criterion of properness applies). On a normal variety this is the
182	defining property of regularity in codimension one; on a smooth variety it is automatic.
183	
184	The smooth-implies-regular-local-stalk implication itself is the Jacobian-criterion
185	direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}; we package it as a separate sub-lemma so that
186	\cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} below reads cleanly as ``regular local of dimension
187	\(1\) is a DVR'', with the regularity prerequisite supplied by the sub-lemma.
188	
189	\begin{lemma}
190	  [Smooth over \(\bar k\) \(\Rightarrow\) regular local stalk (Stacks 00TT)]
191	  \label{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}
192	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00TT --- Jacobian criterion
193	  % (smooth-implies-regular direction) (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex,
194	  % L38593--38611; tag verified against the Stacks website tag/00TT page,
195	  % "Lemma 10.140.3" in section 10.140 "Smooth algebras over fields").
196	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $k$ be any field. Let $S$ be a finite type $k$-algebra.
197	  % Let $X = \Spec(S)$. Let $\mathfrak q \subset S$ be a prime corresponding to
198	  % $x \in X$. The following are equivalent:
199	  % \begin{enumerate}
200	  % \item The $k$-algebra $S$ is smooth at $\mathfrak q$ over $k$.
201	  % \item We have
202	  % $\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \Omega_{S/k} \otimes_S \kappa(\mathfrak q)
203	  % \leq \dim_x X$.
204	  % \item We have
205	  % $\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \Omega_{S/k} \otimes_S \kappa(\mathfrak q)
206	  % = \dim_x X$.
207	  % \end{enumerate}
208	  % Moreover, in this case the local ring $S_{\mathfrak q}$ is regular."
209	  % NOTE (iter-186 plan): the iter-185 writer left an empty
210	  % uses-annotation here intentionally to signal "no project-side label
211	  % prerequisites" — the Stacks 00TT prerequisites (smooth ring map,
212	  % regular local ring) live in Mathlib as typeclasses
213	  % (Algebra.Smooth, IsRegularLocalRing) but are not packaged as
214	  % blueprint-labeled definitions in this project; the project's
215	  % def:codim_one_indeterminacy and Auslander--Buchsbaum-side
216	  % cor:regular_cohen_macaulay are adjacent context, not strict
217	  % prerequisites. blueprint-doctor (iter-185) flagged the empty
218	  % argument as a CRITICAL plastex/depgraph build-breaker and removed
219	  % it; the iter-186 plan agent further reworded this NOTE because the
220	  % literal backtick-quoted token was still tripping the doctor regex.
221	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00TT.}
222	  Let \(X\) be a variety over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) (geometrically
223	  integral, separated, locally of finite type) and assume the structure morphism
224	  \(X \to \Spec(\bar k)\) is \emph{smooth}. Then for every point \(z \in X\) the
225	  stalk \(\mathcal O_{X, z}\) is a regular local ring.
226	
227	  The hypothesis \([IsAlgClosed\,\bar k]\) is load-bearing: tag 00TT is stated
228	  over an arbitrary field, but the variant Mathlib will be able to discharge
229	  axiom-clean at commit \texttt{b80f227} routes through residue fields that are
230	  \(\bar k\)-rational (every closed point of \(X\) has residue field \(\bar k\) under
231	  algebraic closure, and the chain of localisations from a closed point reaches
232	  every \(z \in X\) by the standard chain-of-primes argument). Without algebraic
233	  closure one needs the geometric-regularity refinement of tag 00TT (Stacks tag
234	  \texttt{056T} in the project's \texttt{stacks-varieties.md} card) and an extra
235	  base-change-to-\(\bar k\) step.
236	\end{lemma}
237	
238	\begin{proof}
239	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00TT.} The cited lemma packages the
240	  Jacobian-criterion equivalence and concludes ``in this case the local ring
241	  \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) is regular''. Translated to the scheme level: a smooth
242	  finite-type morphism \(X \to \Spec(\bar k)\) restricted to an affine chart
243	  \(\Spec(S) \subseteq X\) gives an affine algebra \(\bar k \to S\) that is
244	  smooth at every prime \(\mathfrak q \subset S\); tag 00TT then yields
245	  regularity of the localisation \(S_{\mathfrak q} = \mathcal O_{X, z}\) at every
246	  point \(z \in X\) lying over \(\mathfrak q\).
247	
248	  Mathlib hooks the prover will assemble for the formalised version of this
249	  block at commit \texttt{b80f227}: (i) \texttt{Algebra.FormallySmooth} and the
250	  smooth-locus-of-a-scheme typeclass instance threading
251	  \texttt{[Smooth\,X.hom]} down to the affine algebra
252	  \(\bar k \to \mathcal O_X(U)\) on each open affine \(U \subseteq X\); (ii) the
253	  Jacobian-criterion refinement of \texttt{Module.FormallyEtale} /
254	  \texttt{Algebra.Smooth} via the cotangent complex \texttt{H\^{}1\,LSm} (or
255	  equivalent), as encoded in Mathlib's \texttt{Algebra.Smooth.iff} family; (iii)
256	  the closed-point selection that, given a non-closed \(z\), picks a closed
257	  specialisation \(x \rightsquigarrow z\) whose stalk localises to
258	  \(\mathcal O_{X, z}\) (Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.localRing\_at\_closed\_point}
259	  family); (iv) preservation of regularity under localisation of a regular
260	  local ring at a prime (Mathlib
261	  \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing.localization\_isRegularLocalRing}, or the same
262	  fact phrased via Stacks tag \texttt{00OF}). The composition of (i)--(iv) is
263	  the formalised content of the smooth-implies-regular implication; the only
264	  Mathlib gap at \texttt{b80f227} is in step (ii)--(iii), specifically the
265	  Jacobian-criterion scheme-level packaging that promotes
266	  \texttt{[Smooth\,X.hom]\,[IsAlgClosed\,\bar k]} to
267	  \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing\,(X.left.presheaf.stalk\,z)}.
268	
269	  As a backup citation, Matsumura, \emph{Commutative Ring Theory}, Chapter~19
270	  (``Regular Rings'') develops the regular-local-ring theory needed for
271	  step~(iv); see \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} for the depth-/CM-side
272	  references the project already maintains.
273	\end{proof}

275	\begin{lemma}
276	
277	\leanok
278	  [Smooth \(\Rightarrow\) DVR at every codim-\(1\) point]
279	  \label{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}
280	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.localRing_dvr_of_codim_one}
281	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (the definition ``regular in codimension one'',
282	  % together with the immediately preceding remark that on a nonsingular variety every
283	  % local ring is regular) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
284	  % PDF page 147).
285	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textbf{Definition.} We say a scheme \(X\) is \emph{regular in
286	  % codimension one} (or sometimes \emph{nonsingular in codimension one}) if every
287	  % local ring \(\mathcal O_x\) of \(X\) of dimension one is regular."
288	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "The most important examples of such schemes are nonsingular
289	  % varieties over a field (I, \S5) and noetherian normal schemes. On a nonsingular
290	  % variety the local ring of every closed point is regular (I, 5.1), hence all the
291	  % local rings are regular, since they are localizations of the local rings of
292	  % closed points."
293	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its generic
294	  % point. Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{\eta, X}\) of \(X\) at \(\eta\) is a discrete
295	  % valuation ring with quotient field \(K\), the function field of \(X\)."
296	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of regular in codimension one;
297	  the DVR consequence at a codim-1 point).}
298	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\) and let \(\eta \in X\) be a point with
299	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\). Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a
300	  discrete valuation ring with fraction field the function field \(K(X)\) of \(X\).
301	\end{lemma}
302	
303	\begin{proof}
304	
305	\leanok
306	  % NOTE (iter-185 writer): the `\leanok` marker on this proof block reflects
307	  % the Krull-dim half (Tier-1 axiom-clean closure achieved iter-184 via
308	  % `Scheme.ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight` from the iter-183
309	  % `Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean`, see
310	  % `\cref{chap:Albanese_CoheightBridge}`). The `IsRegularLocalRing` half is
311	  % tracked separately as the Stacks 00TT gap and is now packaged in
312	  % `\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}` above; that block carries no
313	  % `\leanok` because the Mathlib hooks (i)--(iv) in its proof sketch are not
314	  % all axiom-clean at commit `b80f227`. The composition (00TT regularity +
315	  % iter-183 Krull-dim bridge) inside the private Lean helper
316	  % `smooth_codim_one_maximalIdeal_isPrincipal_and_ne_bot`
317	  % (`AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean:223`) keeps a single
318	  % typed `sorry` on the regularity half pending an iter-185+ prover round.
319	  By \cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring} (Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}, the
320	  Jacobian-criterion direction), the stalk \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is regular for
321	  every point \(x \in X\) --- in particular at every closed point (the case
322	  Hartshorne~I.5.1 states classically). Localising a regular local ring at a
323	  prime ideal yields a regular local ring; in particular, for \(\eta \in X\) with
324	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\), the local ring
325	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is regular of Krull dimension \(1\). A regular local ring of Krull dimension \(1\) is exactly a
326	  discrete valuation ring (Atiyah--Macdonald, Proposition 9.2; or Stacks tag
327	  \texttt{00PD}). Its fraction field is the function field \(K(X)\) of \(X\) by
328	  irreducibility of \(X\) (the unique generic point of \(X\) lies above \(\eta\), and the
329	  stalk at the generic point of an integral scheme is the function field).
330	
331	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
332	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.localRing\_dvr\_of\_codim\_one} is the
333	  instance/lemma stating that \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a Mathlib
334	  \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing} under the typeclass set
335	  \texttt{[Smooth\,(\dots)\,X.hom]\ [IsIntegral X.left]} and the hypothesis
336	  \texttt{Order.coheight\ \(\eta\) = 1}. The fraction-field identification with
337	  \texttt{X.functionField} is a downstream named instance
338	  \texttt{Scheme.fractionRing\_localRing\_eq\_functionField} (or whatever Mathlib name
339	  the prover chooses at commit \texttt{b80f227}). The regular-local-ring-of-dimension-1
340	  \(\Leftrightarrow\) DVR equivalence is shipped in Mathlib as
341	  \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing.isDiscreteValuationRing\_of\_dim\_one} (or analogous);
342	  the prover re-exports it rather than re-proving.
343	\end{proof}

345	\section{Milne Theorem 3.1: codimension-\(\geq 2\) extension}
346	\label{sec:milne_thm31}
347	
348	The first input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is the following: a rational map from a normal
349	variety to a \emph{complete} variety automatically extends across every codim-\(1\)
350	point.
351	
352	\begin{theorem}
353	
354	\leanok
355	  [Milne Theorem~3.1: codim-\(\geq 2\) extension to a complete target]
356	  \label{thm:codim_one_extension}
357	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth}
358	  
359	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16
360	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
361	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Theorem 3.1.} \emph{A rational map $\varphi \colon V
362	  % \dashrightarrow W\( from a normal variety \)V\( to a complete variety \)W$ is defined
363	  % on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension
364	  % \(\geq 2\).}"
365	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16.}
366	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a complete variety over
367	  \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map. Then the
368	  indeterminacy locus \(Z(f)\) (\cref{def:indeterminacy_locus}) has codimension
369	  \(\geq 2\) in \(X\); equivalently, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
370	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}).
371	
372	  Specialising further: if in addition \(f\) \emph{is} codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
373	  ``for free'' (i.e.\ no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in \(Z(f)\)), then
374	  \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) = X\) and \(f\) extends to a regular morphism $\widetilde f \colon X
375	  \to Y\(. The extension is unique because \)X\( is reduced and \)\mathrm{Dom}(f)$ is dense
376	  in \(X\).
377	\end{theorem}
378	
379	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\textsc{Proof.} Assume first that \(V\) is a curve. Thus we are
380	% given a nonsingular curve \(V\) and a regular map \(\varphi \colon U \to W\) from an
381	% open subset of \(V\) which we want to extend to \(V\). Consider the maps
382	% [diagram: \(U \hookrightarrow V\), $U \xrightarrow{u \mapsto (u, \varphi(u))} Z
383	% \subseteq V \times W\(, \)U \xrightarrow{\varphi} W$, with projections from
384	% \(V \times W\) to \(V\) and to \(W\)]
385	% Let \(U'\) be the image of \(U\) in \(V \times W\), and let \(Z\) be its closure. The image
386	% of \(Z\) in \(V\) is closed (because \(W\) is complete), and contains \(U\) (the composite
387	% \(U \to V\) is the given inclusion), and so \(Z\) maps onto \(V\). The maps $U \to U'
388	% \to U\( are isomorphisms. Therefore, \)Z \to V$ is a surjective map from a complete
389	% curve onto a nonsingular complete curve that is an isomorphism on open subsets.
390	% Such a map must be an isomorphism (complete nonsingular curves are determined by
391	% their function fields). The restriction of the projection map \(V \times W \to W\)
392	% to \(Z\ (\simeq V)\) is the extension of \(\varphi\) to \(V\) we are seeking.
393	%   The general case can be reduced to the one-dimensional case (using schemes). Let
394	% \(U\) be the largest subset on which \(\varphi\) is defined, and suppose that $V
395	% \smallsetminus U\( has codimension 1. Then there is a prime divisor \)Z\( in \)V
396	% \smallsetminus U\(. Because \)V$ is normal, its associated local ring is a discrete
397	% valuation ring \(\mathcal O_Z\) with field of fractions \(k(V)\). The map \(\varphi\)
398	% defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(k(V)) \to W\), which the
399	% valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne 1977, II 4.7) shows extends to a
400	% morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_Z) \to W\). This implies that \(\varphi\)
401	% has a representative defined on an open subset that meets \(Z\) in a nonempty set,
402	% which is a contradiction."
403	\begin{proof}
404	  
405	  Following Milne's two-step proof.
406	
407	  \emph{Step 1: rule out codim-\(1\) components.} Suppose for contradiction that \(Z(f)\)
408	  contains a prime divisor \(W \subseteq X\) (i.e.\ a codim-\(1\) irreducible component).
409	  Let \(\eta \in W\) be its generic point, so \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\). By
410	  \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
411	  valuation ring with fraction field the function field \(K(X)\). Any representative
412	  \((U, \varphi_U) \in f\) has \(U \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f) \neq \emptyset\) and so determines
413	  a \(\bar k\)-algebra map from the function field \(K(Y)\) of \(Y\) to \(K(X)\): explicitly,
414	  \(\varphi_U\) defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X)) \to Y\) (since
415	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X))\) is the generic point of \(X\)). By the valuative criterion
416	  of properness applied to the complete variety \(Y\)
417	  (\emph{cf.}\ Hartshorne~II.4.7, cited verbatim in Milne~3.1's proof), this morphism
418	  extends to a morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}) \to Y\). Standard
419	  algebraic-geometry spreading then exhibits an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(\eta\) in
420	  \(X\) on which \(f\) has a regular representative; in particular $\eta \in
421	  \mathrm{Dom}(f)\(, contradicting \)\eta \in W \subseteq Z(f)\(. Hence no codim-\)1$
422	  component of \(X\) lies in \(Z(f)\), i.e.\ \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\).
423	
424	  \emph{Step 2: extend across codim-\(\geq 2\) points (the "codim-1-indeterminacy-free
425	  \(\Rightarrow\) extension" half).} Suppose now that \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)
426	  but is non-empty (we want to deduce \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) under the additional input
427	  that ``\(f\) is already codim-1-indeterminacy-free, by hypothesis''). Pick a closed
428	  point \(x \in Z(f)\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} \geq 2\). By
429	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} applied to the regular local ring
430	  \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\), we have
431	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = \dim(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2\). The sheaf
432	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on the punctured neighbourhood \(V \smallsetminus \{x\}\)
433	  (any small affine neighbourhood \(V \ni x\)) has \(H^0_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\)
434	  and \(H^1_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\) (the depth-\(\geq 2\) characterisation of
435	  local-cohomology vanishing, Stacks tag \texttt{0AVF}; cf.\ Hartshorne~III.3.5 for
436	  the surface case). Pull back the coordinates of any affine chart of \(Y\) around the
437	  closure \(\overline{f(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \cap V)}\) along \(f\); the resulting sections of
438	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on \(V \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) extend uniquely to sections on \(V\),
439	  yielding a regular representative of \(f\) on \(V\). Hence \(x \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\),
440	  contradicting \(x \in Z(f)\). Therefore \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) and \(f\) extends to a
441	  regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to Y\).
442	
443	  Uniqueness of \(\widetilde f\) is the standard reduced-and-separated agreement
444	  principle: two morphisms \(X \to Y\) that agree on the dense open \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\)
445	  agree on all of \(X\).
446	\end{proof}
447	
448	\begin{remark}
449	  \label{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}
450	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} Step~1 of the proof uses only normality
451	  + the valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne~II.4.7) and is characteristic-free.
452	  Step~2 uses the depth-\(\geq 2\) extension property at a codim-\(\geq 2\) regular
453	  point, which is exactly what \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of
454	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies (regular local of dimension \(d\)
455	  \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay of depth \(d\)). For our application the source \(X\) is a
456	  smooth variety, so the regular-local-ring hypothesis is automatic; the Cohen--Macaulay
457	  consequence is what feeds the local-cohomology vanishing.
458	
459	  Concretely, when \(X\) is a smooth surface (e.g.\ \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) or
460	  \(C \times C\) for a smooth projective curve \(C\)), the codim-\(\geq 2\) closed points of
461	  \(X\) are exactly the closed points of \(X\) (because \(\dim X = 2\)), and at each such
462	  point \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is regular of dimension \(2\), hence Cohen--Macaulay of
463	  depth \(2\). The smooth-surface case is the one A.4.c will invoke when extending the
464	  symmetric-product rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) along the Abel--Jacobi
465	  birational equivalence to \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\).
466	\end{remark}

468	\section{Milne Lemma 3.3: indeterminacy locus of a map to a group variety is codim 1}
469	\label{sec:milne_lem33}
470	
471	The second input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is structural: when the target \(Y\) is a group
472	variety, the indeterminacy locus is either empty or of \emph{pure} codimension \(1\).
473	Combined with \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s ``codimension \(\geq 2\)'' conclusion,
474	this forces the locus to be empty.
475	
476	\begin{lemma}
477	
478	\leanok
479	  [Milne Lemma~3.3: pure-codim-\(1\) structure of the indeterminacy locus into a group
480	  variety]
481	  \label{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}
482	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacy_pure_codim_one_into_grpScheme}
483	  
484	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Lemma~3.3, \S I.3, p.~17
485	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
486	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Lemma 3.3.} \emph{Let \(\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow G\)
487	  % be a rational map from a nonsingular variety to a group variety. Then either
488	  % \(\varphi\) is defined on all of \(V\) or the points where it is not defined form a
489	  % closed subset of pure codimension 1 in \(V\) (i.e., a finite union of prime
490	  % divisors).}"
491	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Lemma~3.3, \S I.3, p.~17.}
492	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(G\) be a group variety over
493	  \(\bar k\) (a smooth group scheme of finite type), and let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow
494	  G\( be a rational map. Then either \)f\( is defined on all of \)X$, or the indeterminacy
495	  locus \(Z(f) \subseteq X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) (i.e.\ a finite
496	  union of prime divisors).
497	
498	  Equivalently: it is \emph{never} the case that \(f\) has a non-empty indeterminacy
499	  locus all of whose irreducible components have codimension \(\geq 2\).
500	\end{lemma}
501	
502	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\textsc{Proof.} Define a rational map
503	%   \(\Phi \colon V \times V \dashrightarrow G\), $\;(x, y) \mapsto \varphi(x) \cdot
504	%   \varphi(y)^{-1}$.
505	% More precisely, if \((U, \varphi_U)\) represents \(\varphi\), then \(\Phi\) is the rational
506	% map represented by
507	%   $U \times U \xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U} G \times G
508	%   \xrightarrow{\mathrm{id} \times \mathrm{inv}} G
509	%   \xrightarrow{m} G$.
510	% Clearly \(\Phi\) is defined at a diagonal point \((x, x)\) if \(\varphi\) is defined at
511	% \(x\), and then \(\Phi(x, x) = e\). Conversely, if \(\Phi\) is defined at \((x, x)\), then
512	% it is defined on an open neighbourhood of \((x, x)\); in particular, there will be an
513	% open subset \(U\) of \(V\) such that \(\Phi\) is defined on \(\{x\} \times U\). After
514	% possible replacing \(U\) by a smaller open subset (not necessarily containing \(x\)),
515	% \(\varphi\) will be defined on \(U\). For \(u \in U\), the formula
516	%   \(\varphi(x) = \Phi(x, u) \cdot \varphi(u)\)
517	% defines \(\varphi\) at \(x\). Thus \(\varphi\) is defined at \(x\) if and only if \(\Phi\) is
518	% defined at \((x, x)\). The rational map \(\Phi\) defines a map
519	%   \(\Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \to k(V \times V)\).
520	% Since \(\Phi\) sends \((x, x)\) to \(e\) if it is defined there, it follows that \(\Phi\)
521	% is defined at \((x, x)\) if and only if
522	%   \(\operatorname{Im}(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subset \mathcal O_{V \times V, (x, x)}\).
523	% Now \(V \times V\) is nonsingular, and so we have a good theory of divisors (AG,
524	% Chapter 12). For a nonzero rational function \(f\) on \(V \times V\), write
525	%   \(\operatorname{div}(f) = \operatorname{div}(f)_0 - \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\),
526	% with \(\operatorname{div}(f)_0\) and \(\operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\) effective divisors
527	% --- note that \(\operatorname{div}(f)_\infty = \operatorname{div}(f^{-1})_0\). Then
528	%   $\mathcal O_{V \times V, (x, x)} = \{f \in k(V \times V) \mid
529	%   \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x)\} \cup \{0\}$.
530	% Suppose \(\phi\) is not defined at \(x\). Then for some $f \in \operatorname{Im}
531	% (\varphi^\ast)\(, \)(x, x) \in \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\(, and clearly \)\Phi$ is
532	% not defined at the points \((y, y) \in \Delta \cap \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\).
533	% This is a subset of pure codimension one in \(\Delta\) (AG 9.2), and when we identify
534	% it with a subset of \(V\), it is a subset of \(V\) of codimension one passing through
535	% \(x\) on which \(\varphi\) is not defined."
536	\begin{proof}
537	  
538	  Following Milne's proof of Lemma~3.3. The argument has four sub-steps. Throughout we
539	  fix a representative \((U, \varphi_U)\) of \(f\).
540	
541	  \emph{Sub-step 1: the difference rational map.} Define
542	  \[
543	    \Phi \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow G,
544	    \qquad
545	    \Phi(x, y) \;:=\; \varphi(x) \cdot \varphi(y)^{-1},
546	  \]
547	  via the composition
548	  \[
549	    U \times U
550	    \;\xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U}\; G \times G
551	    \;\xrightarrow{\mathrm{id} \times \mathrm{inv}}\; G \times G
552	    \;\xrightarrow{m}\; G,
553	  \]
554	  where \(\mathrm{inv} \colon G \to G\) is the group-variety inverse and
555	  \(m \colon G \times G \to G\) is the group law. The map \(\Phi\) is rational by
556	  composition: its domain is at least \(U \times U \subseteq X \times X\), which is dense
557	  because \(U\) is dense in \(X\).
558	
559	  \emph{Sub-step 2: \(\Phi\)-defined at \((x, x)\) \(\Leftrightarrow\) \(\varphi\)-defined at
560	  \(x\).} If \(\varphi\) is defined at \(x\), the explicit formula \(\Phi(x, x) = e\) (the
561	  identity of \(G\)) shows \(\Phi\) is defined at \((x, x)\). Conversely, if \(\Phi\) is
562	  defined at \((x, x)\), openness of \(\mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)\) in \(X \times X\) produces an
563	  open set \(W \ni (x, x)\) on which \(\Phi\) is defined; by projection (taking the slice
564	  \(\{x\} \times W' \subseteq W\) for some open \(W' \subseteq X\), possibly shrinking
565	  \(W'\) so \(\varphi\) is defined on \(W'\) as well --- \(W'\) need not contain \(x\)), the
566	  identity
567	  \[
568	    \varphi(x) \;=\; \Phi(x, u) \cdot \varphi(u),
569	    \qquad u \in W',
570	  \]
571	  exhibits a regular representative of \(\varphi\) at \(x\). Hence $x \in
572	  \mathrm{Dom}(\varphi)\( if and only if \)(x, x) \in \mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)$.
573	
574	  \emph{Sub-step 3: rephrase via pullback of local rings.} The rational map \(\Phi\)
575	  pulls \(\mathcal O_{G, e}\) back to a \(\bar k\)-subalgebra of the function field
576	  \(K(X \times X)\):
577	  \[
578	    \Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \;\longrightarrow\; K(X \times X).
579	  \]
580	  Because \(\Phi\) sends \((x, x)\) to \(e\) wherever it is defined, \(\Phi\) is defined at
581	  \((x, x)\) if and only if $\Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subseteq \mathcal O_{X
582	  \times X, (x, x)}\( (i.e.\ every local function on \)G\( near \)e$ has no pole at
583	  \((x, x)\) after pullback).
584	
585	  \emph{Sub-step 4: pole locus is pure codimension \(1\).} The variety \(X \times X\) is
586	  nonsingular (smoothness is stable under products over \(\bar k\)), so the Weil-divisor
587	  apparatus of \cref{def:codim1_cycles} and the order map of
588	  \cref{def:order_at_point} apply on \(X \times X\). For any nonzero rational function
589	  \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) decompose its divisor as
590	  \[
591	    \mathrm{div}(f) \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f)_0 - \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty,
592	  \]
593	  with \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) effective and
594	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = \mathrm{div}(f^{-1})_0\) (Milne's notation, matching the
595	  project's \(\div(f) = \sum \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y\) of
596	  \cref{def:principal_divisor}: \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) collects the prime divisors with
597	  \(\ord_Y(f) > 0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) those with \(\ord_Y(f) < 0\)). The local
598	  ring at \((x, x)\) can be characterised as
599	  \[
600	    \mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}
601	    \;=\; \bigl\{\, f \in K(X \times X) \;\big|\;
602	    \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x) \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
603	  \]
604	  Suppose \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) is not in \(\mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}\);
605	  equivalently, \((x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty)\). The pole divisor
606	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) is a finite sum of prime divisors of \(X \times X\) (i.e.\ a
607	  closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X \times X\)). Its intersection with the
608	  diagonal \(\Delta_X \subseteq X \times X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\)
609	  in \(\Delta_X\) (this is Milne's reference to AG~9.2, the standard fact that a prime
610	  divisor of \(X \times X\) either contains \(\Delta_X\) or meets it in pure codimension
611	  \(1\); on a nonsingular variety this is an instance of Krull's principal-ideal theorem
612	  applied to the local equation of \(\Delta_X\) inside \(X \times X\)).
613	
614	  \emph{Conclusion.} Suppose \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow G\) is not defined on all of
615	  \(X\). Pick \(x \in Z(f)\). By Sub-steps~2--3, there is some $f_0 \in
616	  \Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e})\( with \)(x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty)$.
617	  By Sub-step~4 the intersection $\mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty) \cap
618	  \Delta_X\( is pure codimension \)1\( in \)\Delta_X\(, passing through \)(x, x)$.
619	  Identifying \(\Delta_X\) with \(X\) via the diagonal isomorphism, the corresponding
620	  closed subset of \(X\) has pure codimension \(1\) and passes through \(x\). By Sub-step~2,
621	  every point of this codim-\(1\) subset is a point where \(\Phi\) is undefined on the
622	  diagonal, hence where \(\varphi\) is undefined. Thus \(Z(\varphi)\) contains a pure
623	  codim-\(1\) closed subset passing through \(x\); since \(x \in Z(\varphi)\) was arbitrary,
624	  every irreducible component of \(Z(\varphi)\) has codimension exactly \(1\), i.e.\
625	  \(Z(\varphi)\) is of pure codimension \(1\).
626	\end{proof}
627	
628	\begin{remark}
629	  \label{rmk:milne_lem33_charfree}
630	  \textbf{Characteristic-free.} The above proof uses only: (i) the group law on \(G\)
631	  and its inverse (formal smoothness of \(G\) as a group object); (ii) the Weil-divisor
632	  apparatus on the nonsingular variety \(X \times X\) (Hartshorne~II.6, or
633	  \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor} in the project's notation); (iii) the
634	  decomposition of a divisor on a nonsingular variety into effective pieces, and
635	  Krull's principal-ideal theorem on \(X \times X\). None of these has a characteristic
636	  restriction, so \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} holds over every
637	  algebraically closed base field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states the
638	  lemma in this generality at \S I.3, p.~17.)
639	\end{remark}

641	\section{Weil-divisor obstruction to codim-1 extension}
642	\label{sec:weil_obstruction}
643	
644	The proof of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s Step~1 (codim-\(1\) ruling-out) goes
645	through the valuative criterion. In the project's Weil-divisor language
646	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}) the same obstruction has a cleaner phrasing:
647	the extension fails at a prime divisor \(W\) if and only if some affine coordinate of
648	\(f\) around the image \(\overline{f(\eta_W)}\) has a strictly negative order
649	\(\ord_W(\cdot) < 0\). This is the form the Lean prover needs when consuming
650	\cref{def:order_at_point} and \cref{def:principal_divisor}.
651	
652	\begin{theorem}
653	
654	  [Weil-divisor characterisation of the codim-1 extension obstruction]
655	  \label{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
656

680	  % valuation ring with quotient field \(K\), the function field of \(X\). We call the
681	  % corresponding discrete valuation \(v_Y\) the \emph{valuation of \(Y\)}."
682	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let \(f \in K^\ast\) be any nonzero rational function on \(X\).
683	  % Then \(v_Y(f)\) is an integer. If it is positive, we say \(f\) has a \emph{zero} along
684	  % \(Y\), of that order; if it is negative, we say \(f\) has a \emph{pole} along \(Y\), of
685	  % order \(-v_Y(f)\)."
686	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\) and the order map).}
687	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a variety over \(\bar k\),
688	  let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map, and let \(W \subseteq X\) be a
689	  prime divisor with generic point \(\eta_W \in W\) (so
690	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta_W\}} = 1\)). Let \((U, \varphi_U)\) be any representative of
691	  \(f\) with \(U \cap (X \smallsetminus W) \neq \emptyset\). The following are equivalent:
692	  \begin{enumerate}
693	    \item \(f\) is defined at \(\eta_W\) (i.e.\ \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\), equivalently
694	      \(W \not\subseteq Z(f)\)).
695	    \item For some (equivalently, every) affine open neighbourhood \(V \subseteq Y\) of
696	      the generic image of \(f\) near \(W\), every regular function $g \in
697	      \mathcal O_Y(V)\( pulls back to a rational function \)\varphi_U^\ast(g) \in K(X)$
698	      satisfying
699	      \[
700	        \ord_W\bigl(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\bigr) \;\geq\; 0.
701	      \]
702	  \end{enumerate}
703	
704	  Consequently: \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
705	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}) if and only if, for every prime divisor
706	  \(W \subseteq X\) and every affine open \(V \subseteq Y\) as above, the pullback
707	  \(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\) has \(\ord_W \geq 0\) for all \(g \in \mathcal O_Y(V)\).
708	\end{theorem}
709	
710	\begin{proof}
711	  
712	  By \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}\) is a
713	  DVR with fraction field \(K(X)\) and uniformiser determined by \(W\); by
714	  \cref{def:order_at_point} its valuation is exactly \(\ord_W\). The local ring at
715	  \(\eta_W\) is therefore characterised inside \(K(X)\) as the subring
716	  \[
717	    \mathcal O_{X, \eta_W} \;=\; \bigl\{\, h \in K(X) \;\big|\; \ord_W(h) \geq 0
718	    \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
719	  \]
720	  The rational map \(f\) is defined at \(\eta_W\) if and only if, for any affine open
721	  \(V = \operatorname{Spec} A \subseteq Y\) around the image \(\overline{f(\eta_W)}\),
722	  the pullback \(\varphi_U^\ast \colon A \to K(X)\) lands in
723	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}\): this is the standard local characterisation of where a
724	  rational map is regular at a codim-\(1\) point of a smooth variety (it is the
725	  ``regular = no pole'' criterion). By the displayed identity, the latter is
726	  equivalent to \(\ord_W(\varphi_U^\ast(g)) \geq 0\) for every \(g \in A\), which is
727	  condition (2). The equivalence (1)~\(\Leftrightarrow\)~(2) is therefore established.
728	
729	  For the consequence, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free iff no prime divisor \(W\) is
730	  contained in \(Z(f)\), which by the equivalence above is iff the \(\ord_W \geq 0\)
731	  condition holds for every prime divisor \(W\).
732	\end{proof}
733	
734	\begin{remark}
735	  \label{rmk:weil_obstruction_application}
736	  \textbf{Why this form is the prover-facing one.} In the consumer chapter
737	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} the Lean prover combines
738	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} with \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}; in
739	  isolation, the abstract codim-\(\geq 2\) statement
740	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} suffices and \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
741	  is not invoked. The Weil-divisor form is the bridge to the project's
742	  \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API, which is the form A.4.a's own internal sub-proofs
743	  use when establishing \emph{specific} extensions in genus-\(0\) classifying-map
744	  arguments and in the symmetric-product Albanese chapter
745	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}.
746	\end{remark}
747	
748	\begin{lemma}
749	
750	\leanok
751	  [Definedness at a prime divisor, repackaged as a through-the-point partial map]
752	  \label{lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle}
753	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.mem_domain_iff_exists_partialMap_through_point}
754	  Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be varieties over \(\bar k\) and let
755	  \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map. For a prime divisor
756	  \(W \subseteq X\) with generic point \(\eta_W = W.\mathrm{point}\), the
757	  following are equivalent:
758	  \begin{enumerate}
759	    \item \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) (\(f\) is defined at the generic point of
760	      \(W\)).
761	    \item There exists a partial-map representative \((U, \varphi_U)\) of \(f\) ---
762	      i.e.\ a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) on a dense open
763	      \(U \subseteq X\) whose induced rational map equals \(f\) --- whose domain
764	      \(U\) contains \(\eta_W\).
765	  \end{enumerate}
766	\end{lemma}
767	
768	\begin{proof}
769	
770	\leanok
771	  The lemma is a definitional reshuffle of the Mathlib membership predicate
772	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.mem\_domain}, which already states that
773	  \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) iff there is some partial-map representative of
774	  \(f\) defined at \(\eta_W\); the only content here is reordering the two
775	  conjuncts under the existential so the \(\varphi_U \mapsto f\) component lands
776	  first (matching the way later witnesses in the project construct partial-map
777	  representatives). The forward and backward implications are immediate from
778	  the unfolded definition.
779	\end{proof}
780	
781	\begin{remark}
782	  \label{rmk:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle_scope}
783	  \textbf{Relationship to \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}.}
784	  \cref{lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle} is the iter-179 Lane~D honest
785	  fallback for the substantive Hartshorne-II.6 ``regular = no pole''
786	  reformulation that \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} states in its full
787	  \(\ord_W \geq 0\) form. The reshuffle captures only the
788	  definitional half of the equivalence (existence of a partial-map
789	  representative through a prime-divisor generic point); upgrading it to the
790	  substantive \(\ord_W \geq 0\) form requires the
791	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap}-to-function-field pullback machinery, which is
792	  not shipped by Mathlib at commit \texttt{b80f227}. See the iter-179
793	  \texttt{\% NOTE} block on \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} for the
794	  history.
795	\end{remark}

909	\section{Out of scope}
910	\label{sec:codim1_out_of_scope}
911	
912	This chapter packages \emph{only} the two extension inputs and the Weil-divisor
913	reformulation needed for A.4.a. The following adjacent content is explicitly out of
914	scope:
915	\begin{itemize}
916	  \item \textbf{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and the regular \(\Rightarrow\)
917	    Cohen--Macaulay corollary.} Proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}
918	    as \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} and \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}; this
919	    chapter only consumes them (in Step~2 of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}; see
920	    \cref{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}).
921	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.2 itself.} The combination of
922	    \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} and \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} into the
923	    statement ``a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety
924	    extends'' lives in \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} as
925	    \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}; this chapter only supplies the two inputs.
926	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.4 / Corollary~3.7 / Theorem~3.8 / Proposition~3.9 /
927	    Proposition~3.10.} The remainder of Milne~\S I.3 (the ``map between AVs is
928	    translated homomorphism'' family of results, and the
929	    \(\mathbb A^1 \dashrightarrow A\) / unirational\(\dashrightarrow A\) constancy
930	    statements) is downstream of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} but is not
931	    needed by A.4 in the project's current routing: the genus-\(0\) base case is
932	    handled via the proven Rigidity Lemma + \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut
933	    (\cref{chap:RigidityKbar}), and the positive-genus arm consumes only the bare
934	    Theorem~3.2 statement.
935	  \item \textbf{Weil-divisor degree on \(X \times X\) for \(X\) a curve.} The proof of
936	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} invokes the Weil-divisor apparatus on the
937	    smooth surface \(X \times X\) but does \emph{not} need the degree map of
938	    \cref{def:divisor_degree} (which is curve-specific); only the codim-\(1\)
939	    decomposition \(\div(f) = \div(f)_0 - \div(f)_\infty\) on a nonsingular surface is
940	    used. The bilinear degree formula on \(X \times X\) for \(X\) a curve (intersection
941	    numbers, Hartshorne~V.1) is out of scope.
942	  \item \textbf{Cartier divisors and the Cartier/Weil comparison.} On a smooth variety
943	    they coincide and the chapter uses Weil throughout; the Cartier formalism is not
944	    introduced.
945	  \item \textbf{Generalisation to non-algebraically-closed base fields.} The
946	    project's positive-genus arm runs over \(\bar k\) throughout; descent of the
947	    extension to a non-algebraically-closed base, if ever needed, would require
948	    Galois-descent machinery and is not in scope.
949	  \item \textbf{Resolution of indeterminacy by blow-ups} (Hironaka-style, for higher
950	    indeterminacy codimensions on a smooth variety with a non-group-variety target):
951	    out of scope. The combination of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} and
952	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} for \(G\) a group variety is sharper than any
953	    blow-up-based approach because it produces an extension on the original \(X\)
954	    rather than on a blow-up; no blow-up apparatus is needed.
955	\end{itemize}

2	\chapter{Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)}
3	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}
4	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II \S6, pp.~130--137 +
5	% IV.1, pp.~294--296 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
6	% Stacks pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (divisors),
7	% 02ME (order at a point on a regular 1-dimensional scheme),
8	% 0BE0 (degree of a divisor on a curve), 0BE3 (principal divisors have
9	% degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve).
10	\section{Setup and motivation}
11	This chapter is the first of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
12	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
13	\[
14	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
15	  \,\colon\,
16	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
17	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
18	\]
19	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
20	divisor of a closed point on a curve (\texttt{RR.1}, this chapter); the
21	Riemann--Roch dimension formula \(\ell(D) = \deg D + 1\) for \(\deg D \geq 0\) on
22	a genus-\(0\) curve (\texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections argument identifying
23	\(\dim_k H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) via \(1\) and a non-constant function
24	(\texttt{RR.3}); the ``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)''
25	classification recovered as a degree-\(1\) morphism into \(\mathbb P^1\) via
26	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
27	Mathlib ships no \texttt{WeilDivisor} on a scheme. Adjacent pieces exist, but
28	none of them is the data structure we need: \texttt{MeromorphicOn.divisor}
29	(\texttt{Mathlib.Analysis.Meromorphic.Divisor}) is the order function of a
30	meromorphic function valued in a \emph{normed} field, not a formal sum of
31	codimension-\(1\) cycles on a scheme; \texttt{CommRing.Pic}
32	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) is the ring-level Picard group of
33	invertible modules under tensor product, with no scheme-level analogue;
34	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap}
35	(\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}) packages rational
36	maps but not divisors. Consequently this chapter, and the three siblings, are
37	\emph{project-bespoke}: the only Mathlib tools they rely on are
38	discrete-valuation rings, the function field, and the ambient scheme API.
39	The audit grounding this scope decision is in
40	\texttt{analogies/rrbridge-survey.md}.
41	This chapter establishes the formal-sum data type
42	\(\mathrm{Div}(X) = \bigoplus_{\substack{Z \subset X \\ \mathrm{codim} 1}} \mathbb Z\) on a
43	Noetherian integral scheme \(X\) satisfying Hartshorne's condition \((*)\), the
44	principal-divisor homomorphism \(\mathrm{div} \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \mathrm{Div}(X)\) on a
45	curve, the degree map \(\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \to \mathbb Z\) on a smooth proper
46	curve over an algebraically closed field, and the linear-equivalence
47	relation. The \emph{degree-zero of a principal divisor} statement
48	(\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}) is included for completeness; its proof
49	decomposes into a separate sub-build inside \texttt{RR.1} (it uses the
50	auxiliary lemma that any non-constant rational function on \(C\) defines a
51	finite morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\)). The line-bundle construction $\mathcal
52	O_C(D)\( and the associated cohomology computation \)H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$
53	live in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3}; the Riemann--Roch dimension
54	formula lives in \texttt{RR.2}; the genus-\(0\) classification proper lives in
55	\texttt{RR.4}.
56	Throughout this chapter, the standing convention is Hartshorne's condition
57	\((*)\):
58	% SOURCE QUOTE: "\((\ast)\) \(X\) is a noetherian integral separated scheme which
59	% is regular in codimension one."
60	\textit{Source: Hartshorne, II \S6, p.~130, the conditional \((*)\).}
61	\[
62	  (\ast)\qquad
63	  X \text{ is a noetherian integral separated scheme which is regular in
64	  codimension one.}
65	\]
66	For the degree map and for principal-divisor statements on a curve, we
67	additionally assume \(X\) is a complete (i.e.\ proper) nonsingular curve over
68	an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), where Hartshorne's condition \((*)\) is
69	automatic.
70	\paragraph{Standing hypothesis \((*)\) in the Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).}
71	Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) does not bundle Hartshorne's four-clause
72	\((\ast)\) as a single typeclass. The chapter pins \((*)\) to a \emph{layered}
73	typeclass set that the prover-agent threads through the Lean signatures in
74	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean}:
75	\begin{itemize}
76	  \item \textbf{Basic formal-sum layer} (carrier of \ref{def:prime_divisor} and
77	    \ref{def:codim1_cycles}, the additive group \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\), the degree
78	    \ref{def:divisor_degree}, the degree-homomorphism
79	    \ref{thm:divisor_degree_hom}, and \ref{def:divisor_closed_point}): the
80	    only Mathlib typeclass invoked is \texttt{[IsIntegral X]}
81	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme} via
82	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Properties}). Integrality of \(X\) already
83	    delivers \texttt{IrreducibleSpace X.carrier} (the carrier-level instance
84	    chain), so \(X\) has a unique generic point and the function field
85	    \texttt{X.functionField} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField})
86	    is a field; separatedness and noetherianness are \emph{not} required for
87	    the bare formal-sum data type.
88	  \item \textbf{Order/principal layer} (\ref{def:order_at_point},
89	    \ref{def:principal_divisor}, \ref{thm:principal_hom}): the Lean
90	    signature additionally threads
91	    \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X]}
92	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Noetherian}) and Hartshorne's
93	    regular-in-codimension-one clause. The latter has \emph{no} Mathlib
94	    typeclass shipping it directly; the chapter's pin is the project-side
95	    predicate ``for every \(x \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} = 1\) the
96	    stalk \(\mathcal O_{X,x}\) is a discrete valuation ring'', encodable as
97	    the conjunction
98	    $\forall x,\; \texttt{Order.coheight}\,x = 1 \Rightarrow
99	    \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing}\,(\mathcal O_{X,x})$. Iter-173+ may
100	    introduce a \texttt{Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne} predicate to
101	    abbreviate this; until then the order/principal lemmas thread the DVR
102	    hypothesis explicitly at each codim-1 point.
103	  \item \textbf{Curve layer} (\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}): the standing
104	    hypothesis tightens to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
105	    over \(\bar k\) with explicit integrality witness, namely
106	    \begin{quote}
107	      \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
108	       (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
109	       \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
110	       \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
111	    \end{quote}
112	    matching the standing typeclass set used by the rest of the project's
113	    curve API (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.Genus},
114	    \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity}). Hartshorne's \((*)\) is
115	    automatic for this layer (\texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian]} from finite type
116	    over a field; regular-in-codim-1 from smoothness; separatedness from
117	    properness; integrality threaded).
118	\end{itemize}
119	\noindent
120	The \texttt{[IsIntegral X]} hypothesis is the only one threaded through the
121	\emph{statement} of every Lean signature in
122	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean} that mentions a
123	function field, principal divisor, or order. The noetherian-and-DVR
124	content lives inside the proof bodies (sub-build), where the prover threads
125	the appropriate stronger hypothesis at the use site.
126	\section{Codim-\(1\) cycle group}
127	\begin{definition}
128	\leanok
129	[Prime divisor of a scheme]
130	  \label{def:prime_divisor}
131	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor}
132	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "prime divisor" under
133	  % condition \((*)\)) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
134	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW (divisors).
135	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\). A \emph{prime
136	  % divisor} on \(X\) is a closed integral subscheme \(Y\) of codimension one."
137	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of prime divisor).}
138	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). A \emph{prime divisor} on \(X\) is a closed integral
139	  subscheme \(Y \subset X\) of codimension one. Equivalently, by the standard
140	  generic-point correspondence (Hartshorne II \S6, p.~130, ``let $\eta \in
141	  Y\( be its generic point\ldots''), \)Y$ is determined by its generic point
142	  \(\eta \in X\): closed integral subschemes of codimension one of \(X\) are in
143	  natural bijection with points \(x \in X\) such that the closure
144	  \(\overline{\{x\}} \subset X\) has codimension one. The closure
145	  \(\overline{\{x\}}\), equipped with the \emph{reduced induced subscheme
146	  structure}, is automatically integral (irreducible, because the closure
147	  of a single point is irreducible, and reduced by construction); the only
148	  non-automatic condition is the codimension.
149	  \paragraph{Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).} The Lean structure
150	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor} bundles a point
151	  \texttt{point : X} together with a codimension-one witness, encoded via
152	  the Mathlib predicate
153	  \[
154	    \texttt{Order.coheight\ point\ =\ 1}
155	  \]
156	  on the specialisation preorder of the topological space underlying \(X\).
157	  Concretely, Mathlib's preorder on \texttt{X.carrier} is defined by
158	  ``\(x \leq y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x\)'' (i.e.\ \(x \in \overline{\{y\}}\);
159	  see \texttt{Mathlib.Topology.Specialization} and
160	  \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}). Under this convention the generic
161	  point of an integral scheme is the unique \emph{maximal} element of the
162	  preorder, so for the generic point \(\eta_Y\) of a prime divisor \(Y\) the
163	  only point strictly above \(\eta_Y\) is the generic point \(\eta_X\) of \(X\),
164	  and the chain \(\eta_Y < \eta_X\) has length one, giving
165	  \texttt{Order.coheight\ \(\eta_Y\)\ =\ 1}. The DVR/integrality content of
166	  ``the closure of \texttt{point} with the reduced subscheme structure is
167	  integral'' is automatic from \(\overline{\{\,\texttt{point}\,\}}\) being
168	  irreducible, so the Lean structure does not need a separate integrality
169	  field. The iter-172 placeholder \texttt{isCodim1AndIntegral : True} in
170	  \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean:84--90} is to be
171	  refined to \texttt{coheight : Order.coheight point = 1} (named field, type
172	  \texttt{Prop}) in the iter-173 prover step, retaining the same
173	  \texttt{point} field for downstream defeq.
174	  \paragraph{Reference predicates considered and rejected.}
175	  Two alternatives were considered and rejected at the iter-172
176	  lean-vs-blueprint-checker stage:
177	  \begin{itemize}
178	    \item \texttt{IsIntegral\ (X.closedSubschemeOfPoint\ x)}: rejected
179	      because Mathlib (\texttt{b80f227}) ships \emph{no}
180	      \texttt{closedSubschemeOfPoint} constructor. The closest available
181	      object is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData}
182	      (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf.Basic}), which packages
183	      a quasi-coherent ideal sheaf but does not produce a reduced induced
184	      subscheme on the closure of a point. Building that constructor is
185	      itself a Mathlib-upstream-scale project (Stacks tag 01J9 ``reduced
186	      induced scheme structure''), outside the scope of \texttt{RR.1}.
187	    \item A bundled \texttt{Scheme.IdealSheafData}-based prime-divisor
188	      carrier: rejected because the chapter only needs the generic-point
189	      witness for downstream order/principal-divisor consumption; carrying
190	      an \texttt{IdealSheafData} adds redundant data the chapter never
191	      uses.
192	  \end{itemize}
193	  The \texttt{Order.coheight}-based encoding is therefore the
194	  minimum-Mathlib-dependence pin compatible with the chapter's downstream
195	  needs.
196	\end{definition}
197	\begin{definition}
198	\leanok
199	[Codim-\(1\) cycle group / Weil divisor group of a scheme]
200	  \label{def:codim1_cycles}
201	  
202	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor}
203	  \uses{def:prime_divisor}
204	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "Weil divisor" under
205	  % condition \((*)\)) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
206	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW.
207	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A \emph{Weil divisor} is an element of the free abelian
208	  % group \(\operatorname{Div} X\) generated by the prime divisors. We write a
209	  % divisor as \(D = \sum n_i Y_i\), where the \(Y_i\) are prime divisors, the
210	  % \(n_i\) are integers, and only finitely many \(n_i\) are different from
211	  % zero. If all the \(n_i \geq 0\), we say that \(D\) is \emph{effective}."
212	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (Weil divisor group).}
213	  Let \(X\) be a Noetherian integral separated scheme that is regular in
214	  codimension one (i.e.\ \(X\) satisfies \((*)\)). The \emph{Weil divisor group}
215	  \[
216	    \mathrm{Div}(X) \;=\; \bigoplus_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
217	    \mathbb Z \cdot Y
218	  \]
219	  is the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of \(X\)
220	  (\ref{def:codim1_cycles}); an element \(D = \sum n_i Y_i\) with finitely
221	  many nonzero coefficients \(n_i \in \mathbb Z\) is called a \emph{Weil
222	  divisor}, and is \emph{effective} when all \(n_i \geq 0\).
223	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
224	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor X} is given for an arbitrary
225	  scheme \texttt{X : Scheme} as the formal-sum data type
226	  \texttt{X.PrimeDivisor \(\to_0\) \(\mathbb Z\)}; integrality of \(X\) is
227	  \emph{not} required to form the additive group \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\) (it becomes
228	  necessary only at the order/principal-divisor layer where the function
229	  field appears). Hartshorne's \((*)\) is the standing convention in the
230	  prose but is not threaded into the typeclass arguments of this base
231	  declaration. See ``Standing hypothesis \((*)\) in the Lean encoding'' above
232	  for the per-layer typeclass discipline.
233	\end{definition}
234	\section{Order of a rational function at a prime divisor}
235	\begin{definition}
236	\leanok
237	[Order of a rational function at a prime divisor]
238	  \label{def:order_at_point}
239	  
240	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
241	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order}
242	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\) of a prime
243	  % divisor; "zero/pole of \(f\) along \(Y\)") (read from
244	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
245	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02ME.
246	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its
247	  % generic point. Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{\eta, X}\) of \(X\) at
248	  % \(\eta\) is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field \(K\), the
249	  % function field of \(X\). We call the corresponding discrete valuation
250	  % \(v_Y\) the \emph{valuation of \(Y\)}."
251	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let \(f \in K^\ast\) be any nonzero rational function
252	  % on \(X\). Then \(v_Y(f)\) is an integer. If it is positive, we say \(f\) has
253	  % a \emph{zero} along \(Y\), of that order; if it is negative, we say \(f\)
254	  % has a \emph{pole} along \(Y\), of order \(-v_Y(f)\)."
255	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\)).}
256	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). For a prime divisor \(Y \subset X\) with generic
257	  point \(\eta \in Y\), the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
258	  valuation ring (by regularity in codimension one) with quotient field the
259	  function field \(K(X)\). For a nonzero rational function \(f \in K(X)^{\times}\)
260	  the \emph{order of \(f\) along \(Y\)} is the integer
261	  \[
262	    \ord_Y(f) \;:=\; v_Y(f) \;\in\; \mathbb Z,
263	  \]
264	  where \(v_Y\) is the normalised discrete valuation associated to
265	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\). If \(\ord_Y(f) > 0\), \(f\) has a \emph{zero of
266	  order \(\ord_Y(f)\) along \(Y\)}; if \(\ord_Y(f) < 0\), \(f\) has a \emph{pole
267	  of order \(-\ord_Y(f)\) along \(Y\)}.
268	  Specialisation to a curve. If \(C\) is a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)
269	  and \(P \in C\) is a closed point, then \(P\) is automatically a prime
270	  divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the \(1\)-dimensional
271	  scheme \(C\)); the local ring \(\mathcal O_{C, P}\) is a discrete valuation
272	  ring by smoothness, and \(\ord_P(f) = v_P(f)\) is the standard DVR
273	  valuation of \(f\) at \(P\).
274	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope (iter-175 pin, per analogist
275	  \texttt{dvr-rationalmap-order}).} The iter-175 Lean signature is
276	  \begin{quote}
277	    \texttt{noncomputable def order \{X : Scheme.\{u\}\} [IsIntegral X]\\
278	    \phantom{}\quad[IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)\\
279	    \phantom{}\quad[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]\\
280	    \phantom{}\quad(f : X.functionField) : \(\mathbb Z\)}
281	  \end{quote}
282	  with \emph{no} \(f \neq 0\) hypothesis (junk-on-\(f = 0\) convention,
283	  see below). The body is
284	  \[
285	    \texttt{order}\ Y\ f \;:=\;
286	      \texttt{WithZero.log}\ \bigl(
287	        \texttt{Ring.ordFrac}\ (X.\texttt{presheaf.stalk}\ Y.\texttt{point})\ f
288	      \bigr).
289	  \]
290	  \emph{Mathlib API path.}
291	  \begin{itemize}
292	    \item \texttt{Ring.ordFrac R : K \(\to\!\!*_0\) \(\mathbb Z^{m_0}\)}
293	      (with \(\mathbb Z^{m_0} = \texttt{WithZero (Multiplicative}\ \mathbb Z)\)),
294	      from \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic} (Stacks
295	      tag~\texttt{02MD}), is Mathlib's canonical monoid-with-zero hom
296	      extending the length-of-quotient valuation
297	      \texttt{Ring.ord} from a Noetherian Krull-dim-\(\leq 1\) ring to
298	      its fraction field via \texttt{Submonoid.LocalizationMap.lift\textsubscript{0}}.
299	      For a DVR-uniformised local ring its value on \(r \in R\) is the
300	      length of \(R/(r)\) (the DVR valuation); on \(a/b \in K\), it is
301	      \(\texttt{ord}(a) - \texttt{ord}(b)\). The hypotheses
302	      \texttt{[CommRing R] [Nontrivial R] [IsNoetherianRing R]\\
303	      [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 R]} are exactly the typeclasses the body
304	      synthesises from \([\texttt{IsIntegral}\ X]\) (delivers
305	      \texttt{IsDomain} hence \texttt{Nontrivial} on the stalk via
306	      \texttt{instIsDomainCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsIntegral},
307	      and \texttt{IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)
308	      X.functionField} via
309	      \texttt{instIsFractionRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafFunctionField}),
310	      from \([\texttt{IsLocallyNoetherian}\ X]\) (delivers
311	      \texttt{IsNoetherianRing} on the stalk via
312	      \texttt{instIsNoetherianRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsLocallyNoetherian}),
313	      and from the explicitly threaded
314	      \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]}.
315	    \item \texttt{WithZero.log : \(\mathbb Z^{m_0} \to \mathbb Z\)}
316	      from \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero} is Mathlib's
317	      canonical projection to the additive value group with
318	      junk-on-zero: \texttt{log\_zero}: \(\log 0 = 0\);
319	      \texttt{log\_mul}: \(\log(x \cdot y) = \log x + \log y\) for
320	      \(x, y \neq 0\). This makes the downstream additive identities
321	      \(v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)\), \(v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)\),
322	      \(v_Y(1) = 0\) available as named lemmas for
323	      \ref{def:order_at_point}.
324	  \end{itemize}
325	  \emph{Junk-on-\(f = 0\) convention.} Setting \(f = 0\) in
326	  \texttt{X.functionField} feeds \(0\) into
327	  \texttt{Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)} (zero of the
328	  monoid-with-zero hom; \texttt{Ring.ordFrac} maps \(0\) to
329	  \(0 \in \mathbb Z^{m_0}\)); then \texttt{WithZero.log 0 = 0} so
330	  \(\texttt{order}\ Y\ 0 = 0\). This is the canonical Mathlib junk
331	  choice; no separate \(f \neq 0\) hypothesis is threaded on the
332	  signature.
333	  \emph{In-tree gap-fill (project-side; Mathlib upstream opportunity).}
334	  Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) has no direct bridge from the
335	  topological condition \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\ Y.\texttt{point} = 1\)
336	  on \texttt{X.carrier} to the algebraic condition
337	  \(\texttt{Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)}\) on the
338	  stalk. The missing fact (Stacks tag~\texttt{02IZ} / \texttt{005X}) is
339	  the topological identity ``coheight of \(x\) in the scheme's
340	  specialisation preorder equals the height of the prime ideal
341	  corresponding to \(x\) in any affine chart''; the algebraic step
342	  ``ideal height \(=\) stalk Krull dim'' is in Mathlib as
343	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height}
344	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height}) and
345	  \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}
346	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}). The iter-175
347	  workaround is to thread the explicit instance
348	  \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]} on
349	  \texttt{order} (and downstream consumers); a future two-line
350	  Mathlib upstream PR pinning the coheight-height bridge would let
351	  the project drop the explicit argument in favour of synthesis from
352	  \texttt{coheight Y.point = 1} via the \texttt{PrimeDivisor} carrier.
353	\end{definition}
354	\section{Divisor of a closed point on a curve}
355	\begin{definition}
356	\leanok
357	[Divisor of a closed point on a curve]
358	  \label{def:divisor_closed_point}
359	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
360	  
361	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint}
362	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
363	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154):
364	  % "A prime divisor is just a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can
365	  % be written \(D = \sum n_i P_i\), where the \(P_i\) are closed points, and
366	  % \(n_i \in \mathbf Z\)."
367	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now we come to the study of divisors on curves. If \(X\)
368	  % is a nonsingular curve, then \(X\) satisfies the condition \((\ast)\) used
369	  % above, so we can talk about divisors on \(X\). A prime divisor is just
370	  % a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can be written $D = \sum n_i
371	  % P_i\(, where the \)P_i\( are closed points, and \)n_i \in \mathbf Z$."
372	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (``Divisors on Curves'').}
373	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over a field \(k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a
374	  closed point. The \emph{Weil divisor associated to \(P\)} is the element
375	  \[
376	    [P] \;:=\; 1 \cdot P \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(C)
377	  \]
378	  i.e.\ the prime divisor \(P\) with multiplicity one. The assignment is
379	  well-defined because the closed point \(P\) is automatically a codimension-\(1\)
380	  integral closed subscheme of the \(1\)-dimensional integral scheme \(C\).
381	  Conversely, every prime divisor on \(C\) is of this form, so an arbitrary
382	  divisor on \(C\) is a finite formal \(\mathbb Z\)-linear combination
383	  \(\sum n_i [P_i]\) of closed points.
384	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope (iter-174 pin).} The Lean signature
385	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint} accepts a
386	  scheme \texttt{C : Scheme.\{u\}}, a point \texttt{P : C}, and a
387	  hypothesis \texttt{(\_hP : IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C))}. It does
388	  \emph{not} typeclass-pin ``smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)'' at the
389	  carrier level, and it does \emph{not} thread the
390	  one-dimensional-integral-scheme content into the declaration head.
391	  Instead the iter-174 encoding is \emph{junk-defined} outside the
392	  codim-\(1\) regime:
393	  \[
394	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP\ :=\
395	      \begin{cases}
396	        \texttt{Finsupp.single}\ \langle P, h\rangle\ 1 &
397	          \text{if } h : \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1,\\
398	        0 & \text{otherwise.}
399	      \end{cases}
400	  \]
401	  On the off-branch --- where \texttt{P} is not a codimension-one point
402	  (e.g.\ \texttt{P} is the generic point of \texttt{C}, or a closed point
403	  of codimension \(\geq 2\) on a higher-dimensional scheme) ---
404	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} returns the zero divisor \(0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\). This
405	  convention lets the Lean signature avoid threading
406	  \texttt{[OneDimensional C]} / \texttt{[IsIntegral C]} typeclass payload
407	  at the declaration head; the well-definedness consumer must instead
408	  invoke the bridge equation lemmas
409	  \ref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_single} and \ref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
410	  to expose the two branches at the point of use.
411	  On the intended regime (one-dimensional integral scheme \(C\), \(P\) a
412	  closed point), the chapter's mathematical statement
413	  \([P] := 1 \cdot P \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) is recovered by combining
414	  \ref{def:divisor_closed_point} with the chain
415	  \(\texttt{IsClosed \{P\}} \Rightarrow \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1\)
416	  that the prover threads inside the consumer (using the
417	  one-dimensional-integrality content).
418	\end{definition}
419	\begin{lemma}
420	\leanok
421	[Bridge equation: \texttt{ofClosedPoint} in the codim-\(1\) regime]
422	  \label{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_single}
423	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint_eq_single}
424	  Let \(C : \texttt{Scheme.\{u\}}\) be a scheme and let \(P \in C\) be a
425	  point with \(\texttt{IsClosed}\,(\{P\} : \texttt{Set}\ C)\). If
426	  \(h : \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1\) (the codimension-one witness
427	  promoting \(P\) to a \texttt{PrimeDivisor} via \ref{def:divisor_closed_point}),
428	  then
429	  \[
430	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP \;=\;
431	      \texttt{Finsupp.single}\ \langle P, h\rangle\ 1
432	      \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(C).
433	  \]
434	\end{lemma}
435	\begin{proof}
436	  \leanok
437	  Unfolds the dependent-\texttt{if} in the definition of
438	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} (\ref{def:divisor_closed_point}) on the
439	  positive branch using the hypothesis \(h\).
440	\end{proof}
441	\begin{lemma}
442	\leanok
443	[Bridge equation: \texttt{ofClosedPoint} junk-branch]
444	  \label{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
445	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
446	  Let \(C : \texttt{Scheme.\{u\}}\) be a scheme and let \(P \in C\) be a
447	  point with \(\texttt{IsClosed}\,(\{P\} : \texttt{Set}\ C)\). If
448	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\ P \neq 1\) (the off-branch: \(P\) is not a
449	  codimension-one point of \(C\) --- e.g.\ \(P\) is the generic point of an
450	  integral scheme, or a higher-codim point of a higher-dimensional
451	  scheme), then
452	  \[
453	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP \;=\;
454	      0 \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(C).
455	  \]
456	\end{lemma}
457	\begin{proof}
458	  \leanok
459	  Unfolds the dependent-\texttt{if} in the definition of
460	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} (\ref{def:divisor_closed_point}) on the
461	  negative branch using the hypothesis
462	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\ P \neq 1\).
463	\end{proof}
464	\section{Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base}
465	\begin{definition}
466	\leanok
467	[Degree of a divisor on a curve over \(\bar k\)]
468	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
469	  \label{def:divisor_degree}
470	  
471	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree}
472	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
473	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154).
474	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE0.
475	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We define the \emph{degree} of \(D\) to be \(\sum n_i\)."
476	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (definition of degree on a
477	  nonsingular curve).}
478	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
