1	\chapter{Genus of a smooth proper curve}
2	\label{chap:Genus}
3	
4	Throughout this chapter, \(k\) denotes a field and \(C\) a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(k\) of relative dimension \(1\). In other words, \(C \to \Spec k\) is a morphism of schemes carrying the geometric typeclasses that make it a curve in the modern sense.
5	
6	\section{Definition}
7	
8	% NOTE: classical definition \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\); see
9	% Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, Ch.~IV \S1 (genus of a curve) — TOC
10	% summarised in `references/hartshorne-ag.md`. The finiteness backing
11	% (Serre on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme) is queued in
12	% `references/stacks-0BUG.md` and chapter
13	% \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK}.
14	\begin{definition}\leanok
15	[Genus of a smooth proper curve]
16	  \label{def:genus}
17	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}
18	  
19	  The \emph{genus} of a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(k\) is the natural number
20	  \[
21	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_k H^1(C,\, \mathcal O_C),
22	  \]
23	  the \(k\)-dimension of the first sheaf-cohomology group of the structure sheaf of \(C\).
24	\end{definition}
25	
26	\begin{proof}
27	  
28	  \leanok
29	  The definition is realised by passing the structure sheaf through the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules construction and applying the \(k\)-linear sheaf cohomology \(H^1\). The result is a \(k\)-vector space; its dimension is extracted via the standard finiteness rank.
30	
31	  \begin{remark}[Lean implementation]
32	    The formal definition uses the project's \(\ModuleCat k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf:
33	    \[
34	      \mathtt{genus}\,C \;:=\; \Module.\mathtt{finrank}\,k\,\bigl(\Scheme.\HModule\,k\,(\Scheme.\toModuleKSheaf\,C)\,1\bigr).
35	    \]
36	    The carrier \(\toModuleKSheaf C\) is the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules constructed from \(\struct C\) via the structure morphism; the wrapper \(\HModule k F 1\) is the \(\Module k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology, given by \(\Ext\) from the constant sheaf at \(\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k\), which carries a canonical \(\Module k\)-structure inherited from the \(\Linear k\)-enrichment of the sheaf category.
37	  \end{remark}
38	
39	  Mathlib's \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\) returns the \(k\)-dimension when the underlying module is finite-dimensional and zero otherwise. For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve, Serre's theorem on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme guarantees \(\dim_k H^1(C, \struct C) < \infty\), so \(g(C)\) is the geometric genus on the relevant case. The closure is therefore the one-liner above, confirmed to typecheck against current Mathlib.
40	\end{proof}
41	
42	Equivalent reformulations available once Riemann--Roch and Serre duality are formalised:
43	\begin{itemize}
44	  \item \(g(C) = \dim_k H^0(C,\, \Omega^1_{C/k})\) (Serre duality).
45	  \item \(g(C) = 1 - \chi(\mathcal O_C)\), where \(\chi\) is the Euler characteristic.
46	  \item For a smooth projective curve over \(\mathbb C\), \(g(C)\) equals the topological genus of \(C(\mathbb C)\).
47	\end{itemize}
48	Each of these requires Mathlib infrastructure that is currently absent. The first reformulation in particular requires the sheaf of relative differentials of schemes (Phase~B), and the others require Riemann--Roch / Serre duality / a topological-vs-algebraic geometry comparison.
49	
50	\section{Mathlib gap}
51	\label{sec:Genus_status}
52	
53	The honest definition \(\dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) requires a \(\Module k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology of \(\struct C\) on the topology of opens of the underlying space of \(C\). Through earlier iterations the project has built this:
54	
55	\begin{itemize}
56	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of \(\AddCommGrpCat\)-valued sheaves} (Mathlib): defined as \(\Ext\) from the constant sheaf at \(\AddCommGrpCat.\mathrm{of}\,(\ULift\,\mathbb Z)\).
57	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of \(\ModuleCat k\)-valued sheaves} (this project): the parallel \(\Module k\)-valued construction with unit object \(\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k\) instead of \(\ULift\,\mathbb Z\), gluing through the \(\Linear k\)-enrichment of the sheaf category to recover a \(\Module k\) structure on the cohomology groups.
58	  \item \textbf{Structure sheaf as a \(\ModuleCat k\)-valued sheaf} (this project): for \(C\) over \(\Spec k\), the sheafification of the presheaf \(U \mapsto \ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,(\Gamma(C, U))\) where the \(k\)-action is the algebra structure induced by the structure morphism.
59	  \item \textbf{\(H^0\) algebraic bridge} (this project): \(H^0(C, \struct C)_{\Module k} \simeq_k \Hom_{\Sheaf}(\mathrm{const}, \toModuleKSheaf C)\), via Mathlib's \(\Ext\) linear equivalence. Used downstream for the geometric content \(H^0(C, \struct C) \simeq k\) on a connected proper \(k\)-curve.
60	\end{itemize}
61	
62	The remaining Mathlib gap for the \emph{theorem} (as opposed to the \emph{definition}) of genus:
63	
64	\begin{itemize}
65	  \item \textbf{Serre finiteness for \(H^i(C, \mathcal F)\) on a proper \(k\)-curve.} Mathlib's \(\ModuleCat.\mathtt{finite\_ext}\) provides \(\Module.\mathtt{Finite}\) for module-Ext only, not sheaf-Ext. The Čech-cohomology scaffold is the recommended direction.
66	\end{itemize}
67	
68	\section{User authorisation of \texttt{noncomputable}}
69	\label{sec:Genus_noncomputable}
70	
71	The original challenge file writes \(\mathtt{def\ genus}\), not \(\mathtt{noncomputable\ def\ genus}\). Because \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\), \(\Abelian.\Ext.\mathtt{instModule}\), and the structure-sheaf-as-\(k\)-module construction are all noncomputable in Mathlib, the honest closure of the body requires the \(\mathtt{noncomputable}\) modifier on the \(\mathtt{def}\). The user explicitly authorised agents to add this modifier as a non-signature change: names, argument types, and argument order remain verbatim.
72	
73	\section{What this iteration does and does not}
74	
75	This iteration \emph{defines} the genus. It does not \emph{compute} it on any specific curve, and it does not prove that the result equals the geometric genus in the classical sense (which requires Serre finiteness for non-vacuity and Riemann--Roch for verification on examples). The downstream theorem \(\mathtt{smoothOfRelativeDimension\_genus}\) in \(\mathtt{Jacobian.lean}\) states that the Jacobian of \(C\) is smooth of relative dimension \(g(C)\) --- that theorem will require Serre finiteness on the way and is queued for the iteration that closes the Jacobian construction itself.

1	\chapter{The Jacobian as an abelian variety}
2	\label{chap:Jacobian}
3	Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(k\) in the sense of Chapter~\ref{chap:Genus}, with genus \(g = g(C)\).
4	The Jacobian is built as the Albanese object of \(C\) in the category of smooth proper group schemes over \(k\), and the construction stratifies by genus. For positive genus the witness \emph{object} is the abelian variety \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) produced by \textbf{Route~A (the Picard scheme via FGA representability)}: this is the project's committed critical path, and it is \emph{mandatory} for the positive-genus object regardless of any other route choice --- the object must be a genuine \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety even when \(C(k) = \emptyset\) (see \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} Route~A and \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness}). The FGA representability for \(\Pic_{C/k}\) and its Hilbert/Quot prerequisites are the single largest Mathlib build-out the project gates on. For genus \(0\) the witness object is instead the trivial scheme \(\Spec k\), \textbf{decoupled} from Route~A's representability engine; the only remaining content in that arm is a rigidity \emph{statement}, the characteristic-free keystone \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} developed in \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} via the Milne \S I.3 rigidity chain (Rigidity Lemma \(+\) additivity Corollary~1.5 \(+\) Corollary~1.2 \(+\) rational-map extension), \emph{cube-free} and \emph{not} the differential / Serre-duality argument. The over-\(\bar k\) declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}, which still carries \([\mathtt{CharZero}]\), is the retained fallback-(a) artifact and is \emph{not} the declaration the genus-\(0\) witness consumes.
5	\section{The Albanese construction}
6	For a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(k\), the Albanese variety is the universal abelian variety receiving a morphism from \(C\). Over an algebraically closed field this is classically identified with the connected component of the identity of the Picard scheme, but we construct it directly from symmetric powers.
7	\subsection{Symmetric powers and the Abel--Jacobi map}
8	Let \(C^{(g)} = \Sym^g(C)\) be the \(g\)\textsuperscript{th} symmetric power of \(C\) (the quotient of \(C^g\) by the action of the symmetric group \(S_g\)). It is a smooth proper scheme over \(k\) of dimension \(g\). Fix a \(k\)-rational point \(P_0 \in C(k)\). The map
9	\[
10	  \sigma \colon C^{(g)} \longrightarrow \Pic^g_{C/k}, \qquad
11	  \sum_{i=1}^g [P_i] \longmapsto \mathcal O_C\bigl(\textstyle\sum_{i=1}^g P_i - g P_0\bigr)
12	\]
13	is surjective and birational onto its image; its fibres are projective spaces (Brill--Noether--Riemann--Roch). Choosing \(P_0\) gives a translation isomorphism \(\Pic^g_{C/k} \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \Pic^0_{C/k}\), and the Stein factorisation of \(\sigma\) yields the Jacobian as the connected component of the identity.
14	In the Lean formalisation we do not have general symmetric powers of schemes (or quotients by finite group actions) in Mathlib. We therefore take a more direct approach: we define the Jacobian as the Albanese object of \(C\) inside the category of smooth proper group schemes over \(k\), using the universal property and the known dimension formula.
15	\subsection{The Albanese universal property}
16	\begin{definition}\leanok
17	[Albanese universal property of a pointed curve]
18	  \label{def:IsAlbanese}
19	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese}
20	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Remark rmk:Alb (the Albanese map), FGA Explained ch.~5; arXiv:math/0504020, p.~50  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)
21	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Reversing the preceding argument, we see that every
22	  % such \(b\) arises from a unique map \(a\:B^*\to \IPic_{X/k}\) such that
23	  % \(a(0)=0\).  Since \(B^*\) is integral, \(a\) factors through \(P\).  Thus the
24	  % maps \(a\:B^*\to P\) and \(b\:X\to B\) are in bijective correspondence.
25	  % Plainly, this correspondence is compatible with maps \(b'\:B\to B'\) such
26	  % that \(b'(0)=0\).  In particular, \(1_P\) corresponds to a natural map
27	  % \(u\:X\to A\) such that \(u(x)=0\), and every map \(b\:X\to B\) factors
28	  % uniquely through \(u\)."
29	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Remark (the Albanese map, rmk:Alb).}
30	  Let \((C, P)\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(k\) with a marked \(k\)-rational point \(P \in C(k)\). A smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(J\) over \(k\) is an \emph{Albanese object} for \((C, P)\) if there exists a morphism \(\iota \colon C \to J\) sending \(P\) to the identity element \(\eta \in J\), and such that every morphism \(f \colon C \to A\) from \(C\) to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(A\) with \(f(P) = \eta_A\) factors uniquely as \(f = \iota \circ g\) for some morphism of group schemes \(g \colon J \to A\).
31	\end{definition}
32	\begin{remark}
33	  \label{rem:IsAlbanese_typeclasses}
34	  The Lean encoding of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese} takes the four abelian-variety conditions on \(J\) --- group-object structure, properness, smoothness, geometric irreducibility --- as \emph{typeclass parameters} of the declaration \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese}, not as conjuncts inside the body. The body is the existential statement on \(\iota\) and its universal-factorisation property; the four hypotheses on \(J\) sit on the binder of the definition. A downstream user constructs an \texttt{IsAlbanese} term against these instances and threads them at every use site (cf.\ the \texttt{letI} chain in \texttt{AbelJacobi.ofCurve} which installs all four instances on \(\Jac(C)\) before applying any Albanese consequence). The same convention is used for the target abelian variety \(A\) in the universal-property clause.
35	\end{remark}
36	\subsection{Extracting the universal morphism}
37	An \texttt{IsAlbanese} term \(h\) packages, behind a single existential, the universal pointed morphism \(\iota \colon C \to J\) together with its two characterising properties: the pointed condition \(P \circ \iota = \eta_J\) and the universal-factorisation property of \(\iota\). The data and the two properties are extracted from the existential body by the following trio of helpers, each implemented in Lean via \texttt{Classical.choose} / \texttt{Classical.choose\_spec} on the existential content of \texttt{IsAlbanese}.
38	\begin{definition}
39	\leanok
40	[The Albanese universal morphism]
41	  \label{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}
42	  
43	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.ofCurve}
44	  Given a pointed curve \((C, P)\) and an Albanese term \(h \colon \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J\), the \emph{universal morphism} of \(h\) is the morphism
45	  \[
46	    \iota := h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J,
47	  \]
48	  obtained by \texttt{Classical.choose} on the existential body of \(h\).
49	\end{definition}
50	\begin{lemma}
51	\leanok
52	[Pointed condition for the universal morphism]
53	  \label{lem:IsAlbanese_comp_ofCurve}
54	  
55	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.comp_ofCurve}
56	  The universal morphism \(h.\mathtt{ofCurve}\) sends the marked point \(P\) to the identity of \(J\):
57	  \[
58	    P \circ h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \;=\; \eta_J.
59	  \]
60	\end{lemma}
61	\begin{lemma}
62	\leanok
63	[Universal factorisation through the Albanese object]
64	  \label{lem:IsAlbanese_exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}
65	  
66	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}
67	  For every morphism \(f \colon C \to A\) from \(C\) to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(A\) over \(k\) with \(P \circ f = \eta_A\), there exists a unique morphism \(g \colon J \to A\) such that \(f = h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \circ g\).
68	\end{lemma}
69	This extraction API is precisely what the protected interface \(\mathtt{Jacobian.ofCurve}\), \(\mathtt{Jacobian.comp\_ofCurve}\), \(\mathtt{Jacobian.exists\_unique\_ofCurve\_comp}\) of Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} consumes: each protected wrapper takes a marked point \(P \in C(k)\) as input, projects the per-point Albanese data from the bundled witness \texttt{JacobianWitness} (Definition~\ref{def:JacobianWitness}) at that \(P\), and applies the corresponding helper above.
70	\begin{theorem}\leanok
71	[Uniqueness of the Albanese object]
72	  \label{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}
73	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.unique}
74	  Let \(J_1, J_2\) be two Albanese objects for the same pointed curve \((C, P)\), with universal pointed morphisms \(\iota_1 := h_1.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J_1\) and \(\iota_2 := h_2.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J_2\) (Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}). Then there is a unique morphism \(e \colon J_1 \to J_2\) satisfying \(\iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ e\). The proof is the standard universal-property argument: each of the two universal morphisms factors through the other, the two composites equal the unique self-factorisation \(\mathrm{id}\), and the produced morphism \(e\) is therefore an isomorphism; only the existence and uniqueness of \(e\) as a morphism are exposed in the conclusion (see Remark~\ref{rem:IsAlbanese_unique_iso}).
75	\end{theorem}
76	\begin{remark}
77	  \label{rem:IsAlbanese_unique_iso}
78	  The Lean proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:IsAlbanese_unique} internally produces an inverse morphism \(h \colon J_2 \to J_1\) and verifies \(g \circ h = \mathrm{id}_{J_1}\) and \(h \circ g = \mathrm{id}_{J_2}\) (the invertibility witnesses are computed as intermediate lemmas of the tactic block) before returning the triple \((g,\ \iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ g,\ \text{uniqueness})\). The invertibility witnesses are computed but not retained in the return type
79	  \[
80	    \exists! (e : J_1 \to J_2),\ \iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ e.
81	  \]
82	  The natural strengthening would be to return
83	  \[
84	    \exists! (e : J_1 \cong J_2),\ \iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ e.\mathtt{hom},
85	  \]
86	  packaging the produced morphism together with its inverse. Tightening the conclusion in this way is a Lean-side refactor of the signature of \texttt{IsAlbanese.unique} that the project may carry out in a future iteration. The downstream consumers of \texttt{IsAlbanese.unique} (the protected \texttt{AbelJacobi.Jacobian.*} interface) use only the morphism-and-uniqueness content of the conclusion, so the current weaker conclusion is sufficient for the present API.
87	\end{remark}
88	\subsection{The Jacobian definition}
89	\begin{definition}\leanok
90	[Jacobian]
91	  \uses{def:genus, def:IsAlbanese, def:JacobianWitness, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}
92	  \label{def:Jacobian}
93	  
94	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian}
95	  The \emph{Jacobian} of \(C\) is the abelian variety \(\Jac(C)\) over \(k\) obtained as the Albanese object of \(C\): a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over \(k\) of dimension \(g(C)\) equipped with a universal morphism \(C \to \Jac(C)\) (for each choice of marked point). It is viewed as an object of the category of \(k\)-schemes over \(\Spec k\). Formally, \(\Jac(C)\) is defined as the underlying scheme of a (uniform-over-\(P\)) Albanese witness for \(C\) provided by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}.
96	  When \(g(C) = 0\) the Jacobian collapses to \(\Spec k\) itself, which is the terminal object in the category of \(k\)-schemes. When \(g(C) > 0\) the underlying scheme is the abelian variety produced by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}; the genus-one case recovers \(C\) itself (after choosing a \(k\)-rational point), and the higher genus case yields the non-trivial \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety. Both cases are handled uniformly: \(\Jac(C)\) is the underlying scheme of the Albanese witness, with no special-case branching at the level of the definition. The genus-\(0\) specialisation is implicit in the fact that any group scheme over \(k\) of relative dimension \(0\) is isomorphic to \(\Spec k\).
97	\end{definition}
98	\section{Group scheme structure and abelian-variety properties}
99	The four protected instances on \(\Jac(C)\) are each obtained, at the level of the Lean formalisation, by projecting a field of the underlying Albanese witness produced by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}. The mathematical content of each property is the corresponding statement about the Albanese variety of a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve.
100	\begin{theorem}\leanok
101	[Group object structure]
102	  \label{thm:Jacobian_grpObj}
103	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.instGrpObj}
104	  \(\Jac(C)\) carries a canonical group-object structure in the category of \(k\)-schemes over \(\Spec k\).
105	\end{theorem}
106	\begin{proof}
107	  By Definition~\ref{def:Jacobian}, \(\Jac(C) = (\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C).J\) as a \(k\)-scheme, where \(\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C\) is the Albanese witness extracted from Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} via \texttt{Classical.choice}. The witness carries the group-object structure as its field \(\mathtt{grpObj} : \mathrm{GrpObj}\, J\), so \(\Jac(C)\) inherits it by projection.
108	  Mathematically, any Albanese variety carries a unique group-object structure compatible with its universal pointed morphism. Indeed, if \(\iota \colon C \to J\) is the universal morphism sending the marked point \(P\) to the identity, then the diagonal \(C \times C \to J \times J\), post-composed with the universal morphism out of \(C \times C\) (factored through \(J\) by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}), produces the multiplication; the unique morphism to a one-point pointed scheme produces the inverse; the marked point produces the identity. Uniqueness of the resulting group-object structure is again the universal-property argument of Theorem~\ref{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}.
109	\end{proof}
110	\begin{theorem}\leanok
111	[Smoothness and dimension]
112	  \label{thm:Jacobian_smooth_genus}
113	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.smoothOfRelativeDimension_genus}
114	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Cor.~cor:sm (smoothness/dimension over a field) + Cor.~cor:ch0 (char-\(0\) smoothness) + Prp.~prp:P0 (smooth over reduced \(S\)); arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~34, 38  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)

117	  % \(\)\dim \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\) Equality holds if and
118	  % only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(0;\) if so, then \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is
119	  % smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\) everywhere."
120	  % SOURCE QUOTE (cor:ch0): "Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).
121	  % Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\). If \(k\) is
122	  % of characteristic \(0\), then \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension
123	  % \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\) everywhere."
124	  % SOURCE QUOTE (prp:P0, smoothness clause): "Assume \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists,
125	  % and represents \(\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}\).  For \(s\in S\), assume all the
126	  % \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\) are smooth of the same dimension.  Then \(\IPic_{X/S}\)
127	  % has an open group subscheme \(\IPicz_{X/S}\) of finite type whose fibers
128	  % are the \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\).  Furthermore, if \(S\) is reduced, then
129	  % \(\IPicz_{X/S}\) is smooth over \(S\)."
130	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Cor.~(cor:sm), Cor.~(cor:ch0), Prp.~(prp:P0).}
131	  \(\Jac(C) \to \Spec k\) is smooth of relative dimension \(g(C)\).
132	\end{theorem}
133	\begin{proof}
134	  The Lean formalisation projects the field \(\mathtt{smoothGenus} : \mathrm{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\,(\genus\, C)\,J.\mathtt{hom}\) of the witness \(\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C\); the relative dimension \(g(C)\) is recorded directly in the witness so that no separate dimension calculation is needed on the Lean side.
135	  Mathematically, the dimension of an Albanese variety of \(C\) equals the genus \(g = g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) (Phase~A, Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_StructureSheafAb}). The tangent space to \(\Jac(C)\) at the identity is canonically identified with \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) by deformation theory of the Picard functor: an infinitesimal deformation of \(\mathcal O_C\) is classified by an element of \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\), and this identifies the tangent space at the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) with \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\). Smoothness of \(\Jac(C)\) over \(k\) follows from the smoothness of any group scheme over a field whose tangent space at the identity is of constant rank equal to the relative dimension --- a consequence of homogeneity of the group action, since translation by any \(k\)-rational point is an automorphism and so the local structure of \(\Jac(C)\) at every \(k\)-rational point is the same as at the identity.
136	\end{proof}

138	[Properness]
139	  \label{thm:Jacobian_proper}
140	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.instIsProper}

142	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Assume \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists, and represents
143	  % \(\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}\).  For \(s\in S\), assume all the \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\) are
144	  % smooth of the same dimension.  Then \(\IPic_{X/S}\) has an open group
145	  % subscheme \(\IPicz_{X/S}\) of finite type whose fibers are the
146	  % \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\).  Furthermore, if \(S\) is reduced, then \(\IPicz_{X/S}\)
147	  % is smooth over \(S\).  Moreover, if all the \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\) are complete
148	  % and if \(\IPic_{X/S}\) is separated over \(S\), then \(\IPicz_{X/S}\) is closed
149	  % in \(\IPic_{X/S}\) and proper over \(S\)."

152	\end{theorem}
153	\begin{proof}
154	  The Lean formalisation projects the field \(\mathtt{proper} : \mathrm{IsProper}\, J.\mathtt{hom}\) of the witness.
155	  Mathematically, properness of the Albanese variety is part of its construction. In the Picard-scheme route, \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a closed subgroup scheme of the relative Picard scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\), which is itself proper over \(\Spec k\) because \(C\) is proper and geometrically connected (FGA representability for \(\Pic\) of a smooth proper geometrically connected curve produces a proper group scheme; cf.\ FGA Explained, Chapter~9). In the symmetric-power route, properness of \(\Pic^g_{C/k}\) (and hence \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) after translation) follows from properness of \(\Sym^g(C)\) together with the universal closedness of the surjection \(\sigma\). In either case, properness descends to \(\Jac(C)\).
156	\end{proof}

158	[Geometric irreducibility]
159	  \label{thm:Jacobian_geomIrred}

164	  % identity element \(e\).  \tu{(1)} Then \(G\) is separated.  \tu{(2)} Then \(G\)
165	  % is smooth if it has a geometrically reduced open subscheme.  \tu{(3)}
166	  % Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite type; it is
167	  % geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with extending \(k\)."
168	  % SOURCE QUOTE (prp:pic0): "Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).

172	  % identity \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite

174	  % extending \(k\)."
175	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Lem.~(lem:agps), Prp.~(prp:pic0).}
176	  \(\Jac(C) \to \Spec k\) is geometrically irreducible.
177	\end{theorem}
178	\begin{proof}
179	  The Lean formalisation projects the field \(\mathtt{geomIrred} : \mathrm{GeometricallyIrreducible}\, J.\mathtt{hom}\) of the witness.
180	  Mathematically, the Albanese variety \(\Jac(C)\) is the identity component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (the Picard scheme), and the identity component of a group scheme of finite type over a field is geometrically irreducible (in fact, geometrically connected, and since it is smooth at the identity by Theorem~\ref{thm:Jacobian_smooth_genus} it is geometrically irreducible). Equivalently, in the symmetric-power route, \(\Jac(C)\) arises as the Stein factorisation of \(\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}\) followed by a translation; \(\Sym^g(C)\) is geometrically irreducible (being the quotient of \(C^g\), itself geometrically irreducible as a product of geometrically irreducible factors, by a finite group action), so its image under the Stein factorisation is geometrically irreducible as well.
181	\end{proof}
182	\subsection{Sanity check in low genus}
183	\begin{itemize}
184	  \item \(g = 0\): Then \(\Jac(C) = \Spec k\), the trivial group scheme; \(\Jac(\mathbb P^1_k) = \Spec k\) is smooth of relative dimension \(0 = g\).
185	  \item \(g = 1\): Then \(\Jac(C) \cong C\) once a \(k\)-rational point on \(C\) is fixed; \(C\) is itself an elliptic curve (abelian variety of dimension \(1\)).
186	  \item \(g \ge 2\): \(\Jac(C)\) is a non-trivial \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety, never isomorphic to \(C\) as a scheme.
187	\end{itemize}
188	\section{Existence of an Albanese variety}
189	The classical construction of the Jacobian goes through the Picard scheme machinery: \(\Jac(C)\) is the connected component of the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\). An equivalent route, available whenever a \(k\)-rational point \(P \in C(k)\) has been fixed, is to take the Stein factorisation of the Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}\) and translate by \(-gP\).
190	\subsection{The Albanese witness bundle}
191	Before stating the existence theorem, we package its conclusion into a single structure --- the \emph{Albanese witness} of \(C\). This bundling reverses the quantifier order of the classical statement (see Remark~\ref{rem:JacobianWitness_quantifier_order}) and is the data structure consumed by the \texttt{Jacobian} family of declarations in this file and by the \texttt{AbelJacobi} interface of Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi}.
192	\begin{definition}
193	\leanok
194	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:genus}
195	[Albanese witness for a curve]
196	  \label{def:JacobianWitness}
197	  
198	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.JacobianWitness}
199	  An \emph{Albanese witness} for the smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C\) over \(k\) is a bundle of the following seven fields:
200	  \begin{itemize}
201	    \item \(J\): an object of the category of \(k\)-schemes over \(\Spec k\) --- the candidate Albanese object.
202	    \item \(\mathtt{grpObj}\): a group-object structure on \(J\) in the category of \(k\)-schemes over \(\Spec k\).
203	    \item \(\mathtt{proper}\): a proof that the structure morphism \(J \to \Spec k\) is proper.
204	    \item \(\mathtt{smooth}\): a proof that the structure morphism \(J \to \Spec k\) is smooth.
205	    \item \(\mathtt{geomIrred}\): a proof that the structure morphism \(J \to \Spec k\) is geometrically irreducible.
206	    \item \(\mathtt{smoothGenus}\): a proof that the structure morphism \(J \to \Spec k\) is smooth of relative dimension \(g(C)\). This refines \(\mathtt{smooth}\) at the specific relative dimension \(\genus\, C\) (see Remark~\ref{rem:JacobianWitness_smooth_redundancy}).
207	    \item \(\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}\): a proof that, for every \(k\)-rational marked point \(P \in C(k)\), the scheme \(J\) together with its group-object, proper, smooth, and geometrically-irreducible structures satisfies the Albanese universal property for the pointed curve \((C, P)\) in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese}.
208	  \end{itemize}
209	  Formally, an Albanese witness is a Lean-level record (a structure) whose name is \texttt{JacobianWitness}.
210	\end{definition}
211	\begin{remark}
212	  \label{rem:JacobianWitness_quantifier_order}
213	  The classical phrasing of the existence statement of an Albanese variety is
214	  \[
215	    \forall\, P \in C(k),\ \exists\, J,\ J \text{ is an Albanese object for } (C, P).
216	  \]
217	  The witness bundle reverses these quantifiers: a single \(J\) is chosen once (intrinsic to \(C\)), and the marked-point-dependent data is recorded as the family-valued field
218	  \[
219	    \mathtt{isAlbaneseFor} \colon \forall\, P \in C(k),\ \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J.
220	  \]
221	  Mathematically this reversal is justified because the underlying scheme of any Albanese object is intrinsic to \(C\): a different choice of \(P \in C(k)\) alters the universal morphism \(\iota_P \colon C \to J\) by a translation of \(J\), which is an automorphism of \(J\), and so leaves \(J\) unchanged as a scheme. The reversed bundling is what makes the per-\(P\) projection in Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} clean: \(\mathtt{Jacobian.ofCurve}\), \(\mathtt{Jacobian.comp\_ofCurve}\), and \(\mathtt{Jacobian.exists\_unique\_ofCurve\_comp}\) each take a marked point \(P\) as input and project the corresponding component of \(\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}\,P\) via the extraction trio (Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}, Lemmas~\ref{lem:IsAlbanese_comp_ofCurve}, \ref{lem:IsAlbanese_exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}).
222	\end{remark}
223	\begin{remark}
224	  \label{rem:JacobianWitness_smooth_redundancy}
225	  The fields \(\mathtt{smooth}\) and \(\mathtt{smoothGenus}\) of an Albanese witness are mathematically redundant: the relative-dimension version implies the unqualified version via Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.SmoothOfRelativeDimension.smooth}. Keeping both fields is a Lean-level convenience: \(\mathtt{smooth}\) is the form required by the typeclass binder of \texttt{IsAlbanese} (and by the protected \(\mathtt{Jacobian.instGrpObj}\) projection), whereas \(\mathtt{smoothGenus}\) is the form required by the protected \(\mathtt{Jacobian.smoothOfRelativeDimension\_genus}\) projection. The redundancy carries no mathematical cost (the two fields agree) and no soundness cost (deriving one from the other would only re-run the same Mathlib instance synthesis at the projection sites).
226	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:genus, def:JacobianWitness}
227	\end{remark}
228	\begin{theorem}\leanok
229	[Existence of an Albanese variety uniformly over the marked point]
230	  \label{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}
231	  
232	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.nonempty_jacobianWitness}
233	  % Cycle-fix (iter-149 plan-agent): drop the spurious 
234	  % from this statement-block. Mathematically `def:Jacobian` is the entity
235	  % that depends on this existence theorem (def:Jacobian's  lists
236	  % thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness), not the other way around. Listing
237	  % def:Jacobian here as a hypothesis of the existence theorem introduced
238	  % a 2-cycle in plastex's depgraph (def:Jacobian ↔ thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness)
239	  % that crashes `leanblueprint web` with RecursionError in depgraph.ancestors.
240	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Exercise ex:jac (generalized Jacobians of a family of curves form a smooth quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\), projective iff \(X/S\) smooth); arXiv:math/0504020, p.~50  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)
241	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Assume \(X/S\) is projective and flat, its geometric fibers
242	  % are integral curves of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.
243	  % Show the ``generalized Jacobians'' \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
244	  % quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\).  And show this
245	  % family is projective if and only if \(X/S\) is smooth."
246	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Exercise (ex:jac).}
247	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over a field \(k\). There exists a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(J\) over \(k\) of relative dimension \(g(C)\) such that, for every \(k\)-rational point \(P \in C(k)\), the scheme \(J\) is an Albanese object for the pointed curve \((C, P)\) in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese}. Equivalently, the type of Albanese witnesses for \(C\) (Definition~\ref{def:JacobianWitness}) is non-empty.
248	\end{theorem}
249	\begin{proof}
250	  The proof is split into three classical constructions plus the genus-\(0\) sub-case absorbed into the same witness (the latter packaged as the explicit construction \cref{def:genusZeroWitness}, which routes through the characteristic-free keystone \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} of \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} --- rigidity proved over the algebraic closure \(\bar k = \mathrm{AlgebraicClosure}\,k\) --- and then \emph{descends} the resulting morphism equality from \(\bar k\) to the base field \(k\) along the faithfully-flat surjection \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\)). Each route produces the same intrinsic scheme \(J\); only the universal morphism \(\iota_P \colon C \to J\) varies with the marked point \(P\). We outline each route and name the Mathlib infrastructure each one requires.
251	  \paragraph{(0) Reduction to a \(P\)-independent witness.}
252	  The witness \(J\) depends only on \(C\), not on the marked point \(P\). Indeed, both classical constructions below produce \(J\) as an intrinsic object of \(C\) (the identity component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\), or the Stein-factorisation target of \(\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}\) translated by a fixed \(P_0\)); a different choice of \(P\) in \(C(k)\) alters \(\iota_P\) by a translation of \(J\), which is an automorphism, and so does not change \(J\) as a scheme. The Lean encoding reflects this by bundling \(J\) as a single field and the universal-property data as \(\forall P,\, \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\, C\, P\, J\). We now turn to producing \(J\).
253	  \paragraph{Route A --- Picard scheme.}
254	  This route follows Hartshorne~III.4 and FGA Explained, Chapter~9.
255	  \begin{itemize}
256	    \item[\textbf{(A.1)}] \emph{The relative Picard functor.} For a \(k\)-scheme \(T\), write \(C_T := C \times_k T\) and let \(\pi \colon C_T \to T\) be the projection. The relative Picard functor is
257	    \[
258	      \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k} \colon (\Sch/k)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \Sets, \qquad
259	      T \longmapsto \Pic(C_T) / \pi^*\Pic(T).
260	    \]
261	    A \(T\)-point is a line bundle on \(C_T\) modulo a line bundle pulled back from \(T\). The set-valued formulation suffices for representability; the canonical abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(C_T)\) descends to the quotient by \(\pi^*\Pic(T)\) and gives \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}\) a natural group-functor refinement.
262	    \item[\textbf{(A.2)}] \emph{Representability.} For \(C\) smooth proper geometrically connected over \(k\) with a \(k\)-rational point, \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}\) (after \'etale sheafification, if \(k\) is not algebraically closed) is representable by a \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\), locally of finite type. This is FGA's main representability theorem (Grothendieck, FGA~232); the smooth-proper-geometrically-connected hypothesis ensures that the cohomology-and-base-change machinery applies and that \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = k\), which is needed for the quotient by \(\pi^*\Pic(T)\) to be \'etale-locally injective.
263	    \item[\textbf{(A.3)}] \emph{The identity component.} Let \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) be the identity component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\). As the identity component of a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type, \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is geometrically irreducible; for \(C\) smooth proper of genus \(g\), it is moreover a smooth proper \(k\)-group scheme of dimension \(g\). Smoothness comes from the deformation-theoretic identification of its tangent space at the identity with \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) and homogeneity of the group action; properness from the fact that the natural map \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\) is an open and closed immersion onto a proper component (a consequence of the cohomological criterion: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the kernel of the degree map \(\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb Z}_k\), which is locally constant). Take \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\).
264	    \item[\textbf{(A.4)}] \emph{The Abel--Jacobi universal morphism.} For each \(P \in C(k)\), the Abel--Jacobi morphism
265	    \[
266	      \iota_P \colon C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}, \qquad
267	      Q \longmapsto [\mathcal O_C(Q - P)]
268	    \]
269	    sends \(P\) to the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\). It is universal among morphisms \(f \colon C \to A\) to smooth proper geometrically irreducible \(k\)-group schemes \(A\) with \(f(P) = \eta_A\): this is the classical Albanese property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\), proved by reducing to the case \(k = \overline k\) (Galois descent), where it is a standard consequence of the autoduality of the Jacobian and the fact that line bundles on \(C \times A\) trivialised along \(\{P\} \times A\) correspond bijectively to morphisms \(C \to A^{\vee} = A\) sending \(P\) to \(\eta_A\).
270	  \end{itemize}
271	  \emph{Mathlib status for Route A.} Each sub-step is missing.
272	  \begin{itemize}
273	    \item Sub-step A.1 requires a working theory of relative line bundles on a product \(C \times_k T\) and the descent of the abelian-group structure on \(\Pic\) along \(\pi^*\). The pieces are present individually but not assembled. (See per-sub-phase budget below: \(\sim 700\)--\(1100\) LOC, \(\sim 6\)--\(10\) iters.)
274	    \item Sub-step A.2 requires FGA representability for \(\Pic_{C/k}\), which in turn requires Hilbert / Quot scheme infrastructure not in Mathlib (\texttt{HilbertScheme}, \texttt{QuotScheme}, and the cohomology-and-base-change for proper morphisms with finitely presented kernels). The construction engine for this Quot/Hilbert layer is Nitsure, ``Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes'' (FGA Explained; arXiv:math/0504590), \S\,4 (generic flatness and the flattening stratification) and \S\,5 (the construction of \(\mathrm{Quot}\), hence \(\mathrm{Hilb}\), as a scheme); see \texttt{references/nitsure-hilbert-quot.md}. (See per-sub-phase budget below: \(\sim 2200\)--\(3000\) LOC, \(\sim 15\)--\(25\) iters --- the dominant Route A block.)
275	    \item Sub-step A.3 requires connectedness machinery for group schemes locally of finite type, the identity-component construction \(G^0 \subseteq G\), and the degree map \(\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb Z}_k\). (See per-sub-phase budget below: \(\sim 600\)--\(900\) LOC, \(\sim 5\)--\(8\) iters.)
276	    \item Sub-step A.4 requires the universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\), which is the classical Albanese functoriality and is not in Mathlib in this form. \textbf{Bypass via Milne autoduality FAILS}: as Milne III \S~6 writes the proof of Proposition~6.1, it invokes Theorem~3.2 (rational-map-extension from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety) directly to upgrade the symmetric-product rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) to a regular map \(J \to A\), and the would-be Picard-functor / autoduality detour \(J \cong J^\vee\) routes through the theorem of the cube, excised from this project iter-163. Sub-step~A.4 therefore inherits the Theorem~3.2 + Lemma~3.3 codim-\(1\) extension + Weil-divisor API + Auslander--Buchsbaum sub-build (the last absent from Mathlib). (See iter-172 audit at \cref{sec:Jacobian_routeA4_albaneseUP}, NOTE block + \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property} proof.) (See per-sub-phase budget below: \(\sim 2500{+}\) LOC, \(\sim 22\)--\(35\) iters.)
277	  \end{itemize}
278	  \paragraph{Route A --- per-sub-phase LOC and iter budget.}
279	  The Route A build-out breaks into four sub-phases with the following cost
280	  estimates (LOC = lines of Lean to land axiom-clean; iters = prover iterations
281	  at the project's current \(\sim 80\)--\(100\) net-LOC-per-iter velocity on
282	  infrastructure material). The Mathlib-prerequisite cascade for each
283	  sub-phase is also recorded so subsequent planner decisions can sequence the
284	  upstream pieces.
285	  \begin{itemize}
286	    \item[(A.1)] \emph{Relative Picard functor + relative-line-bundles
287	      theory.} Net new project material: \(\sim 700\)--\(1100\) LOC. Iters:
288	      \(\sim 6\)--\(10\). The smallest standalone Mathlib entry point is the
289	      \texttt{RelativeSpec} functor (per the iter-123 audit
290	      \texttt{analogies/m3-route-audit.md}); the rest is line-bundle / \(\Pic\)
291	      machinery on a product \(C \times_k T\), which Mathlib has only partially
292	      in the form needed.
293	    \item[(A.2)] \emph{Hilbert / Quot scheme representability + FGA
294	      representability of \(\Pic_{C/k}\).} Net new project material:
295	      \(\sim 2200\)--\(3000\) LOC. Iters: \(\sim 15\)--\(25\). The dominant cost.
296	      The Quot/Hilbert construction engine is Nitsure \S 4--5
297	      (\texttt{references/nitsure-hilbert-quot.md}); the cohomology-and-base-change
298	      layer underneath is Stacks tag 02KH
299	      (\texttt{references/stacks-coherent.md}). The \'etale-sheafification
300	      step when \(k\) is not algebraically closed adds \(\sim 200\) LOC and
301	      requires Mathlib's \'etale-sheafification machinery (verified present:
302	      \texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Sheafification.lean} family,
303	      together with \texttt{Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Sites/Etale.lean}).
304	    \item[(A.3)] \emph{Identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) + degree map.}
305	      Net new project material: \(\sim 600\)--\(900\) LOC. Iters: \(\sim 5\)--\(8\).
306	      Connectedness machinery for group schemes locally of finite type; the
307	      identity component construction \(G^0 \subseteq G\); the degree map
308	      \(\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb Z}_k\) via the locally-constant
309	      pushforward (cf.\ \texttt{references/stacks-coherent.md}, base change of
310	      \(R^i f_*\)). Smoothness via the deformation-theoretic identification of
311	      the tangent space with \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) uses the cohomology layer
312	      of (A.2).
313	    \item[(A.4)] \emph{Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\).}
314	      Net new project material: \(\sim 2500{+}\) LOC. Iters: \(\sim 22\)--\(35\)

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:relpic_setup}
37	
38	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
39	\(k\). As in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, write
40	\(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) for the projection of a relative curve over a
41	\(k\)-scheme \(T\) and
42	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the induced pullback of
43	isomorphism classes of line bundles (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}).
44	
45	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} was set up forgetting the
50	abelian-group structure inherited from tensor product. This chapter restores
51	that structure and then sheafifies. The first half (\S~REF
52	and \S~REF) verifies that the quotient is canonically
53	an abelian group and the functoriality of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} respects
54	that group structure; the result is a group-valued presheaf
55	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) on the category of \(k\)-schemes. The
56	second half (\S~REF) takes the \'etale sheafification
57	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\) in the \'etale topology on
58	\((\Sch/k)^{op}\), and verifies that sheafification preserves the abelian-group
59	target. The \'etale-sheafified functor is the object represented by the Picard
60	scheme in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
61	
62	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
63	\label{sec:relpic_group}
64	
65	The Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
66	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of \([\mathcal{L}]\)
67	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
68	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
69	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of
70	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
71	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules sends \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)
72	and is multiplicative on tensor products.
73	
74	\begin{lemma}\leanok
75	[Group structure descends to the relative Picard quotient]
76	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}
78	  
79	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
80	  % Definition df:aPf + Definition df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard
81	  % functors are \emph{abelian-group}-valued, by construction; the relative
82	  % Picard functor is defined as a quotient \emph{group})
83	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
84	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
85	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
86	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
87	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
88	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
89	  %  [\dots]
90	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
91	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
92	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
93	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
94	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the Picard functors are abelian-group-valued, and the
95	  relative one is a quotient of abelian groups; cf.\ Stacks tag 01CR for the
96	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic\)).}
97	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve and let \(T\) be a
98	  \(k\)-scheme. The Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is an abelian group under
99	  tensor product of line bundles, and the pullback map
100	  \[
101	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
102	  \]
103	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a group homomorphism. Its image
104	  \[
105	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T)
106	  \]
107	  is therefore a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\), and the quotient set of
108	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
109	  \[
110	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T
111	  \]
112	  inherits a canonical abelian-group structure under which the quotient map
113	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjective
114	  homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
115	\end{lemma}
116	
117	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not supply a separate "proof" block
118	% for df:Pfs; the abelian-group structure is part of the definition (the word
119	% \emph{group} appears in df:aPf already, and df:Pfs is a quotient of the same
120	% abelian groups). The verbatim quotes for the statements above are therefore
121	% the same as for the lemma. The project-side proof is a structural restatement.
122	\begin{proof}
123	  
124	  The abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) under tensor product is
125	  standard (Stacks tag 01CR or Hartshorne II~\S 6): for line bundles
126	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}'\), the tensor product
127	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\) is again
128	  invertible, the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) is the unit, and
129	  the dual \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the
130	  inverse. Pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules sends
131	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) (the structure sheaf is
132	  preserved by inverse image) and satisfies
133	  $\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_T} \mathcal{N}') \cong
134	   \pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \pi_T^*\mathcal{N}'$
135	  by the standard tensor compatibility of pullback. Therefore \(\pi_T^*\)
136	  preserves the unit, sends inverses to inverses, and is multiplicative on
137	  tensor products; it is a homomorphism of abelian groups.
138	
139	  The image \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is then a subgroup. The quotient set
140	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) of
141	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries the canonical quotient
142	  abelian-group structure (Lagrange's theorem on cosets specialised to an
143	  abelian quotient): for \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}' \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\), set
144	  \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{L}'] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}']\);
145	  well-definedness follows from the subgroup condition. The canonical map
146	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is by construction a
147	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
148	\end{proof}
149	
150	\section{The relative Picard presheaf as a group-valued presheaf}
151	\label{sec:relpic_groupvalued}
152	
153	We now refine the set-valued functor of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} into a
154	group-valued presheaf.
155	
156	\begin{definition}\leanok
157	[Relative Picard presheaf]
158	  \label{def:rel_pic_sharp}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp}
160	  
161	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
162	  % Definition df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor)
163	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
164	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
165	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
166	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
167	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
168	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
169	  % topologies  by
170	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
171	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
172	  %  \end{dfn}"
173	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
174	  Def.~df:Pfs.}
175	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. The
176	  \emph{relative Picard presheaf} of \(C\) over \(k\) is the functor
177	  \[
178	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \Ab,
179	    \qquad
180	    T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T),
181	  \]
182	  where the right-hand side is the quotient abelian group of
183	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, and the structure morphisms are induced by
184	  the pullback maps \(g^\sharp\) of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} (which restrict
185	  to group homomorphisms on the quotient because the source square commutes
186	  with all pullback functors, see the proof below). In Kleiman's notation with
187	  \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\), this is exactly \(\Pic_{X/S}\) of df:Pfs.
188	\end{definition}
189	
190	\begin{lemma}\leanok
191	[Naturality respects the group structure]
192	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}
193	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.functorial}
194	  
195	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
196	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor is functorial
197	  % into abelian groups; the word \emph{functor} into the category of abelian
198	  % groups encodes both the identity / composition laws and the
199	  % group-homomorphism property of the restriction maps)
200	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
201	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
202	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
203	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
204	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
205	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
206	  %  [\dots]
207	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
208	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
209	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
210	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
211	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
212	  groups; the structure maps are group homomorphisms).}
213	  For a morphism \(g : T' \to T\) of \(k\)-schemes, the induced set map
214	  \(g^\sharp : \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T')\) of
215	  \cref{thm:pullback_natural} is a homomorphism of abelian groups with respect
216	  to the structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, and the identity and
217	  composition laws \(\mathrm{id}^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) and
218	  \((g \circ h)^\sharp = h^\sharp \circ g^\sharp\) hold as identities of group
219	  homomorphisms.
220	\end{lemma}
221	
222	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not write out a separate proof for
223	% functoriality; the structural assertion is part of the definition. No
224	% verbatim proof body is available in the source; the project-side argument
225	% is a direct unfolding of the pullback of modules.
226	\begin{proof}
227	  
228	  By \cref{thm:pullback_natural}, the set map \(g^\sharp\) is defined by
229	  \([\mathcal{L}] \mapsto [g_C^* \mathcal{L}]\), where
230	  \(g_C := \mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\). Pullback
231	  of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(g_C\) preserves tensor products and the
232	  structure sheaf, hence the map
233	  \(g_C^* : \Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T')\) on Picard groups is
234	  itself an abelian-group homomorphism. By
235	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, the quotient map
236	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a group homomorphism, and
237	  \(g_C^*\) takes \(H_T\) into \(H_{T'}\) by \cref{lem:pullback_compose}; therefore
238	  \(g^\sharp\) is a group homomorphism on the quotient. The identity and
239	  composition laws are inherited from \(\mathrm{id}^* = \mathrm{id}\) and
240	  \((g \circ h)^* = h^* \circ g^*\) on \(\mathcal{O}\)-module pullback.
241	\end{proof}
242	
243	\begin{theorem}\leanok
244	[\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) is a group-valued presheaf]
245	  \label{thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf}
246	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.presheaf}
247	  
248	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
249	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (relative Picard functor as a functor into
250	  % abelian groups)
251	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
252	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
253	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
254	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
255	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
256	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
257	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
258	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.}
259	  The assignment \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) of \cref{def:rel_pic_sharp},
260	  together with the structure maps \(g \mapsto g^\sharp\) of
261	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}, defines a presheaf of abelian groups
262	  \[
263	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\;
264	                                     \AddCommGroup
265	  \]
266	  on the category of \(k\)-schemes.
267	\end{theorem}
268	
269	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with the prior theorems, Kleiman \S 2 does not supply
270	% a separate proof body --- the presheaf-of-abelian-groups property is part of
271	% the definition df:aPf + df:Pfs. The project-side proof assembles the lemmas
272	% above; no external proof is being translated.
273	\begin{proof}
274	  
275	  The object map is well defined by
276	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}: for each \(k\)-scheme \(T\), the value
277	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is an abelian group. The morphism map
278	  \(g \mapsto g^\sharp\) is a group homomorphism by
279	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}, and satisfies the identity and
280	  composition laws by the same lemma. These data assemble into a functor
281	  \((\Sch/k)^{op} \to \AddCommGroup\), i.e.\ a presheaf of abelian groups on
282	  \((\Sch/k)\).
283	\end{proof}
284	
285	\section{\'Etale sheafification}
286	\label{sec:relpic_etale_sheaf}
287	
288	The presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) is generally not an \'etale (or even Zariski)
289	sheaf. The standard obstruction (Kleiman \S 2, L1292--L1302): for a \(k\)-scheme
290	\(T\) carrying a non-trivial line bundle whose pullback to \(C \times_k T\) becomes
291	non-trivial after an open cover, the Zariski separated-presheaf condition
292	fails. Therefore, to obtain a representable functor in the sense of
293	\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}, one must replace
294	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) by its \emph{associated \'etale sheaf}. Kleiman \S 2 names
295	this sheaf \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) and singles it out as the functor that Kleiman's
296	main existence theorem \S 4 (th:main) represents.
297	
298	\begin{definition}\leanok
299	[\'Etale sheafification of the relative Picard presheaf]
300	  \label{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification}
301	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheaf}
302	  % NOTE (iter-176 review): the iter-176 Lane G prover renamed this declaration
303	  % from ``AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme`` to
304	  % ``AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheaf`` to avoid a name collision with
305	  % ``Picard_FGAPicRepresentability`` (which legitimately uses ``PicScheme``
306	  % for the REPRESENTING SCHEME, a different mathematical object). The
307	  % ``\lean{...}`` pin is updated accordingly. The §"Lean encoding" bullet 5
308	  % below is also corrected.
309	  
310	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
311	  % Definition df:Pfs --- the second half of df:Pfs names the \'etale sheaf
312	  % associated to the relative Picard functor
313	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318
314	  %  and the surrounding paragraph L1320-L1328).
315	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
316	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
317	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
318	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
319	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
320	  % topologies  by
321	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
322	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
323	  %  \end{dfn}
324	  %  [\dots]
325	  %  if the latter functor is one of the three just displayed,
326	  %  then it is a sheaf in the indicated topology; in fact, it is the sheaf
327	  %  associated to any one of its predecessors, and the map between them is
328	  %  the natural map from a presheaf to its associated sheaf, as is easy to
329	  %  check.  In particular, the three displayed sheaves are the sheaves
330	  %  associated to the absolute Picard functor \(\Pic_X\), as well as to the
331	  %  relative Picard functor \(\Pic_{X/S}\)."
332	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
333	  Def.~df:Pfs (the \'etale-sheaf notation \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\)) and the
334	  associated-sheaf paragraph immediately following df:Pfs.}
335	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. The
336	  \emph{\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor} is the sheafification of
337	  the presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf}) in the
338	  global \'etale topology on \((\Sch/k)\):
339	  \[
340	    \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \;\;:=\;\;
341	       \bigl(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\bigr)^{\sim_{\et}}.
342	  \]
343	  Equivalently, \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is the unique (up to canonical
344	  isomorphism) sheaf of abelian groups on the \'etale site of \(\Sch/k\)
345	  equipped with a presheaf morphism $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to
346	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ which is universal among presheaf morphisms from
347	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) to \'etale sheaves of abelian groups. In Kleiman's
348	  notation this is \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) with \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\). When \(k\)
349	  is algebraically closed and \(T = \Spec k'\) for an algebraically closed field
350	  extension \(k'/k\), the natural map
351	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) is a bijection (Kleiman
352	  Exercise ex:Alr, L1357--L1361).
353	\end{definition}
354	
355	\begin{theorem}[\'Etale sheafification preserves the abelian-group structure]
356	  \label{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure}
357	  
358	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
359	  % Def.~df:Pfs --- the \'etale-sheafified functor \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) is named
360	  % alongside the abelian-group-valued \(\Pic_{X/S}\) in the same definition,
361	  % i.e.\ the sheafification lives in the same target category
362	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
363	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
364	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
365	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
366	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
367	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
368	  % topologies  by
369	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
370	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
371	  %  \end{dfn}"
372	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
373	  Def.~df:Pfs (the \'etale sheaf \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) is named together with the
374	  abelian-group-valued relative Picard functor, meaning the sheaf is taken in
375	  the category of abelian groups).}
376	  The \'etale sheafification
377	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) of \cref{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification} is a
378	  sheaf with values in the category of abelian groups: the canonical morphism
379	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is a homomorphism of group
380	  presheaves, and for each \(k\)-scheme \(T\) the value
381	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) is naturally an abelian group whose structure
382	  maps make \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) a presheaf
383	  \((\Sch/k)^{op} \to \AddCommGroup\).
384	\end{theorem}
385	
386	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not supply a self-contained proof of
387	% this auxiliary statement --- it is the standard fact that the sheafification
388	% of a presheaf of abelian groups in a Grothendieck topology is again a
389	% presheaf of abelian groups (equivalently, the forgetful functor
390	% \(\AddCommGroup \to \Set\) preserves filtered colimits, and the
391	% plus-construction is built from such colimits). The Lean side appeals to
392	% Mathlib's \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sheafification}\) machinery.
393	\begin{proof}
394	  
395	  The \'etale topology on \((\Sch/k)\) is a Grothendieck topology, and the
396	  forgetful functor \(\AddCommGroup \to \Set\) preserves and reflects the
397	  filtered colimits used by the plus-construction. Therefore the
398	  sheafification of a presheaf \(\mathcal{F} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \AddCommGroup\)
399	  in the underlying \'etale site agrees, after the forgetful functor to sets,
400	  with the sheafification of the underlying set-valued presheaf
401	  \(U \circ \mathcal{F}\); equivalently, the sheaf
402	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} = (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^{\sim_\et}\) is canonically a
403	  sheaf of abelian groups, the canonical morphism
404	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is a homomorphism of group
405	  presheaves, and the restriction maps are group homomorphisms. This is a
406	  standard consequence of the plus-construction with values in an algebraic
407	  category; on the Lean side it is the Mathlib pattern
408	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sheafification}\) applied with target category
409	  \(\AddCommGroup\).
410	\end{proof}

412	\section{Lean encoding}
413	\label{sec:relpic_lean_encoding}
414	
415	The Lean target file is the new
416	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean}, sibling to
417	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean}
418	(\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}) and
419	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}
420	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}). The blueprint declarations correspond to
421	Lean targets as follows.
422	\begin{itemize}
423	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}
424	    (\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}) is the abelian-group instance on the
425	    quotient set built in
426	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage\_subgroup}
427	    (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}). Mathlib's
428	    \texttt{QuotientAddGroup} machinery on a normal subgroup of an abelian
429	    group is the backbone; the project-side work is to certify that
430	    \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism (one extra
431	    \texttt{LinearMap.map\_mul}--style line on tensor product preservation).
432	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp}
433	    (\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}) is the group-valued presheaf
434	    \(T \mapsto \Pic(C \times_k T)/\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\). The Lean form is a functor
435	    \((\Sch/k)^{op} \to \AddCommGroup\) built from the data of
436	    \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} +
437	    \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
438	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.functorial}
439	    (\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}) packages the structure-map
440	    homomorphism property: the set map \(g^\sharp\) of
441	    \cref{thm:pullback_natural} is a group homomorphism. On the Lean side
442	    this is a one-step strengthening of the existing set-valued naturality
443	    proof.
444	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.presheaf}
445	    (\cref{thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf}) wraps the previous three lemmas as a
446	    functor instance into \(\AddCommGroup\).
447	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheaf}
448	    % NOTE (iter-176 review): renamed from ``AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme``
449	    % to avoid a collision with the representing-scheme declaration of the same
450	    % name in ``Picard_FGAPicRepresentability``.
451	    (\cref{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification}) is the \'etale sheafification
452	    of \texttt{PicSharp}. The Lean form is
453	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology.Sheafify} applied to the
454	    \'etale topology on \(\Sch/k\) with target category \(\AddCommGroup\).
455	    Mathlib provides the abelian-group-valued sheafification API
456	    (\texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheafification}) for any concrete sheafifiable
457	    presheaf and any target with the required filtered-colimit + finite-limit
458	    structure; \cref{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure} reduces the
459	    statement to that pattern. \textbf{Verification flag.} The exact Mathlib
460	    declaration name and the input-shape of the \'etale-Grothendieck-topology
461	    instance on \(\Sch/k\) are not pinned in this chapter; the prover should
462	    consult the current Mathlib snapshot (or open a follow-up if the API is
463	    not yet wired to \(\AddCommGroup\)). If the abelian-group-valued
464	    sheafification is absent from Mathlib at the pinned commit, the
465	    project-side sub-build is to combine the set-valued sheafification with
466	    Mathlib's \texttt{AddCommGroup} forgetful functor.
467	\end{itemize}
468	
469	The relationship with \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability} is captured by
470	the slogan: the FGA Picard scheme of A.2.c represents
471	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (Kleiman \S 4, th:main: ``\dots represents
472	\(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\)''), \emph{not} \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) directly. The \'etale
473	sheafification step of this chapter is precisely what makes the
474	representability theorem in A.2.c well-posed.

392	  \paragraph{Route B --- Symmetric powers and Stein factorisation (historical alternative; not pursued).}
393	  \textbf{Iter-144 disposition.} Route B is a historical alternative \emph{not pursued} by the project. It is preserved here for scholarly context only. Route A --- Picard scheme via FGA --- is the committed M3 route per STRATEGY.md \S~M3 iter-144 disposition. The decomposition, gating Mathlib pieces, and midpoint LOC figure (\(\sim 9000\) LOC) recorded below are the iter-123 audit values (\texttt{analogies/m3-route-audit.md}); the chapter does not re-cost Route B under current Mathlib snapshots.
394	  This route follows Milne's \emph{Abelian Varieties} (Chapter~III) and avoids FGA representability at the cost of requiring quotients of schemes by finite group actions.
395	  \begin{itemize}
396	    \item[\textbf{(B.1)}] \emph{The \(g\)-fold symmetric power.} Form the product \(C^g\) and let \(S_g\) act by permutation of the factors. The categorical quotient \(C^{(g)} := C^g / S_g\) exists as a \(k\)-scheme, smooth and proper over \(k\) of dimension \(g\). Smoothness follows because the action of \(S_g\) on the smooth \(k\)-scheme \(C^g\) is free outside the diagonal locus and the local-ring computation at the diagonal points produces a smooth ring (this is the symmetric-product computation of Fogarty / Mumford). Properness descends from properness of \(C^g\) via the geometric-quotient property.
397	    \item[\textbf{(B.2)}] \emph{The Abel--Jacobi morphism on symmetric powers.} Fix a \(k\)-rational point \(P_0 \in C(k)\). The map
398	    \[
399	      \sigma \colon C^{(g)} \longrightarrow \Pic^g_{C/k}, \qquad
400	      \sum_{i=1}^g [Q_i] \longmapsto \mathcal O_C\bigl(\textstyle\sum_{i=1}^g Q_i\bigr)
401	    \]
402	    is well-defined (the right-hand side is symmetric in the \(Q_i\) and depends only on the unordered tuple) and is surjective. By Brill--Noether--Riemann--Roch, \(\sigma\) is birational onto its image: a generic divisor of degree \(g\) on \(C\) has \(h^0(\mathcal O_C(D)) = 1\), so the fibre \(\sigma^{-1}([\mathcal O_C(D)])\) is a point at generic divisors; the special fibres are projective spaces \(\mathbb P(h^0 - 1)\) over the locus where \(h^0 > 1\).
403	    \item[\textbf{(B.3)}] \emph{The Stein factorisation.} Because \(\sigma\) is a proper surjection with geometrically connected fibres (each fibre \(\mathbb P^{h^0-1}_k\) is geometrically irreducible), its Stein factorisation
404	    \[
405	      C^{(g)} \xrightarrow{\;\sigma_*\;} \Spec_{\Pic^g_{C/k}}\bigl(\sigma_* \mathcal O_{C^{(g)}}\bigr) \longrightarrow \Pic^g_{C/k}
406	    \]
407	    presents the second arrow as a finite morphism whose source is the intermediate scheme. Since the fibres of \(\sigma\) are connected, this intermediate scheme equals \(\Pic^g_{C/k}\) scheme-theoretically (the Stein factorisation is trivial on the second arrow when fibres are connected, by Stein's classical theorem). The middle scheme is therefore \(\Pic^g_{C/k}\) itself, which is smooth proper geometrically irreducible of dimension \(g\). Translating by \(-g P_0\) yields \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\), and we take \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as in Route A. The universal property is then established as in Sub-step A.4.
408	  \end{itemize}
