142	Piece (ii) (scheme-level \(\mathrm df = 0 \Rightarrow f\) factors through \(\Spec k\), the C.2.d / M2.a body consumer of piece (i)'s cotangent-trivial output) is the load-bearing body under the iter-144 chart-algebra pivot. Total envelope \textasciitilde\(600\)--\(1050\) LOC, decomposed as:
143	\begin{itemize}
144	  \item Chart-algebra (\(\alpha\)) \texttt{Algebra.IsPushout}-from-affine-product helper: \textasciitilde\(80\)--\(150\) LOC. Constructs the \texttt{Algebra.IsPushout} instance on the chart-level ring extensions \(\Gamma(\Spec k, U) \to \Gamma(W, \mathcal O_G)\) for affine charts \(V \to W\) in the product \(G \otimes G\). Built from \texttt{pullbackSpecIso} and the \texttt{\_hom\_fst} / \texttt{\_inv\_fst} simp companions in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Pullbacks}, chained with \texttt{CommRingCat.isPushout\_iff\_isPushout} and \texttt{isPullback\_SpecMap\_of\_isPushout} (cf.\ \cref{lem:GrpObj_omega_basechange_proj} Route (a) item 1).
145	  \item Chart-algebra (\(\beta\)) per-chart translation-invariance Kähler-derivation argument: \textasciitilde\(150\)--\(300\) LOC. The load-bearing core of M2.a's \(\mathrm df = 0\) derivation chain (per STRATEGY.md \S "Soundness rules: M2.a \(\mathrm df = 0\) derivation chain --- articulated (added iter-144)"). The three-layer chain is:
146	    \begin{enumerate}
147	      \item \textbf{Chart-local layer.} On each affine chart \(W = \Spec B \subseteq A.\mathrm{left}\) with affine \(V \subseteq C.\mathrm{left}\) such that \(f\) restricts to a ring map \(f^{\sharp} \colon B \to \Gamma(C, V)\), the differential \(\mathrm df = 0\) at the chart level means the composite \(\Omega_{B/k} \to \Omega_{\Gamma(C, V)/k}\) obtained from the universal Kähler derivation \(\mathrm d \colon B \to \Omega_{B/k}\) post-composed with \(f^{\sharp}\) vanishes. Combined with piece (i)'s chart-level free-of-rank-\(n\) presentation of \(\Omega_{B/k}\) (\texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free\_kaehlerDifferential}), the kernel-extraction step gives \(f^{\sharp}(b) \in \Gamma(C, V)^{p^{e}}\) for every \(b \in B\), where \(p = \operatorname{char} k\) and \(e \geq 0\) is a chart-uniform Frobenius depth.
148	      \item \textbf{Char-\(p\) Frobenius-iteration layer.} The chart-local conclusion \(f^{\sharp}(b) \in \Gamma(C, V)^{p^{e}}\) is promoted to "for every \(b \in B\), there exists \(e\) with \(f^{\sharp}(b) = \mathrm{iterateFrobenius}_{p, e}(c_{b})\) for some \(c_{b} \in \Gamma(C, V)\)" via \texttt{RingHom.iterateFrobenius\_comm} (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.CharP.Frobenius}, verified iter-144). The uniform-\(e\)-on-\(W\) glue extracts a globally-bounded Frobenius depth on \(C\) from the smoothness of \(A\) at the image of \(f\) (using \(A\)'s tangent-bundle triviality at each point in the image of \(f\) to control the depth uniformly across \(W\)). In characteristic \(0\) this layer is vacuous: \(\mathrm df = 0 \Rightarrow f^{\sharp}(b) \in k \subseteq \Gamma(C, V)\) directly via the standard derivation-kernel argument (no Frobenius).
149	      \item \textbf{Constancy-from-\(\mathrm df = 0\) layer (the converse only).} \emph{Iter-155 correction.} This layer was previously titled the ``No-Serre-duality layer'' and claimed the chart-algebra (\(\beta\)) chain ``replaces'' the \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/k}) = 0\) route, ``eliminating piece (iv) as a critical-path dependency''. \textbf{That claim is false} (per \texttt{analogies/df-zero-production-iter155.md}) and is corrected here. What the chain actually delivers is the \emph{converse} implication: \textbf{given} the chart-uniform input \(f^{\sharp}(b) \in \Gamma(C, V)^{p^{e}}\) (which is the chart-level shadow of the hypothesis \(\mathrm df = 0\)), the conclusion ``\(f\) factors through \(\Spec k\)'' follows from the integrally-closed-constants helper (next item) and the scheme-level lift via \texttt{Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_eqOnOpen} (final item). It does \emph{not} produce the hypothesis \(\mathrm df = 0\), and it does not avoid the \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/k}) = 0\) input: that input is exactly what is needed to \emph{establish} \(\mathrm df = 0\) in the first place. Concretely, \(\mathrm df = 0\) is a global-sections fact --- with the gap-(i) trivialisation \(f^{*}\Omega_{A/k} \cong \mathcal O_C^{\oplus g}\), \(\mathrm df \in \bigl(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/k})\bigr)^{\oplus g}\), so \(\mathrm df = 0 \iff H^0(C, \Omega_{C/k}) = 0\) --- and the chart-by-chart machinery cannot detect it because \(\Omega_{C/k}(V)\) is free of rank \(1\) (nonzero) on every affine chart \(V\). Hence piece (iv) (or an equivalent yielding \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/k}) = 0\)) is \textbf{on} the critical path for the keystone, not eliminated by this chain.
150	    \end{enumerate}
151	  \item Algebra-level core \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.mem\_range\_algebraMap\_of\_D\_eq\_zero}: \textasciitilde\(200\)--\(350\) LOC. Mathlib-shaped helper for the ring-level half of \(\mathrm df = 0 \Rightarrow f^{\sharp}\) factors through \(k\), packaged as the statement "for \(D \colon R \to M\) the universal Kähler derivation with \(D \circ f^{\sharp} = 0\), the image of \(f^{\sharp}\) lies in the integral closure of the image of the base ring". This is the chart-uniform core consumed by layer (1) of the \(\beta\)-chain.
152	  \item Integrally-closed-constants helper: \textasciitilde\(50\)--\(100\) LOC. The wrapper around "constants in \(C\) are integral over \(k\) for \(C\) smooth proper geometrically irreducible", used to promote the chart-uniform Frobenius-depth conclusion to a global \(\Spec k\)-factorisation.
153	  \item Scheme-level lift via \texttt{Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_eqOnOpen}: \textasciitilde\(100\)--\(150\) LOC. Closes the argument by exhibiting a non-empty open \(U \subseteq C\) on which \(f|_{U}\) equals the constant morphism at the image of \(f\)'s chart-uniform output, then applies the iter-125-refactored \cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_eqOnOpen}) to globalise.
154	\end{itemize}
155	
156	The chart-algebra route replaces the bundled scheme-level absolute-Frobenius construction (piece (iii) PHANTOM) with chart-uniform ring-level idioms already in Mathlib snapshot \texttt{b80f227}. \textbf{It does not, however, replace the Serre-duality cohomology vanishing (piece (iv))} --- the iter-155 correction above and in \cref{subsec:RigidityKbar_piece_i_decomposition} retracts that earlier claim. The chart-algebra (\(\beta\)) chain is the \emph{converse} ``\(\mathrm df = 0 \Rightarrow\) constant''; producing \(\mathrm df = 0\) remains a global-sections fact gated on \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/k}) = 0\) (piece (iv)) plus the gap-(i) trivialisation. The Lean target name for the scheme-level converse half is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_diff\_zero} (per the iter-126 analogist's idiom alignment); see \cref{lem:Scheme_Over_ext_of_diff_zero} for the present thin-wrapper Lean decl versus the gated substantive \(\mathrm df = \mathrm dg\) refinement.
157	
158	\paragraph{Iter-128 / iter-129 prover lane re-scoping (per \texttt{strategy-critic-iter128} + iter-129 rename).} Sub-step (i.a) was originally a 2-target pair and is now (post-iter-129) a 3-target trio: \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace} (the \emph{definition} of \(\mathfrak g^\vee\), plus the finitely-generated-free claim — iter-128 lane, body landed), \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangent_bridge} (the \emph{bridge} to the local-ring cotangent space \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^{2}\) — iter-130+ build target), and \cref{lem:GrpObj_lieAlgebra_finrank} (the \emph{rank-equals-relative-dimension} claim — iter-130+ build target). The iter-128 prover lane attacks ONLY the first: it scaffolds the Lean declaration \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.cotangentSpaceAtIdentity} (renamed iter-129 from the placeholder \texttt{lieAlgebra}; signature relaxed to a free \texttt{\{n : \mathbb N\} [SmoothOfRelativeDimension n G.hom]} binder so downstream consumers of arbitrary relative dimension can instantiate it) and closes its body. The bridge lemma \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangent_bridge} is vestigial on the live path under Replacement (B) (see \S Q4 collapse footer at the rank-lemma proof's end) and remains deferred indefinitely; the rank lemma \cref{lem:GrpObj_lieAlgebra_finrank} is the iter-132 prover-lane target (closure path: Step 1 chart-side Kähler rank from \cref{thm:smooth_locally_free_omega}'s existential + Step 2 base-change to \(k\) via \texttt{Module.finrank\_baseChange}, both detailed in that lemma's proof body). Rationale: (i) bundling definition + bridge + rank into one prover-iter task is borderline-too-ambitious per \texttt{progress-critic-iter128}; (ii) the rank-lemma RHS Lean encoding needs blueprint pinning iter-129 before being prover-ready (it must read \texttt{Module.finrank k (cotangentSpaceAtIdentity G) = n} parameterised by an explicit \texttt{[SmoothOfRelativeDimension n G.hom]} instance; the bare \(\dim G\) in the prose does not type-check directly because Mathlib has no \(\dim G\) function on \texttt{Over (Spec k)}, per \texttt{strategy-critic-iter128} CHALLENGE on (i.a) signature ambiguity).
159	
160	Throughout this subsection, \(k\) is an arbitrary field and \(G : \Over\,(\Spec k)\) is a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over \(k\), with identity section \(\eta_G : \mathbf{1} \to G\).
161	
162	\begin{lemma}\leanok
163	[(i.a) Cotangent space at the identity of a group scheme]
164	  \label{lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace}
165	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.cotangentSpaceAtIdentity}
166	  % Lean signature stub (pinned post-iter-129 fixup; relaxed from the
167	  % iter-128 hardcoded rel-dim-1 form so the same declaration serves
168	  % consumers of arbitrary relative dimension, e.g. \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}
169	  % applied to an abelian variety of dimension \(g\)):
170	  %   noncomputable def cotangentSpaceAtIdentity (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
171	  %       [CategoryTheory.GrpObj G] {n : ℕ} [SmoothOfRelativeDimension n G.hom]
172	  %       [IsProper G.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible G.hom] : ModuleCat k
173	  The pullback along the identity section \(\eta_G\) of the relative cotangent presheaf,
174	  \[
175	    \mathfrak g^{\vee} \;:=\; \eta_G^{*}\,\Omega_{G/k},
176	  \]
177	  is a finitely generated free \(k\)-module. We call \(\mathfrak g^{\vee}\) the \emph{cotangent space of \(G\) at the identity}. The \emph{Lie algebra of \(G\)} as a \(k\)-vector space is recovered as the \(k\)-linear dual \(\mathfrak g := \mathfrak g^{\vee\vee} = \mathrm{Hom}_{k}(\mathfrak g^{\vee}, k)\) (the bracket structure is not used in the rigidity argument and is not packaged here).
178	
179	  \emph{Lean encoding note (iter-129 dualisation pinning).} The Lean declaration \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.cotangentSpaceAtIdentity} returns a \texttt{ModuleCat k} which is the \emph{un-dualised} cotangent \(\mathfrak g^{\vee}\) — the direct output of the pullback-along-section construction described in the proof, with no internal \texttt{Module.Dual} hop. Downstream consumers that need the tangent \(\mathfrak g\) take \texttt{Module.Dual k (cotangentSpaceAtIdentity G)} externally. This convention pins what was left open in the iter-128 encoding note: the rigidity argument is symmetric under dualisation (only the finite-rank-free claim is consumed), so the un-dualised form is preferred as it matches the body construction without an extra contravariant hop. The declaration embeds into Mathlib's \texttt{ModuleCat} infrastructure and supplies the \texttt{Module.Free k} / \texttt{Module.Finite k} typeclasses needed for the rank lemma \cref{lem:GrpObj_lieAlgebra_finrank} via the bridge lemma \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangent_bridge}.
180	\end{lemma}
181	
182	\begin{proof}
183	
184	\leanok
185	  The identity section \(\eta_G\) is a canonical \(k\)-rational point of \(G\) supplied by the \(\GrpObj\) structure. The iter-131 Lean construction (Replacement (B), pure-term \texttt{noncomputable def}) realises \(\mathfrak g^{\vee} = \eta_G^{*}\,\Omega_{G/k}\) as a chart-base-changed Kähler module: by the forward Jacobian criterion \cref{thm:smooth_locally_free_omega} (already in the tree as \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.smooth\_locally\_free\_omega} in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Differentials.lean}), there exists an affine chart \(V \subseteq G\) around the image of the identity section \(\eta_G(\mathrm{pt})\), with affine base chart \(U \subseteq \Spec k\) and the structural ring map \(\Gamma(\Spec k, U) \to \Gamma(G, V)\) exhibiting \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension} \(n\) (and consequently \(\Omega_{\Gamma(G,V) \,/\, \Gamma(\Spec k,\,U)}\) is a free \(\Gamma(G, V)\)-module of rank \(n\) by \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank\_kaehlerDifferential} in \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmoothCotangent}). The cotangent space at the identity is then defined as the base change to \(k\) of this algebraic Kähler module along the ring map \(\psi_V \colon \Gamma(G, V) \to k\) obtained by restricting the identity section \(\eta_G\) to \(V\) and composing with the canonical isomorphism \(\Gamma(\Spec k, \cdot) \cong k\):
186	  \[
187	    \mathfrak g^{\vee} \;\;:=\;\; k \,\otimes_{\Gamma(G,\,V)}\, \Omega_{\Gamma(G,\,V) \,/\, \Gamma(\Spec k,\,U)}.
188	  \]
189	  This is a finitely generated free \(k\)-module of rank \(n\) by base-change of a free module along \(\psi_V\) (the rank is preserved; see \cref{lem:GrpObj_lieAlgebra_finrank}).
190	
191	  \emph{Caveat on canonicity.} The construction depends on a chart choice \(V\) extracted via a \texttt{Classical.choose}-chain from the existential statement of \texttt{smooth\_locally\_free\_omega}; it is therefore not canonically attached to \(G\) in the strictest sense. (The iter-131 fix-up refactored what was previously a top-level \texttt{Classical.choice}-wrapper into a pure-term \texttt{let}-chain of \texttt{Classical.choose} / \texttt{Classical.choose\_spec} accessors, exposing the outer head symbol \texttt{(ModuleCat.extendScalars\,\(\psi_V\).hom).obj\,(ModuleCat.of\,\(\Gamma\)(G, V)\,\(\Omega\)[\(\Gamma\)(G, V)\,/\,\(\Gamma\)(Spec k, U)])} to downstream consumers; see \S "Iter-131 \texttt{Classical.choose}-chain body shape" below for the Lean encoding details, and the companion \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace_extendScalars_witness} for the structural-shape rewrite handle.) For the live consumer (rigidity over \(k\), via \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}), the existence of a rank-\(n\) free \(k\)-module is the only structural content needed, so canonicity is not load-bearing. A canonical (chart-independent) presentation via the stalk-side cotangent \(\mathfrak m_{\eta_G}/\mathfrak m_{\eta_G}^{2}\) is available as a future bridge (\cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangent_bridge} below, currently \texttt{\textbackslash notready}) at additional cost; the project defers that bridge until a non-rigidity consumer materialises that requires canonicity.
192	\end{proof}
193

195	[Structural-shape rewrite handle for the cotangent space at the identity]
196	  \label{lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace_extendScalars_witness}

1	\chapter{Milne Theorem 3.2: Rational maps into abelian varieties}
2	\label{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.c sub-row of Route A
6	% (the positive-genus arm of \texttt{nonempty\_jacobianWitness}). It is the
7	% join of the two preceding sub-rows: A.4.a, the codim-1 indeterminacy /
8	% codim-\(\geq 2\) extension package (\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}),
9	% and A.4.b, the Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary
10	% ``regular local \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay''
11	% (\cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}). The single output of this
12	% chapter is Milne's Theorem~3.2 of Chapter~I~\S 3 of \emph{Abelian Varieties}:
13	% \emph{every rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety
14	% extends, uniquely, to a regular morphism}. This is the input that A.4.d
15	% (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}) consumes to upgrade the symmetric-product
16	% rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) to a regular morphism \(J \to A\),
17	% which in turn yields the Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
18	% (Milne III~\S 6, Proposition~6.1).
19	%
20	% This chapter is also the canonical home of the Lean target
21	% \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_to\_av}. A placeholder
22	% lemma block \texttt{lem:rational\_map\_to\_av\_extends} was reserved
23	% earlier in \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} (under the alternative Lean
24	% name \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalMap\_to\_av\_extends}); a Plan-Agent
25	% reconciliation pass should retarget that reservation at the theorem proved
26	% here (see "Notes for Plan Agent" in the writer report).
27	%

29	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
30	% creating it.
31	
32	\section{Setup and statement}
33	\label{sec:thm32_setup}
34	
35	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), a smooth variety \(X\) over \(\bar k\) (in the
36	project's convention: a geometrically integral, geometrically reduced, separated scheme of
37	finite type over \(\bar k\) that is moreover smooth, hence nonsingular at every closed point),
38	and an abelian variety \(A\) over \(\bar k\) (in particular \(A\) is complete, smooth, and a
39	group object in \(\mathrm{Sch}/\bar k\)). A rational map
40	\(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) is, in the project's notation, the equivalence class of a
41	regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to A\) defined on a dense open subset
42	\(U \subseteq X\), two such pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\) and \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) being equivalent
43	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\).
44	
45	The result of this chapter is:
46	
47	\begin{theorem}
48	
49	\leanok
50	[Milne Theorem~3.2: a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extends]
51	  \label{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}
52	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_to_av}
53	  
54	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, doc p.~17
55	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
56	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
57	  % "THEOREM 3.2. A rational map \(\alpha\colon V--\to A\) from a nonsingular
58	  %  variety to an abelian variety is defined on the whole of \(V\)."
59	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, p.~17.}
60	
61	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), let \(A\) be
62	  an abelian variety over \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) be a rational
63	  map. Then \(f\) is defined on the whole of \(X\): there is a unique regular morphism
64	  \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\) whose restriction to some (equivalently, every) dense open
65	  set of definition \(U \subseteq X\) of \(f\) agrees with \(\varphi_U\).
66	\end{theorem}
67	
68	The uniqueness clause is immediate from \(X\) being reduced and separated: two regular
69	morphisms \(X \to A\) that agree on a dense open subset agree everywhere. The substance is
70	existence of the extension. Milne deduces it in one line by combining the codim-\(\geq 2\)
71	extension result for rational maps to a complete variety with the codim-1 indeterminacy
72	structure result for rational maps to a group variety:
73	
74	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
75	% "PROOF. Combine Theorem 3.1 with the next lemma."
76	\begin{proof}
77	  
78	  Milne's one-line proof is: \emph{combine Theorem~3.1 with Lemma~3.3}.
79	  Theorem~3.1 (the codim-\(\geq 2\) extension theorem for a rational map from a normal
80	  variety to a complete variety; valuative criterion of properness) is
81	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} of the present project; Lemma~3.3 (the
82	  pure-codimension-\(1\) structure of the indeterminacy locus of a rational map into a
83	  group variety) is \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} of the same chapter.
84	
85	  Concretely: let \(U \subseteq X\) be a maximal open set of definition of \(f\), and let
86	  \(Z := X \setminus U\) be its complement.
87	  \begin{enumerate}
88	    \item By \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} (Milne~3.1, the valuative-criterion extension
89	      to codim \(\geq 2\)), applied with the normal variety \(X\) and the complete variety
90	      \(A\), we have \(\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2\).
91	    \item By \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} (Milne~3.3, the pure-codim-\(1\)
92	      indeterminacy structure for maps into the group variety \(A\)), the closed subset
93	      \(Z\) is either empty or of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X\).
94	    \item Both conditions together force \(Z = \emptyset\): a non-empty pure-codimension-\(1\)
95	      closed subset has codimension exactly \(1\), contradicting \(\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2\).
96	  \end{enumerate}
97	  Therefore \(U = X\) and \(f\) admits a regular representative on all of \(X\), which is the
98	  required regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\). Uniqueness is the standard
99	  reduced-and-separated agreement principle (see the paragraph preceding the theorem).
100	\end{proof}
101	
102	\begin{remark}
103	  \label{rmk:thm32_role_of_ab}
104	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} The combination above relies on
105	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}, which is itself proved in
106	  \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} in two stages: a codim-\(1\) step
107	  (valuative criterion of properness, applied at the DVR
108	  \(\mathcal O_{X, Z}\) of each codimension-\(1\) point \(Z\) --- works because \(X\) is normal
109	  and \(A\) is complete) and a codim-\(\geq 2\) step (depth-\(\geq 2\) at codim-\(\geq 2\) points,
110	  giving the local-cohomology / Hartshorne~III.8 sheaf-extension property). The
111	  depth-\(\geq 2\) input on a smooth (regular) variety is exactly what
112	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies:
113	  for a regular local ring \((\mathcal O_{X, x}, \mathfrak m_x)\) of Krull dimension \(d\), the
114	  Auslander--Buchsbaum formula \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} (applied to \(M = R\)
115	  trivially, or to the depth-via-regular-sequence characterisation directly) gives
116	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = d\), so in particular
117	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2\) whenever \(\mathrm{codim}_X(\{x\}) \geq 2\).
118	  Concretely, when \(X\) is a smooth surface (e.g.\ \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) or
119	  \(C \times C\) for a smooth projective curve \(C\)), every closed point \(x\) is regular of
120	  dimension \(2\), so \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is Cohen--Macaulay of depth \(2\); sections of
121	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on \(U = X \setminus \{x\}\) extend uniquely across \(x\), and the same is
122	  true for affine-locally pulled-back sections of \(\mathcal O_A\) along \(f\).
123	
124	  Auslander--Buchsbaum therefore does not appear by name in the proof above; it appears
125	  inside \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s codim-\(\geq 2\) half. The dependency
126	  \(\) is recorded on
127	  \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} to keep the leanblueprint dependency graph
128	  faithful: A.4.c truly does sit on top of A.4.b through A.4.a.
129	\end{remark}
130	
131	\section{Why the proof is char-free}
132	\label{sec:thm32_char_free}
133	
134	Each of the two inputs is char-free:
135	\begin{itemize}
136	  \item \emph{Theorem~3.1 / codim-\(\geq 2\) extension} (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}):
137	    the proof uses only the valuative criterion of properness and depth-\(\geq 2\) extension
138	    of sections on regular local rings; both are characteristic-agnostic.
139	  \item \emph{Lemma~3.3 / pure-codim-\(1\) indeterminacy for maps into a group variety}
140	    (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}): Milne's proof forms the difference map
141	    \(\Phi(x, y) := \varphi(x)\,\varphi(y)^{-1} \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow A\) and
142	    examines its zero/pole divisor along the diagonal --- a Weil-divisor argument that
143	    works for any algebraically closed base field. The argument uses only the group law
144	    on \(A\) (its smoothness as a group object), the regularity of \(X\) (so divisors and
145	    DVRs at codim-\(1\) points are available), and the existence of a divisor of zeros and
146	    poles for a nonzero rational function on a nonsingular variety. None of these inputs
147	    has a characteristic restriction.
148	\end{itemize}
149	Consequently \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} holds over every algebraically closed
150	field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states this proof verbatim in characteristic-free
151	generality at \S I.3, pp.~16--17.)
152	
153	\section{Application: the symmetric-product Albanese map (gating A.4.d)}
154	\label{sec:thm32_application_to_a4d}
155	
156	The downstream consumer is A.4.d (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}):
157	the Albanese universal property of the Jacobian \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\). Milne's proof of
158	Proposition~6.1 of \S III.6 (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~104) constructs the unique
159	morphism \(\psi \colon J \to A\) factoring a given pointed regular morphism
160	\(\varphi \colon C \to A\) through the canonical \(f_P \colon C \to J\) as follows. The
161	symmetric power \(C^{(g)}\) admits a regular morphism \(\varphi^{(g)} \colon C^{(g)} \to A\)
162	defined by
163	\[
164	  \varphi^{(g)}\bigl(P_1 + \dots + P_g\bigr) \;=\; \varphi(P_1) + \dots + \varphi(P_g),
165	\]
166	and a birational morphism \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow J\) (the Abel--Jacobi map). One then
167	seeks a morphism \(J \to A\) making the obvious diagram commute. The construction goes via
168	\(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) followed by descent to \(J\); the rational map
169	\(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) obtained at the intermediate step is exactly the kind that
170	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} promotes to a regular morphism, because \(C^{(g)}\) is
171	a nonsingular variety (Milne~III.3) and \(A\) is an abelian variety. Without
172	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} this step has no replacement: Milne's autoduality
173	shortcut (\(J \cong J^\vee\)) routes through the theorem of the cube, which the project
174	excised at iter-163; see the analysis in \cref{sec:Jacobian_routeA4_albaneseUP} of
175	\cref{chap:Jacobian}.
176	
177	\section{Lean encoding}
178	\label{sec:thm32_lean_encoding}
179	
180	The Lean target file is the new
181	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean}. The single declaration
182	this chapter targets is the theorem
183	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_to\_av}, with signature (in informal
184	notation):
185	\[
186	  \texttt{X : Scheme} \;\to\; \texttt{[Smooth X over \(\bar k\)]} \;\to\;
187	  \texttt{A : AbelianVariety \(\bar k\)} \;\to\;
188	  (f : \texttt{X.RationalMap A}) \;\to\;
189	  \exists ! \, \widetilde f \colon X \to A, \;\; \widetilde f \;\text{extends}\; f.
190	\]
191	The proof body imports \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s exports and
192	\cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}'s exports from
193	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean} and
194	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} respectively, and combines them
195	in the three-step pattern above (codim \(\geq 2\) from A.4.a + pure-codim \(1\) from A.4.a
196	\(\Rightarrow\) empty indeterminacy locus).
197	
198	The Mathlib namespace target is
199	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap} (already housed in
200	\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap} at the project's pinned commit;
201	see \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, where the same namespace hosts the
202	order-at-a-prime-divisor map). The extension is therefore a method on the existing
203	\texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure rather than a free-standing predicate, allowing
204	downstream consumers in \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP} to call it as
205	\texttt{f.extend\_to\_av}.
206	
207	No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a two-line combination of A.4.a and
208	A.4.b. The chapter is therefore inexpensive on the prover side (the LOC budget is dominated
209	by A.4.a and A.4.b themselves); its purpose is to record the precise statement and the

59	\section{Setup and statement}
60	\label{sec:albaneseup_setup}
61	
62	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
63	irreducible curve \(C\) over \(\bar k\) of genus \(g = g(C) > 0\) (the positive-genus
64	hypothesis is essential: it is precisely the standing hypothesis of Milne's
65	\S III.6). Fix moreover a \(\bar k\)-rational point \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) to serve
66	as the basepoint of the Albanese construction.
67	
68	By the A.3 row of Route~A (the identity-component construction applied to the
69	representable Picard scheme of
70	\cref{def:pic_scheme} in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) the
71	identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \subseteq \Pic_{C/k}\) is a smooth proper
72	geometrically irreducible \(\bar k\)-group scheme of dimension \(g\); we write
73	\(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\) and let \(\eta_J \colon \Spec \bar k \to J\) denote its
74	identity section, in the project's convention the chosen \(\mathbf 1\)-pointed
75	morphism that represents the unit element of the underlying group object on
76	\(J\).
77	
78	The universal \(T\)-points of \(J\) are degree-\(0\) line bundles on $C \times_{\bar k}
79	T\(, taken modulo line bundles pulled back from \)T$ (\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback};
80	the identity component then refines this to the degree-zero subfunctor on the
81	\'etale sheafification). This moduli interpretation is what underlies the
82	Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J\) of
83	\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. The universal homomorphism $\psi \colon J
84	\to A\( corresponding to a pointed morphism \)\varphi \colon C \to A$ is
85	constructed by Milne's symmetric-power route: the symmetric assignment
86	\((P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)\) defines a
87	regular morphism \(\widetilde \psi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\), the
88	birational morphism \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C \dashrightarrow J\) inverts to a
89	rational map \(J \dashrightarrow A\), and the rational-map extension theorem
90	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} promotes that rational map to the
91	desired regular morphism \(\psi\).
92	
93	The goal of the chapter is to establish:
94	
95	\begin{theorem}
96	
97	\leanok
98	[Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) -- Milne III~\S 6, Proposition~6.1]
99	  \label{thm:albanese_universal_property}
100	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.albanese_universal_property}
101	  
102	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition~6.1, \S III.6, doc p.~104
103	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
104	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
105	  % "PROPOSITION 6.1. Let \(P\) be a \(k\)-rational point on \(C\).  The map
106	  %  \(f^P\colon C\,\to\,J\) has the following universal property: for any
107	  %  map \(\varphi\colon C\to A\) from \(C\) into an abelian variety sending
108	  %  \(P\) to \(0\), there is a unique homomorphism \(\psi\colon J\to A\) such
109	  %  that \(\varphi=\psi\circ f^P\)."
110	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~6.1, \S III.6, p.~104.}
111	
112	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve of genus \(g > 0\) over an algebraically
113	  closed field \(\bar k\), let \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) be a \(\bar k\)-rational point,
114	  and write \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\), \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J\) for the
115	  Abel--Jacobi morphism of \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. Then for every
116	  abelian variety \(A\) over \(\bar k\) and every pointed morphism
117	  \(\varphi \colon C \to A\) with \(\varphi(P_0) = \eta_A\), there exists a unique
118	  homomorphism of group schemes \(\psi \colon J \to A\) such that
119	  \(\varphi = \psi \circ \iota_{P_0}\).
120	\end{theorem}
121	
122	The rest of this chapter develops the sub-lemmas
123	(\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}, \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve},
124	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map}, \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian},
125	and \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma}) that the proof rests on,
126	then gives the proof.
127	
128	\section{The Abel--Jacobi morphism at a marked point}
129	\label{sec:albaneseup_abel_jacobi}
130	
131	The first sub-lemma packages the construction and the elementary regularity of
132	the Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\iota_{P_0}\) at the basepoint \(P_0\). It is the
133	content gated on A.3 (existence of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a representable group
134	scheme).
135	
136	\begin{lemma}\leanok
137	[Abel--Jacobi morphism at a basepoint]
138	  \label{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}
139	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.abelJacobi}
140	  
141	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S III.6, the canonical map
142	  % \(f^P\colon C\to J\) (introduction to Proposition~6.1), doc p.~104
143	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
145	  % "PROPOSITION 6.1. Let \(P\) be a \(k\)-rational point on \(C\).  The map
146	  %  \(f^P\colon C\to J\) has the following universal property: for any map
147	  %  \(\varphi\colon C\to A\) from \(C\) into an abelian variety sending \(P\)
148	  %  to \(0\), there is a unique homomorphism \(\psi\colon J\to A\) such that
149	  %  \(\varphi=\psi\circ f^P\)."
150	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S III.6, Summary 6.11, doc p.~107
151	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 113).
152	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
153	  % "SUMMARY 6.11.  Between \((C,P)\) and itself, there is a divisorial
154	  %  correspondence \(\mathcal L^P = \mathcal L(\Delta-\{P\}\times C-C\times\{P\})\).
155	  %    Between \((C,P)\) and \(J\) there is the divisorial correspondence
156	  %  \(\mathcal M^P\); for any divisorial correspondence \(\mathcal L\) between
157	  %  \((C,P)\) and a pointed \(k\)-scheme \((T,t)\), there is a unique morphism
158	  %  of pointed \(k\)-schemes \(\varphi\colon T\to J\) such that
159	  %  \((1\times \varphi)^*\mathcal M^P\approx \mathcal L\).  In particular,
160	  %  there is a unique map \(f^P\colon C\to J\) such that
161	  %  \((1\times f^P)^*\mathcal M^P\approx \mathcal L^P\) and \(f(P)=0\)."
162	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~6.1 and
163	  Summary~6.11, \S III.6.}
164	
165	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve of genus \(g > 0\) over \(\bar k\), let
166	  \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) be a basepoint, and write \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\). There is a
167	  unique regular morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
168	  \[
169	    \iota_{P_0} \;\colon\; C \;\longrightarrow\; J, \qquad
170	    Q \;\longmapsto\; \bigl[\mathcal O_C(Q - P_0)\bigr],
171	  \]
172	  sending the basepoint \(P_0\) to the identity element \(\eta_J \in J(\bar k)\)
173	  and characterised on \(T\)-points by the formula
174	  \((1 \times \iota_{P_0})^*\mathcal M^{P_0} \approx \mathcal L^{P_0}\), where
175	  $\mathcal L^{P_0} := \mathcal O_{C \times C}\bigl(\Delta - \{P_0\} \times C -
176	  C \times \{P_0\}\bigr)$ is the rigidified diagonal correspondence on
177	  \(C \times C\) and \(\mathcal M^{P_0}\) is the Poincar\'e--Picard correspondence
178	  on \(C \times J\).
179	\end{lemma}
180	
181	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (no separate verbatim source; the existence of
182	% \(f^P\) is part of Milne's standing setup at the head of \S III.6 -- ``\(J\)
183	% will be its Jacobian variety'' -- and is the moduli-of-degree-0-line-bundles
184	% interpretation of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) packaged into Summary~6.11.)
185	\begin{proof}
186	  
187	  By A.3, \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the moduli scheme of degree-zero line bundles
188	  on \(C\); concretely, for every \(\bar k\)-scheme \(T\) its \(T\)-points are
189	  isomorphism classes of line bundles \(\mathcal L\) on \(C \times_{\bar k} T\)
190	  whose fibre degree at every geometric point of \(T\) is zero, taken modulo line
191	  bundles pulled back from \(T\) (the universal property of the Picard scheme
192	  packaged in \cref{def:pic_scheme}; the identity component imposes the
193	  degree-zero condition).
194	
195	  Apply this to \(T := C\) and the line bundle
196	  \[
197	    \mathcal L^{P_0} \;:=\; \mathcal O_{C \times_{\bar k} C}\bigl(\Delta -
198	      \{P_0\} \times C - C \times \{P_0\}\bigr),
199	  \]
200	  the rigidified diagonal correspondence on \(C \times_{\bar k} C\). Its fibre
201	  over a \(\bar k\)-point \(Q \in C(\bar k)\) along the second projection is the
202	  degree-zero line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(Q - P_0)\) on \(C\), so \(\mathcal L^{P_0}\)
203	  is a relative degree-zero line bundle, hence a \(C\)-point of \(J\). The
204	  representability of \(J\) at \(T = C\) produces the unique morphism
205	  \[
206	    \iota_{P_0} \;\colon\; C \;\longrightarrow\; J
207	  \]
208	  such that \((1 \times \iota_{P_0})^*\mathcal M^{P_0} \approx \mathcal L^{P_0}\)
209	  for the Poincar\'e--Picard correspondence \(\mathcal M^{P_0}\) on \(C \times J\).
210	
211	  At the basepoint, restriction of \(\mathcal L^{P_0}\) to \(C \times \{P_0\}\)
212	  gives \(\mathcal O_C(P_0 - P_0) \cong \mathcal O_C\), the trivial line bundle,
213	  whose moduli class in \(J(\bar k)\) is the identity element \(\eta_J\). Hence
214	  \(\iota_{P_0}(P_0) = \eta_J\). Uniqueness of \(\iota_{P_0}\) is the moduli
215	  uniqueness clause of representability: any other morphism \(\iota'_{P_0}\)
216	  satisfying \((1 \times \iota'_{P_0})^*\mathcal M^{P_0} \approx \mathcal L^{P_0}\)
217	  and the pointed condition agrees with \(\iota_{P_0}\) on \(T\)-points for every
218	  \(T\), hence equals \(\iota_{P_0}\) by the Yoneda embedding.
219	\end{proof}
220	
221	\section{The \(g\)-th symmetric power \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\)}
222	\label{sec:albaneseup_sym_g}
223	
224	The Albanese universal property is built from \(C\) by taking the
225	\(g\)-th \emph{symmetric power} \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) -- the quotient of the
226	\(g\)-fold Cartesian power \(C^g\) by the natural action of the symmetric group
227	\(S_g\). We collect the definition here as the first sub-lemma block.
228	
229	\begin{definition}\leanok
230	[Symmetric power of a curve]
231	  \label{def:symmetric_power_curve}
232	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.SymmetricPower}
233	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition~3.1, \S III.3,
234	  % doc p.~94 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 100).
235	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
236	  % "For any variety \(V\), the symmetric group \(S_r\) on \(r\) letters acts on
237	  %  the product of \(r\) copies \(V^r\) of \(V\) by permuting the factors, and
238	  %  we want to define the \(r\)th symmetric power \(V^{(r)}\) of \(V\) to be the
239	  %  quotient \(S_r \backslash V^r\).  The next proposition demonstrates the
240	  %  existence of \(V^{(r)}\) and lists its main properties.
241	  %    A morphism \(\varphi\colon V^r\to T\) is said to be {\bf symmetric} if
242	  %  \(\varphi\sigma=\varphi\) for all \(\sigma\) in \(S_r\).
243	  %  PROPOSITION 3.1. Let \(V\) be a variety over \(k\).  Then there exists a
244	  %  variety \(V^{(r)}\) and a symmetric morphism \(\pi\colon V^r\to V^{(r)}\)
245	  %  having the following properties:
246	  %    (a) as a topological space, \((V^{(r)},\pi)\) is the quotient of \(V^r\)
247	  %        by \(S_r\);
248	  %    (b) for any open affine subset \(U\) of \(V\), \(U^{(r)}\) is an open
249	  %        affine subset of \(V^{(r)}\) and
250	  %             $\Gamma(U^{(r)},\mathcal O_{V^{(r)}}) = \Gamma(U^r,
251	  %             \mathcal O_{V^r})^{S_r}$
252	  %        (set of elements fixed by the action of \(S_r\)).
253	  %  The pair \((V^{(r)},\pi)\) has the following universal property: every
254	  %  symmetric \(k\)-morphism \(\varphi\colon V^r\to T\) factors uniquely
255	  %  through \(\pi\).
256	  %  The map \(\pi\) is finite, surjective, and separable."
257	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~3.1, \S III.3, p.~94.}
258	
259	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve over an algebraically closed field
260	  \(\bar k\) and let \(g \ge 1\). The \emph{\(g\)-th symmetric power}
261	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) is a \(\bar k\)-scheme equipped with a finite surjective
262	  symmetric morphism \(\pi \colon C^g \to \mathrm{Sym}^g C\) characterised by
263	  the following universal property: for every \(\bar k\)-scheme \(T\) and every
264	  symmetric \(\bar k\)-morphism \(\varphi \colon C^g \to T\) (i.e.\ a morphism
265	  satisfying \(\varphi \circ \sigma = \varphi\) for all \(\sigma \in S_g\)
266	  acting by permutation of the factors of \(C^g\)) there exists a unique
267	  morphism \(\overline\varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to T\) with
268	  \(\overline\varphi \circ \pi = \varphi\). As a topological space
269	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) is the quotient of \(C^g\) by \(S_g\), and for every open
270	  affine \(U \subseteq C\) the open subset $U^{(g)} \subseteq \mathrm{Sym}^g
271	  C\( is affine with global sections the \)S_g$-invariants
272	  \(\Gamma(U^g, \mathcal O_{C^g})^{S_g}\).
273	\end{definition}
274	
275	\medskip
276	
277	\textbf{Mathlib status.} Mathlib has no formalised \(g\)-th symmetric power of
278	a scheme: only \texttt{Sym}/\texttt{SymmetricPower} for types and modules.
279	The corresponding scheme-theoretic construction must be supplied
280	project-side. The intended Lean construction follows Milne's recipe of
281	\cref{def:symmetric_power_curve}: on affine open \(U = \Spec A \subseteq C\)
282	form \(\Spec(A^{\otimes g})^{S_g}\), then glue via the standard affine
283	patching for an open cover \(C = \bigcup U_i\). This sub-build is a
284	non-trivial dependency of A.4.d that the prover lane must absorb before the
285	main universal property closes; a \texttt{\textbackslash notready} marker is
286	appropriate on the corresponding Lean target until the construction lands.
287	For the proof of Proposition~3.1 itself, Milne refers to Mumford 1970,
288	\S II~\S 7, p.~66 and \S III \S 11, p.~112, for the patching details (the
289	quoted reference at the end of Milne's Proof on \S III.3, p.~94).
290	
291	\section{The morphism \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\)}
292	\label{sec:albaneseup_sym_g_av_map}
293	
294	The second sub-lemma is the elementary fact that the symmetric assignment
295	\((P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)\) from
296	\(C^g\) to \(A\) descends through \(\pi \colon C^g \to \mathrm{Sym}^g C\). The
297	key point is that the addition morphism \(A^g \to A\) is itself
298	\(S_g\)-symmetric because \(A\) is commutative as a group scheme.
299	
300	\begin{lemma}\leanok
301	[Symmetrisation of a curve-to-AV morphism]
302	  \label{lem:symmetric_product_av_map}
303	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.symmetricPowerAVMap}
304	  
305	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proof of Proposition~6.1, \S III.6,
306	  % doc p.~104 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
307	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
308	  % "PROOF.  Consider the map
309	  %     \((P_1,...,P_g)\mapsto\sum_i\psi(P_i)\colon C^g\to A.\)
310	  %  Clearly this is symmetric, and so it factors through \(C^{(g)}\)."
311	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proof of Proposition~6.1,
312	  \S III.6, p.~104.}
313	
314	  Let \(\varphi \colon C \to A\) be a morphism into an abelian variety \(A\)
315	  over \(\bar k\). The composition
316	  \[
317	    \mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}
318	    \;\colon\; C^g \;\longrightarrow\; A^g \;\longrightarrow\; A,
319	    \qquad (P_1, \ldots, P_g) \;\longmapsto\; \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g),
320	  \]
321	  is \(S_g\)-equivariant (where \(S_g\) acts on \(C^g\) by permutation of the
322	  factors and on \(A\) trivially). Consequently, by the universal property of
323	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) (\cref{def:symmetric_power_curve}), there is a unique
324	  morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
325	  \[
326	    \mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \;\colon\; \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\longrightarrow\; A
327	  \]
328	  such that
329	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \circ \pi = \mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}\).
330	\end{lemma}
331	
332	\begin{proof}
333	  
334	  Since \(A\) is a commutative group scheme, the \(g\)-fold addition morphism
335	  \(\mathrm{add}_A \colon A^g \to A\) satisfies $\mathrm{add}_A \circ \tau =
336	  \mathrm{add}_A\( for every \)\tau \in S_g\( permuting the factors of \)A^g$
337	  (this is the scheme-theoretic statement that summation in a commutative
338	  monoid is independent of the order of summands; on \(T\)-points it is the
339	  identity \(a_1 + \cdots + a_g = a_{\tau(1)} + \cdots + a_{\tau(g)}\) in the
340	  abelian group \(A(T)\)). The diagonal action of \(\tau\) on $\varphi^{\times
341	  g} \colon C^g \to A^g$ is also given by permutation of factors. Therefore
342	  \[
343	    (\mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}) \circ \tau
344	    \;=\; \mathrm{add}_A \circ (\varphi^{\times g} \circ \tau)
345	    \;=\; \mathrm{add}_A \circ (\tau \circ \varphi^{\times g})
346	    \;=\; (\mathrm{add}_A \circ \tau) \circ \varphi^{\times g}
347	    \;=\; \mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g},
348	  \]
349	  so the composition \(\mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}\) is symmetric
350	  in the sense of \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve}. The universal property
351	  of \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) produces the unique factorisation
352	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\).
353	\end{proof}
354	
355	\section{The birational morphism \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C \dashrightarrow J\)}
356	\label{sec:albaneseup_sym_g_to_jacobian}
357	
358	The third sub-lemma is the birational identification of \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\)
359	with \(J\). After choosing the basepoint \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\), the cycle map
360	\((P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto [\mathcal O_C(P_1 + \cdots + P_g - g P_0)]\)
361	defines a morphism \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\) which is
362	birational by Milne III~Theorem~5.1(a).
363	
364	\begin{lemma}\leanok
365	[Birational morphism from \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) to \(J\)]
366	  \label{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}
367	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.symmetricPowerToJacobian}
368	  
369	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~5.1(a), \S III.5,
370	  % doc p.~101 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 107).
371	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
372	  % "Let \(f^r\) be the map \(C^r\to J\) sending \((P_1,...,P_r)\) to
373	  %  \(f(P_1)+\cdots+f(P_r)\).  On points, \(f^r\) is the map
374	  %  \((P_1,...,P_r)\mapsto[P_1+\cdots+P_r-rP]\).  Clearly it is symmetric,
375	  %  and so induces a map \(f^{(r)}\colon C^{(r)}\to J\).  We can regard
376	  %  \(f^{(r)}\) as being the map sending an effective divisor \(D\) of degree
377	  %  \(r\) on \(C\) to the linear equivalence class of \(D-rP\).  The fibre of
378	  %  the map \(f^{(r)}\colon C^{(r)}(k)\to J(k)\) containing \(D\) can be
379	  %  identified with the space of effective divisors linearly equivalent
380	  %  to \(D\), that is, with the linear system \(|D|\).  The image of \(C^{(r)}\)
381	  %  in \(J\) is a closed subvariety \(W^r\) of \(J\), which can also be written
382	  %  \(W^r=f(C)+\cdots+f(C)\) (\(r\) summands).
383	  %  THEOREM 5.1.  (a) For all \(r\le g\), the morphism
384	  %  \(f^{(r)}\colon C^{(r)}\to W^r\) is birational; in particular,
385	  %  \(f^{(g)}\) is a birational map from \(C^{(g)}\) onto \(J\)."
386	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~5.1(a) and the
387	  preceding paragraph, \S III.5, p.~101.}
388	
389	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve of genus \(g > 0\) over \(\bar k\),
390	  let \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) be a basepoint, and write \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\),
391	  \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J\) for the Abel--Jacobi morphism of
392	  \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. The regular morphism
393	  \(\iota_{P_0}^{\times g} \colon C^g \to J^g\) composed with the \(g\)-fold
394	  group-law addition \(\mathrm{add}_J \colon J^g \to J\) descends through
395	  \(\pi \colon C^g \to \mathrm{Sym}^g C\) to a regular morphism
396	  \[
397	    f^{(g)} \;\colon\; \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\longrightarrow\; J,
398	    \qquad (P_1, \ldots, P_g) \;\longmapsto\;
399	      \bigl[\mathcal O_C(P_1 + \cdots + P_g - g P_0)\bigr].
400	  \]
401	  The morphism \(f^{(g)}\) is birational: it is dominant (its image is
402	  \(J\), the \(g\)-fold sum \(\iota_{P_0}(C) + \cdots + \iota_{P_0}(C)\) exhausts
403	  \(J\) since \(\dim J = g\) and the iterated sum reaches full dimension), and
404	  its generic fibre is a single \(\bar k\)-point (a degree-\(g\) effective
405	  divisor \(D\) on \(C\) with \(h^0(D) = 1\) is the unique element of its linear
406	  system \(|D|\); such divisors form a dense open subset of $\mathrm{Sym}^g
407	  C\( -- a consequence of \)\dim |D| = 0\( generically on a curve of genus \)g$
408	  by Riemann--Roch). Equivalently, \(f^{(g)}\) is a birational morphism in
409	  the sense that there exist dense opens
410	  \(U \subseteq \mathrm{Sym}^g C\), \(V \subseteq J\) such that
411	  \(f^{(g)}|_U \colon U \to V\) is an isomorphism.
412	\end{lemma}
413	
414	\begin{proof}
415	  
416	  Symmetry of \(\mathrm{add}_J \circ \iota_{P_0}^{\times g}\) is the
417	  specialisation of \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} to the abelian
418	  variety \(A := J\) and the morphism \(\varphi := \iota_{P_0}\); the universal
419	  property of \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) produces the regular morphism \(f^{(g)}\).
420	  The closed-form description on \(\bar k\)-points,
421	  \(f^{(g)}(P_1, \ldots, P_g) = [\mathcal O_C(P_1 + \cdots + P_g - g P_0)]\),
422	  follows because \(\iota_{P_0}(P_i) = [\mathcal O_C(P_i - P_0)]\) by
423	  \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism} and the group law on \(J\) is the
424	  tensor-product of degree-zero line bundles.
425	
426	  We verify birationality. The image of \(f^{(g)}\) in \(J\) is the iterated
427	  sum \(\iota_{P_0}(C) + \cdots + \iota_{P_0}(C)\) (\(g\) summands), which is
428	  closed (it is the image of the proper morphism
429	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\), \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) being proper because \(C^g\)
430	  is proper and \(\pi\) is finite surjective). By the dimension count
431	  \(\dim \mathrm{Sym}^g C = g = \dim J\) (Riemann--Roch and the standing
432	  hypothesis \(g > 0\)), the image \(W^g \subseteq J\) has dimension at most
433	  \(g\), and equality forces \(W^g = J\) since \(J\) is irreducible. Hence
434	  \(f^{(g)}\) is surjective with \(\dim\)-equal source and target.
435	
436	  For the generic-fibre identification, Riemann--Roch
437	  \(h^0(D) - h^1(D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) at \(\deg D = g\) gives $h^0(D) - h^1(D)
438	  = 1\(, so for a general \)D \in \mathrm{Sym}^g C(\bar k)\( with \)h^1(D) =
439	  0\( (the locus where \)h^1$ jumps is closed of positive codimension on a
440	  smooth curve, by upper semicontinuity of cohomology), we have $h^0(D) =
441	  1\( and the linear system \)|D|\( consists of \)D$ alone. Therefore the
442	  fibre of \(f^{(g)}\) over the generic point of \(J\) contains a single point
443	  \(D\), i.e.\ \(f^{(g)}\) is birational onto \(J\).
444	
445	  By \cref{def:pic_scheme} the underlying \(\bar k\)-scheme \(J\) is the
446	  Picard scheme \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) obtained at A.3, so the moduli-theoretic
447	  reading of \(f^{(g)}\) (effective divisor class \(\mapsto\) degree-zero
448	  line bundle class via \(D \mapsto [\mathcal O_C(D - g P_0)]\)) is the
449	  same as the Yoneda image of the \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\)-point
450	  $[\mathcal O_{C \times \mathrm{Sym}^g C}(\mathcal D_g - g\{P_0\} \times
451	  \mathrm{Sym}^g C)]\(, where \)\mathcal D_g \subseteq C \times \mathrm{Sym}^g
452	  C\( is the universal effective Cartier divisor of degree \)g\( on \)C$. We
453	  do not need this Yoneda repackaging for the proof of the Albanese UP --
454	  the symmetric-power route uses only the morphism \(f^{(g)}\) and its
455	  birationality.
456	\end{proof}
457	
458	\section{Descent through the birational quotient \(\sigma\)}
459	\label{sec:albaneseup_descent}
460	
461	The final auxiliary lemma packages the two-step descent that promotes a
462	morphism on \(C^g\) (after passing through \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) via
463	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} and inverting \(f^{(g)}\) via
464	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}) to a regular morphism on
465	\(J\). The output is a rational map \(J \dashrightarrow A\), which is then
466	upgraded to a regular morphism via Milne's Theorem~3.2
467	(\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}). This is the unique step of the
468	proof that consumes A.4.c.
469	
470	\begin{lemma}\leanok
471	[Descent of \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi\) to a regular morphism \(J \to A\)]
472	  \label{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma}
473	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.descentThroughBirationalSigma}
474	  
475	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proof of Proposition~6.1, \S III.6,
476	  % doc p.~104 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
477	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
478	  % "It therefore defines a rational map \(\psi\colon J\to A\), which (I 3.2)
479	  %  shows to be a regular map."
480	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proof of Proposition~6.1,
481	  \S III.6, p.~104 (the sentence ``which (I~3.2) shows to be a regular
482	  map'').}
483	
484	  Let \(\varphi \colon C \to A\) be a morphism into an abelian variety \(A\)
485	  over \(\bar k\). Let \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\)
486	  be the symmetrised morphism of \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} and
487	  \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\) the birational morphism of
488	  \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}. Then there is a unique
489	  regular morphism
490	  \[
491	    \psi \;\colon\; J \;\longrightarrow\; A
492	  \]
493	  such that \(\psi \circ f^{(g)} = \mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi\) on the dense open
494	  on which \(f^{(g)}\) is an isomorphism (equivalently, \(\psi\) is the unique
495	  regular morphism extending the rational map
496	  $\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \circ (f^{(g)})^{-1} \colon J \dashrightarrow
497	  A$).
498	\end{lemma}
499	
500	\begin{proof}
501	  
502	  By \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}, there exist dense opens
503	  \(U \subseteq \mathrm{Sym}^g C\) and \(V \subseteq J\) with $f^{(g)}|_U \colon
504	  U \xrightarrow{\sim} V\( an isomorphism. Setting \)\psi_0 \colon V \to A$
505	  to be the composition $\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi|_U \circ (f^{(g)}|_U)^{-1}
506	  \colon V \to U \to A\( produces a rational map \)J \dashrightarrow A$.
507	
508	  The target \(A\) is an abelian variety and the source \(J\) is smooth over
509	  \(\bar k\) (it is itself an abelian variety, hence nonsingular). Milne's
510	  Theorem~3.2 (\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}) therefore promotes
511	  \(\psi_0\) to a unique regular morphism \(\psi \colon J \to A\) extending
512	  \(\psi_0\) on \(V\). Uniqueness of \(\psi\) follows from the
513	  reduced-and-separated agreement principle: two regular morphisms
514	  \(J \to A\) agreeing on a dense open are equal.
515	\end{proof}
516	
517	\section{Proof of the universal property}
518	\label{sec:albaneseup_proof}
519	
520	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
521	% "PROOF.  Consider the map
522	%    \(\)(P_1,\ldots,P_g)\mapsto\sum_i\psi(P_i)\colon C^g\to A.\(\)
523	%  Clearly this is symmetric, and so it factors through \(C^{(g)}\).  It
524	%  therefore defines a rational map \(\psi\colon J\to A\), which (I 3.2)
525	%  shows to be a regular map.  It is clear from the construction that
526	%  \(\psi\circ f^P=\varphi\) (note that \(f^P\) is the composite of $Q\mapsto
527	%  Q+(g-1)P\colon C\to C^{(g)}\( with \)f^{(g)}\colon C^{(g)}\to J$).  In
528	%  particular, \(\psi\) maps 0 to 0, and (I 1.2) shows that it is therefore
529	%  a homomorphism.  If \(\psi'\) is a second homomorphism such that
530	%  \(\psi'\circ f^P=\varphi\), then \(\psi\) and \(\psi'\) agree on
531	%  \(f^P(C)+\cdots+f^P(C)\) (\(g\) copies), which is the whole of \(J\)."
532	\begin{proof}[Proof of \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property}]
533	  
534	  The proof follows Milne's Proposition~6.1 verbatim, using the four
535	  sub-lemmas of this chapter:
536	  \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism} (Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\iota_{P_0}\)),
537	  \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} (symmetrisation of \(\varphi\) to
538	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\)),
539	  \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian} (birational morphism
540	  \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\)), and
541	  \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma} (descent of $\mathrm{Sym}^g
542	  \varphi\( to a regular morphism \)\psi \colon J \to A$ via
543	  \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}).
544	  \begin{enumerate}
545	    \item \emph{Symmetrisation.} Consider the map \(C^g \to A\) sending
546	      \((P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)\)
547	      (Milne's typeset proof writes \(\sum_i \psi(P_i)\) in this line, but
548	      the construction is the symmetrisation of the input \(\varphi\); we
549	      use the unambiguous symbol \(\varphi\) throughout). Since \(A\) is
550	      commutative this map is \(S_g\)-symmetric. By
551	      \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} it factors through the
552	      symmetric power \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\), defining a regular morphism
553	      \[
554	        \widetilde \psi \;:=\; \mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi
555	        \;\colon\; \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\longrightarrow\; A.
556	      \]
557	    \item \emph{Descent to a regular morphism on \(J\).} By
558	      \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}, the morphism
559	      \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\) is birational. The
560	      composite \(\widetilde \psi \circ (f^{(g)})^{-1}\) is, a priori,
561	      only a rational map \(J \dashrightarrow A\). By
562	      \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma} -- whose proof is an
563	      application of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} (Milne
564	      Theorem~I~3.2, rational map from a nonsingular variety to an
565	      abelian variety is regular) -- this rational map extends uniquely
566	      to a regular morphism
567	      \[
568	        \psi \;\colon\; J \;\longrightarrow\; A.
569	      \]
570	    \item \emph{Factorisation \(\psi \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi\).}
571	      Milne's identification: \(\iota_{P_0}\) is the composite
572	      \[
573	        C \;\xrightarrow{\;Q \mapsto Q + (g-1) P_0\;}\;
574	        \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\xrightarrow{\;f^{(g)}\;}\; J,
575	      \]
576	      where the first arrow sends a point \(Q \in C(\bar k)\) to the
577	      effective divisor \(Q + (g-1) P_0 \in \mathrm{Sym}^g C(\bar k)\)
578	      (its image under \(f^{(g)}\) in \(J\) is
579	      $[\mathcal O_C(Q + (g-1) P_0 - g P_0)] = [\mathcal O_C(Q - P_0)]
580	      = \iota_{P_0}(Q)$, matching \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}). On
581	      \(\bar k\)-points,
582	      \[
583	        \psi(\iota_{P_0}(Q)) = \widetilde\psi(Q + (g-1) P_0)
584	          = \varphi(Q) + (g-1)\varphi(P_0) = \varphi(Q),
585	      \]
586	      using \(\varphi(P_0) = \eta_A\) and that \(\eta_A\) is the identity of
587	      \(A\). Hence \(\psi \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi\) on \(\bar k\)-points,
588	      and therefore \(\psi \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi\) as morphisms of
589	      \(\bar k\)-schemes (both sides are regular morphisms \(C \to A\) from
590	      a reduced separated scheme to a separated scheme, agreeing on
591	      dense \(\bar k\)-points, hence agreeing everywhere by the
592	      reduced-and-separated agreement principle).
593	    \item \emph{Homomorphism property.} The morphism \(\psi\) sends
594	      \(\eta_J\) to \(\eta_A\): indeed,
595	      \(\psi(\eta_J) = \psi(\iota_{P_0}(P_0)) = \varphi(P_0) = \eta_A\).
596	      A regular morphism of abelian varieties sending the identity to
597	      the identity is a homomorphism of group schemes, by Milne
598	      Corollary~I~1.2 (a consequence of the Rigidity Lemma
599	      \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, see
600	      \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}, Corollary~1.2). Therefore
601	      \(\psi\) is a homomorphism of group schemes \(J \to A\).
602	    \item \emph{Uniqueness.} Suppose \(\psi'\) is a second homomorphism
603	      with \(\psi' \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi\). Then \(\psi\) and
604	      \(\psi'\) agree on the set-theoretic image $\iota_{P_0}(C) \subseteq
605	      J$. Both are homomorphisms, so they agree on the subgroup
606	      generated by \(\iota_{P_0}(C)\) -- which contains the \(g\)-fold sum
607	      \(\iota_{P_0}(C) + \cdots + \iota_{P_0}(C)\) (\(g\) copies). The
608	      \(g\)-fold sum is the image of
609	      \(\mathrm{add}_J \circ \iota_{P_0}^{\times g} \colon C^g \to J\),
610	      which descends to \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\), which
611	      is surjective by \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}.
612	      Therefore \(\psi\) and \(\psi'\) agree on a dense open subset of \(J\)
613	      (in fact on all of \(J\) as a set), hence everywhere by the
614	      reduced-and-separated agreement principle.
615	  \end{enumerate}
616	  This completes the proof.
617	\end{proof}
618	
619	\section{Wiring for the positive-genus Albanese witness}
620	\label{sec:albaneseup_witness_wiring}
621	
622	\cref{thm:albanese_universal_property} supplies the universal-factorisation
623	field \(\texttt{isAlbaneseFor}\) of an Albanese witness for \((C, P_0)\) in the
624	sense of \cref{def:JacobianWitness} (\cref{chap:Jacobian}); the remaining
625	fields are supplied by A.3:
626	\begin{itemize}
627	  \item \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\) (the underlying scheme).
628	  \item \(\texttt{grpObj}\): the group-object structure on \(J\) obtained from the
629	    abelian-group structure on \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(T)\) for varying \(T\), refined to a
630	    group object in the category of \(\bar k\)-schemes (A.3, A.2.c group-scheme
631	    refinement).
632	  \item \(\texttt{proper}\): properness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec \bar k\) from
633	    the closed-immersion property of the identity component in the proper
634	    representing Picard scheme (A.3; cf.\
635	    \cref{thm:Jacobian_proper}'s discussion).
636	  \item \(\texttt{smoothGenus}\): smoothness of relative dimension \(g(C)\) from
637	    the deformation-theoretic tangent-space identification with $H^1(C,
638	    \mathcal O_C)\(, which has dimension \)g(C)$ by Riemann--Roch.
639	  \item \(\texttt{geomIrred}\): geometric irreducibility of the identity
640	    component of a \(\bar k\)-group scheme locally of finite type (Kleiman
641	    Lem.~lem:agps and Prp.~prp:pic0; cf.\
642	    \cref{thm:Jacobian_geomIrred}'s discussion).
643	  \item \(\texttt{isAlbaneseFor}\): the present
644	    \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property}, applied at each
645	    \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\).
646	\end{itemize}

648	The witness so produced is over \(\bar k\). The Galois-descent step from $\bar
649	k\( back to the original base field \)k$ -- packaged at
650	\cref{chap:Jacobian}, sub-step C.2.f -- closes the loop and supplies the
651	\(k\)-side Albanese witness consumed by \cref{def:Jacobian}.

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its three core properties --- universal property, affine
26	base, base change --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30

32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).

36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}

79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
87	
88	\begin{theorem}\leanok
89	[Relative spectrum exists]
90	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
92

94	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).

101	  %  with the following properties:
102	  %  \begin{enumerate}

104	  %  \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\), and
105	  %  \item for \(U \subset U' \subset S\) affine open the composition
106	  %  \(\)

109	  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &

111	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
112	  %  }
113	  %  \(\)
114	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma REF above.

117	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over \(S\)."
118	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
119	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
120	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra
121	  (Definition~REF). There exists a scheme
122	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism
123	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that
124	  \begin{enumerate}
125	    \item for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism
126	      \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);
127	    \item for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite
128	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
129	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
130	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
131	      \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).
132	  \end{enumerate}
133	  The pair \((\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)\) is unique up to unique isomorphism
134	  over \(X\).
135	\end{theorem}

139	% REF, and

141	% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of

143	\begin{proof}
144	  
145	  
146	\leanok
147	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
148	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
149	  affine local pieces \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) for each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) and
150	  open-immersion transition data
151	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\) for \(U \subseteq U'\)
152	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
153	  \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\); quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
154	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\), so each transition is an
155	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
156	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
157	\end{proof}

184	  %  We will use all three ways of thinking about \(\varphi\),
185	  %  without further mention. [...]
186	  %  In this way we have defined a functor
187	  %  \begin{eqnarray}
188	  %  \label{equation-spec}
189	  %  F : \Sch^{opp} & \longrightarrow & \textit{Sets} \\
190	  %  T & \longmapsto & F(T) = \{\text{pairs }(f, \varphi) \text{ as above}\}
191	  %  \nonumber
192	  %  \end{eqnarray}
193	  %  [...]
194	  %  The functor \(F\) is representable by a scheme."
195	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-spec + section-spec
196	  functor description); [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(b).}
197	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra.
198	  Then \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) represents the functor
199	  \(F : \Sch^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\),
200	  $T \mapsto F(T) := \{(f, \varphi) : f : T \to X \text{ a morphism},\;
201	  \varphi : f^* \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T \text{ an } \mathcal{O}_T\text{-algebra map}\}.$
202	  Equivalently, for any \(X\)-scheme \(g : T \to X\),
203	  there is a natural bijection
204	  \[
205	    \Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})) \;\;\cong\;\;
206	    \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_* \mathcal{O}_T)
207	  \]
208	  (using the adjunction between \(g_*\) and \(g^*\) to convert between
209	  \(\mathcal{A} \to g_*\mathcal{O}_T\) and \(g^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T\)).
210	  The bijection is natural in \(T\).
211	\end{theorem}

214	% (proof of lemma-spec, L553-L600, ~50 lines covering the Zariski sheaf property,
215	% the subfunctors F_i associated to an affine cover, and identifying each F_i
216	% with the affine-base case via lemma-spec-base-change and lemma-spec-affine).
217	% Verbatim quote omitted for length; the structural steps are restated below in
218	% project notation.
219	\begin{proof}
220	  
221	  \leanok
222	  % NOTE (iter-179 review): supersedes the iter-176 placeholder-discharge note.
223	  % The iter-179 Block A refactor replaces the iter-176 placeholder bodies with
224	  % the Mathlib values ``(AffineZariskiSite.relativeGluingData _).glued`` /
225	  % ``.toBase``; iter-179 Lane B closes the Lean ``UniversalProperty`` proof
226	  % via ``isAffineHom_of_forall_exists_isAffineOpen`` +
227	  % ``relativeGluingData.toBase_preimage_eq_opensRange_ι`` +
228	  % ``isAffineOpen_opensRange`` — a real proof against the gluing infrastructure,
229	  % NOT against a trivial identity placeholder. The Lean type remains the
230	  % strictly weaker ``IsAffineHom (structureMorphism 𝒜)'' rather than the
231	  % Yoneda-bijection form pinned above (iter-174+ commitment; still pending
232	  % iter-180+). Consumers may now rely on the affineness conclusion, but the
233	  % Yoneda-bijection witness is still deferred.
234	  Following Stacks tag 01LQ (proof of lemma-spec): the functor \(F\) is a Zariski sheaf
235	  because morphisms of schemes and \(\mathcal{O}\)-algebra maps both glue under open covers.
236	  Choose an affine open cover \(X = \bigcup U_i\). The subfunctor
237	  \(F_i \subseteq F\) of pairs \((f, \varphi)\) with \(f(T) \subseteq U_i\) is representable by
238	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U_i))\) via the affine-base case
239	  (Theorem~REF) together with the
240	  base-change identification \(F_i \cong F_{U_i,\,\mathcal{A}|_{U_i}}\)
241	  (Theorem~REF). Each \(F_i \hookrightarrow F\) is an open
242	  subfunctor (pull back \(U_i\) along \(f\)). The \(F_i\) jointly cover \(F\) since the \(U_i\)
243	  cover \(X\). The gluing of representable open subfunctors produces a representing scheme,
244	  which is canonically isomorphic to the glued scheme \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\)
245	  of Theorem~REF.
246	\end{proof}

251	\begin{theorem}\leanok
252	[Relative spectrum over an affine base]
253	  \label{thm:relative_spec_affine_base}
254	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.affine_base_iff}
255	  % NOTE (iter-173 review): the iter-173 file-skeleton encodes this as the
256	  % weaker ``IsAffine ((Spec R).RelativeSpec 𝒜)'' rather than the canonical
257	  % iso ``Spec_X(𝒜) ≅ Spec(Γ(X, 𝒜))'' pinned by this prose. The Lean name
258	  % ``affine_base_iff'' is also misleading (the encoded type is not an iff).
259	  % Iter-174+ refines to the iff-with-Γ statement once Γ : QcohAlgebra → CommRingCat
260	  % is available.
261	  
262	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LO (lemma-spec-affine)
263	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L491-L545).
264	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
265	  % "In Situation REF.
266	  %  Let \(F\) be the functor associated to \((S, \mathcal{A})\) above.
267	  %  If \(S\) is affine, then \(F\) is representable by the
268	  %  affine scheme \(\Spec(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{A}))\)."
269	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LO (lemma-spec-affine).}
270	  Let \(X = \Spec R\) be an affine scheme and let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a quasi-coherent
271	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra. Set \(A := \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A})\), regarded as an
272	  \(R\)-algebra. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of \(X\)-schemes
273	  \[
274	    \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \;\;\cong\;\; \Spec(A).
275	  \]
276	  In particular, on an affine base the relative spectrum reduces to the absolute
277	  spectrum of the global sections.
278	\end{theorem}
279	
280	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Write \(S = \Spec(R)\) and \(A = \Gamma(S, \mathcal{A})\).
281	% Then \(A\) is an \(R\)-algebra and \(\mathcal{A} = \widetilde A\).
282	% The ring map \(R \to A\) gives rise to a canonical map
283	% \(\)
284	% f_{univ} : \Spec(A)
285	% \longrightarrow
286	% S = \Spec(R).
287	% \(\)
288	% We have
289	% \(f_{univ}^*\mathcal{A} =  \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}\)
290	% by Schemes, Lemma REF.
291	% Hence there is a canonical map
292	% \(\)
293	% \varphi_{univ} :
294	% f_{univ}^*\mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}
295	% \longrightarrow
296	% \widetilde A = \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(A)}
297	% \(\)
298	% coming from the \(A\)-module map \(A \otimes_R A \to A\),
299	% \(a \otimes a' \mapsto aa'\). We claim that the pair
300	% \((f_{univ}, \varphi_{univ})\) represents \(F\) in this case."
301	\begin{proof}
302	  
303	  \leanok
304	  % NOTE (iter-179 review): supersedes the iter-176 placeholder-discharge note.
305	  % The iter-176 ``change IsAffine (Spec R); inferInstance`` form is now stale
306	  % — iter-179 Lane B closes via ``UniversalProperty 𝒜`` + ``isAffine_of_isAffineHom``
307	  % against the iter-179 Mathlib-backed ``RelativeSpec``/``structureMorphism``
308	  % bodies. The Lean type remains the strictly weaker ``IsAffine`` rather than
309	  % the canonical iso ``Spec_X(𝒜) ≅ Spec(Γ(X,𝒜))'' named below (iter-174+
310	  % commitment; still pending iter-180+).
311	  Write \(X = \Spec R\) and \(A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A})\). Quasi-coherence of
312	  \(\mathcal{A}\) gives \(\mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A}\) as \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
313	  (with the \(R\)-algebra structure on \(A\) inducing the algebra structure on
314	  \(\widetilde A\)). The ring map \(R \to A\) gives a morphism
315	  \(f_{\mathrm{univ}} : \Spec A \to \Spec R = X\) and a canonical
316	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A}\)-algebra map
317	  $\varphi_{\mathrm{univ}} : f_{\mathrm{univ}}^* \mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}
318	  \to \mathcal{O}_{\Spec A} = \widetilde A$ given by multiplication
319	  \(a \otimes a' \mapsto aa'\). We verify \((\Spec A, f_{\mathrm{univ}}, \varphi_{\mathrm{univ}})\)
320	  represents \(F\) on the affine base: given a test scheme \(T\) and a pair
321	  \((f, \varphi)\), the composite
322	  $A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A}) \xrightarrow{f^\sharp}
323	  \Gamma(T, f^*\mathcal{A}) \xrightarrow{\varphi} \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)$
324	  is a ring map \(A \to \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)\) and hence corresponds to a morphism
325	  \(T \to \Spec A\) by the affine universal property
326	  (Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.toSpec\_naturality} /
327	  \texttt{Scheme.Hom.toSpec}). One checks this assignment inverts the universal map
328	  \((f, \varphi) \mapsto a\) defined by \(f = f_{\mathrm{univ}} \circ a\),
329	  \(\varphi = a^* \varphi_{\mathrm{univ}}\).
330	\end{proof}

346	  % lemma-spec-properties part (2))

363	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LS (lemma-spec-base-change +
364	  lemma-spec-properties).}
365	  Let \(g : T \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a quasi-coherent
366	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra. Then \(g^* \mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
367	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-algebra and there is a canonical isomorphism of \(T\)-schemes

374	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "A pair \((f' : T \to S', \varphi' : (f')^*\mathcal{A}' \to \mathcal{O}_T)\)
375	% is the same as a pair \((f, \varphi : f^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T)\)
376	% together with a factorization of \(f\) as \(f = g \circ f'\). Namely with
377	% this notation we have
378	% \((f')^* \mathcal{A}' = (f')^*g^*\mathcal{A} = f^*\mathcal{A}\).
379	% Hence the lemma."
380	\begin{proof}
381	  
382	  \leanok
383	  % NOTE (iter-185 review, supersedes iter-179): the iter-185 Lane D HARD-BAR
384	  % closed BOTH iter-179 Mathlib-gap sorries kernel-clean:
385	  % (i) ``Scheme.QcohAlgebra.pullback_coequifibered`` (the ``coequifibered``
386	  % field, RelativeSpec.lean L358-377) via
387	  % ``coequifibered_iff_forall_isLocalizationAway`` +
388	  % ``IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_of_eq_basicOpen``;
389	  % (ii) ``Scheme.RelativeSpec.pullback_iso_desc_isIso`` (the canonical iso body,
390	  % RelativeSpec.lean L546-632) via the explicit 3-iso chain
391	  % (hPre identity \(\to\) isoOpensRange.symm \(\to\) pullback_iso_affine_piece.symm)
392	  % and ``IsZariskiLocalAtTarget`` affineness criterion.
393	  % The Lean type remains the weaker existential rather than the canonical iso
394	  % with a *named* pullback ``g^*𝒜'' pinned by this prose (iter-174+ signature
395	  % refinement still pending — see chapter NOTE on the statement block above).
396	  % Consumers may now consume ``pullback_iso''s witness as a fully axiom-clean
397	  % closure (no more "load-bearing-pending" qualifier); A.1.a body-level work
398	  % is functionally complete.
399	  By Theorem~REF, \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) represents
400	  the functor \(F\) sending an \(X\)-scheme \(h : S \to X\) to the set of
401	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra maps \(h^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_S\). A morphism
402	  \(S \to T \times_X \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) over \(T\) is the same as a pair of an
403	  \(X\)-morphism \(S \to \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with a \(T\)-factorisation
404	  of \(S \to X\), i.e.\ a morphism \(S \to T\) whose composition with \(g\) equals
405	  \(S \to X\). Tracing through, this is a \(T\)-morphism \(f' : S \to T\) together with an
406	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra map \((f')^*(g^*\mathcal{A}) = f^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_S\).
407	  This is precisely \(F'(S)\), the functor for \((T, g^*\mathcal{A})\), so the canonical map
408	  \(T \times_X \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to \underline{\Spec}_T(g^*\mathcal{A})\)
